findings.title: start_ferment_batch creates a phantom batch with NO ferment_started ledger event when its idempotency_key was already used by any other event kind
findings.dimension: DATA-LAYER INVARIANT BYPASS (append-only ledger ↔ domain-row atomicity)
findings.location: start_ferment_batch, lines 242-266 (short-circuit filters kind='ferment_started'; INSERT into ferment_batches has no idempotency guard; record_lot_event uses ON CONFLICT (idempotency_key) DO NOTHING)
findings.problem: The slice's load-bearing invariant is that every domain write is appended to the hash-chained lot_event in ONE atomic txn (ADR-002). start_ferment_batch's exactly-once short-circuit is `select … from lot_event where idempotency_key = p_idempotency_key AND kind = 'ferment_started'`. It is scoped to ONE kind. But idempotency_key is GLOBALLY unique across lot_event (all kinds, all streams). So if the same key already exists under a DIFFERENT kind, the short-circuit misses, the ferment_batches row is INSERTed unconditionally (no ON CONFLICT, fresh gen_random_uuid()), and then record_lot_event does ON CONFLICT (idempotency_key) DO NOTHING — silently dropping the ferment_started event. Net: a batch row exists in the domain table with ZERO ledger event backing it — append-only provenance is broken and the batch is invisible to verify_chain / EUDR traceability.
findings.scenario: Empirically confirmed in PGlite (migration replay): seed `record_lot_event('JC-800','some_other_kind',…,'shared-key-3')`, then `start_ferment_batch('JC-800',…,'shared-key-3')`. Result: 2 Anaerobic batches on JC-800, 0 ferment_started events with that key. Reachable because the offline outbox (P2-S0) is the design's whole point: any client-minted idempotency key shared across two intents (or a reading-key reused as a start-key) triggers it, and the RPC is granted to authenticated so it is directly callable with an arbitrary key. The same one-kind-scoped short-circuit pattern is in advance_processing_stage / record_cherry_intake in phase-1, so the collision surface is real.
findings.fix: Make the start idempotent on the DOMAIN write, not on a kind-scoped event lookup. Either (a) add a UNIQUE column on ferment_batches carrying the idempotency_key and `insert … on conflict (idempotency_key) do nothing returning id`, returning the existing id on conflict; or (b) drop the `kind = 'ferment_started'` filter so the short-circuit keys purely on idempotency_key (matching record_lot_event's global uniqueness) and raise if the existing event's kind ≠ ferment_started rather than silently forking a batch. Add a DB test that pre-seeds a colliding key under another kind and asserts NO second batch + a backing event always exists.

findings.title: Reading/water row persists but its hash-chained ledger event is silently dropped when the derived 'ferment-reading:'/'mill-water:' key collides in lot_event
findings.dimension: DATA-LAYER INVARIANT BYPASS (append-only provenance / one-txn atomicity)
findings.location: record_ferment_reading lines 302-318 ('ferment-reading:' || p_idempotency_key) and log_mill_water lines 351-365 ('mill-water:' || p_idempotency_key)
findings.problem: The readings table dedupes on the RAW p_idempotency_key, but the lot_event append uses a DERIVED key ('ferment-reading:'+key). The two namespaces are independent, so a reading can insert (raw key free) while its lot_event append no-ops via ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (derived key already taken). record_ferment_reading does NOT check record_lot_event's return — it returns the reading id regardless. Result: a reading row exists in the immutable series with NO ferment_reading event in the chain. The curve/cut-point views read from ferment_readings, so the data looks fine in the UI, but the hash-chained evidence (the actual durable provenance the slice promises) is missing.
findings.scenario: Empirically confirmed in PGlite: pre-seed `record_lot_event(…, 'ferment-reading:READKEY')`, then `record_ferment_reading(batch,'ph',4.5,…,'READKEY')`. Result: 1 reading row, 0 ferment_reading events with key 'ferment-reading:READKEY'. Reachable: an attacker/owner who can name idempotency keys (direct authenticated RPC, or a crafted offline-outbox replay) can deterministically suppress the ledger event for any reading while keeping the reading visible — quietly de-anchoring data from the chain. Even without malice, any genuine raw-key reuse across the reading and a prior event silently loses the event.
findings.fix: Either share ONE namespace (pass the raw p_idempotency_key to record_lot_event and let it dedupe), or capture record_lot_event's returned event_uid and RAISE if it is null/unexpected (a collision must abort the whole txn, not silently lose the event). Do the same in log_mill_water. Add a DB test that pre-occupies the derived key and asserts the whole RPC fails closed (reading is NOT inserted without its event).

findings.title: NULL idempotency_key fully bypasses exactly-once on readings, water log, AND lot_event (multiple NULLs allowed in a UNIQUE column)
findings.dimension: DATA-LAYER INVARIANT BYPASS (exactly-once / append-only replay safety)
findings.location: ferment_readings.idempotency_key (line 126, `text unique` nullable), mill_water_log.idempotency_key (line 159), and the RPC dedup at lines 292 / 341 (`where idempotency_key = p_idempotency_key` is never true for NULL)
findings.problem: All idempotency_key columns are nullable UNIQUE. In Postgres a UNIQUE constraint permits unlimited NULLs, and `idempotency_key = NULL` is never true, so the RPC's replay short-circuit (`select … where idempotency_key = p_idempotency_key`) and the `ON CONFLICT (idempotency_key) DO NOTHING` are both no-ops when the key is NULL. The exactly-once / append-only-replay invariant — the headline guarantee of the offline outbox (P2-S0/D4) — is therefore bypassable: pass NULL and every call inserts a fresh row. Worse, in record_ferment_reading the derived event key becomes `'ferment-reading:' || NULL = NULL`, so the lot_event also gets a NULL idempotency_key — defeating exactly-once on the ledger itself.
findings.scenario: Empirically confirmed in PGlite: two `record_ferment_reading(batch,'ph',4.8,…, null)` calls insert 2 reading rows (exactly-once would be 1). The Server Action always mints a UUID, but the RPC is granted to authenticated and directly callable via PostgREST with p_idempotency_key => null, bypassing the TS validator entirely. For a single-owner farm the abuse risk is low, but the correctness risk is real: any client/offline-sync bug that submits a null/blank key silently double-records ferment readings and water draws, corrupting the curve and the L/kg sustainability number that Phase-3/4 dossiers read.
findings.fix: Make idempotency_key NOT NULL on ferment_readings, mill_water_log (and ideally lot_event), or add explicit `if p_idempotency_key is null then raise exception` guards at the top of each RPC. Add a DB test asserting a NULL/blank key is rejected (fail-closed), not silently double-inserted.

findings.title: apply_ferment_recipe uses whole-second epoch as device_seq → unique(device_id,device_seq) collision aborts a legitimate rebind
findings.dimension: DATA-LAYER INVARIANT BYPASS (ledger write-door reliability / advisory-timing race)
findings.location: apply_ferment_recipe line 216: `now(), 'server-ferment', extract(epoch from clock_timestamp())::bigint, …`
findings.problem: apply_ferment_recipe derives device_seq from `extract(epoch from clock_timestamp())::bigint` — whole-second resolution — and hardcodes device_id='server-ferment'. lot_event has `unique (device_id, device_seq)` across ALL events. Two recipe applications for the same fixed device_id within the same wall-clock second collide on that unique key and the second RPC throws a raw 23505. This is the ONE write door for binding/rebinding a recipe; under realistic timing (rebind a wrong recipe immediately, or two batches getting recipes seeded in the same second) it fails. It also means device_seq is not a real Lamport/causal counter here — it is a clock value, diverging from the offline-replay contract the rest of the slice honors by passing client device_seq.
findings.scenario: Empirically confirmed in PGlite: `apply_ferment_recipe(batch,'rec-A')` then `apply_ferment_recipe(batch,'rec-B')` back-to-back → second call: `duplicate key value violates unique constraint "lot_event_device_id_device_seq_key"`. Only 1 recipe_applied event lands; the rebind is lost and the user sees a raw constraint error (no command wrapper / friendly mapping exists for apply_ferment_recipe at all — it has no TS command or Server Action).
findings.fix: Accept p_device_id/p_device_seq/p_idempotency_key as parameters (like every other S3 RPC) and let the client mint a unique device_seq, or use a per-stream monotonic counter. Make it idempotent on a real key rather than relying on clock uniqueness. Also add a TS command + action + friendly-error mapping so the rebind path exists and surfaces clean errors.

findings.title: ferment_readings.value has no DB-layer bound — out-of-range pH (only the TS validator enforces 0–14) corrupts the cut-point signal
findings.dimension: DATA-LAYER INVARIANT BYPASS (validation lives in the app, not the data layer)
findings.location: ferment_readings.value line 121 (`value numeric not null` — NO check); record_ferment_reading line 304 inserts p_value unchecked; v_ferment_cutpoint line 411 (`lp.latest_ph <= rec.target_ph`)
findings.problem: The pH 0–14 bound exists only in the TS validator (recordFermentReading.ts validateFermentReading). The RPC and table impose no CHECK, and the RPC is granted to authenticated (directly callable, bypassing the validator). A reading of pH = -5 (or 0, or 99) lands in the append-only series, and because the cut-point fires on `latest_ph <= target_ph`, a single absurd low value FALSELY signals 'cut the ferment' — the reposo/quality decision this slice exists to drive. The project's own rule is 'promise → enforcement at the data layer, not an honor system in the UI'; here the cup-defining pH gate is honor-system-only.
findings.scenario: Authenticated direct RPC: `record_ferment_reading(batch,'ph',-1,…)` (or a buggy/offline client that skips the validator) inserts pH -1; v_ferment_cutpoint then reports cut_reached=true for any positive target_ph. On an immutable, append-only series this bad reading can never be corrected (only appended over), permanently skewing the curve and the BoP-grade cut decision.
findings.fix: Add table-level CHECKs mirroring the kind: `check (reading_kind <> 'ph' or (value >= 0 and value <= 14))` and a sane non-negative bound for brix; reject NaN. Enforce in the RPC too. Add a DB test asserting an out-of-range pH is rejected at the data layer.

findings.title: Reading occurred_at is unbounded vs the batch window — a reading before started_at yields negative hours_elapsed and can become the cut-driving 'latest' reading
findings.dimension: DATA-LAYER INVARIANT BYPASS (cut-point ordering / time-window integrity)
findings.location: record_ferment_reading (no occurred_at vs started_at check), v_ferment_curve line 386 (`(r.occurred_at - b.started_at)/3600`), v_ferment_cutpoint lateral `order by r.occurred_at desc, r.id desc` (lines ~405-411)
findings.problem: occurred_at is a client wall-clock with no constraint that it falls within (or after) the batch's started_at, and no batch-ended gate (the ended_at column exists but no RPC sets it, and nothing blocks post-end readings). v_ferment_cutpoint picks the latest pH by `occurred_at desc`. A reading whose occurred_at is set far in the future jumps to 'latest' and drives cut_reached regardless of the true sequence; a reading before started_at produces a negative hours_elapsed on the curve x-axis.
findings.scenario: Empirically confirmed in PGlite: batch started 12:00, reading at 06:00 → v_ferment_curve.hours_elapsed = -6.0. And a reading with occurred_at = 2030 inserts cleanly (no gate), then dominates the cut-point lateral as 'latest_ph'. Reachable via the offline outbox (skewed device clocks are the explicit D5 motivation) or a direct RPC. The cut decision is the reposo/cup quality gate, so a mis-ordered 'latest' reading is a quality-data integrity bug.
findings.fix: Constrain occurred_at >= started_at (and, if a batch can end, <= ended_at) in record_ferment_reading; clamp/guard negative hours_elapsed in the views; consider ordering the cut-point lateral by (recorded_at, id) or (device_seq) rather than client occurred_at so a skewed field clock can't reorder the cut signal. Add a DB test asserting a pre-start reading is rejected or excluded from the cut signal.

findings.title: por-obra supersede is window-blind; v_active_por_obra excludes superseded rows, so payroll cannot resolve a past date's rate once a second contract is signed
findings.location: sign_por_obra_contract supersede UPDATE lines 637-640; v_active_por_obra lines 456-477
findings.problem: The supersede UPDATE is unconditional — `where worker_id=p_worker_id and task_kind=p_task_kind and id<>new_id and superseded_by is null` — it stamps superseded_by on EVERY still-open contract for that worker+task, regardless of date window. But v_active_por_obra (the rate-resolver payroll calls) filters `c.superseded_by is null` AND the date window. Those two mechanisms contradict each other for historical lookup: as soon as a second contract is signed, the first is marked superseded and becomes invisible to v_active_por_obra for ANY date, including dates inside its own effective window. This directly violates the slice's stated invariant ('the rate a worker agreed to on a given day is forever auditable' — header lines 213-216) and the function's own doc ('the EFFECTIVE, non-superseded contract for a worker+task on a date').
findings.scenario: A picker signs contract A: task 'picking', effective_from 2026-01-01, effective_to 2026-06-30, $0.50/lata. Mid-season the rate is renegotiated; the owner signs contract B effective_from 2026-07-01 at $0.60/lata. sign_por_obra_contract stamps A.superseded_by=B (A was open). Payroll later recomputes a January lata count (back-pay correction or an audit): `select * from v_active_por_obra('w-03','picking','2026-01-15')` returns ZERO rows because A is excluded by `superseded_by is null`, even though Jan-15 is inside A's window. The January piece-work pay can no longer be priced — a money/compliance correctness failure for the ~90% Ngäbe-Buglé piece-rate crew.
findings.fix: Make the two mechanisms consistent. Either (a) drop the `superseded_by is null` predicate from v_active_por_obra and let the window + `order by effective_from desc, id desc limit 1` pick the rate (treat supersede as audit metadata only, not a resolution filter); or (b) make the supersede window-aware so only a contract whose window OVERLAPS the new one is superseded (e.g. close A by setting its effective_to instead of marking it superseded). Add a DB-level test that signs A then B and asserts v_active_por_obra still resolves A's rate for a date inside A's window.

findings.title: sign_por_obra_contract / record_certification exactly-once is a read-then-write TOCTOU: the domain row is inserted OUTSIDE the idempotency-key conflict guard, so a concurrent double-submit creates two contracts/certs
findings.location: sign_por_obra_contract lines 618-649 (esp. insert at 629); record_certification lines 671-690 (esp. insert at 680); por_obra_contracts/worker_certifications tables (lines 217-248) have NO idempotency_key column/unique
findings.problem: Exactly-once for these two RPCs is anchored ONLY on a `select 1 from worker_stream_event where idempotency_key=...` check at function entry (lines 618 / 671), then the contract/cert row is INSERTed (lines 629 / 680) with no dedupe of its own — por_obra_contracts and worker_certifications have neither an idempotency_key column nor a unique on it. The only deduped insert is the trailing worker_stream_event (`on conflict (idempotency_key) do nothing`). Under READ COMMITTED (the Postgres default), two concurrent calls with the SAME idempotency_key both pass the entry check (neither event is committed yet), both insert a contract/cert, then race on the event insert; one wins, one is a no-op. Net result: TWO domain rows for one logical signing. This is unlike record_attendance/enroll_crew_member, whose domain mutation is the conflict-guarded event insert itself (attendance) or guarded by the one-active membership index (enroll), so they are race-safe.
findings.scenario: Owner double-taps 'Sign contract' (or an offline retry fires twice before the first response). Both requests carry the same idempotencyKey (minted once by idempotencyKeyOrNew, reused on retry). Both transactions: pass the entry `if exists` check, INSERT a por_obra_contracts row (ids 10 and 11), both run the unconditional supersede UPDATE, then race on the worker_stream_event insert. Two contract rows now exist for one signing; the supersede UPDATE in the second txn may even mark the first 'duplicate' superseded, so the roster shows a phantom rate change. For certifications the same race double-issues a hazard-work cert that S12's spray gate then trusts.
findings.fix: Make the domain insert itself idempotent: add `idempotency_key text unique` to por_obra_contracts and worker_certifications, insert it with `on conflict (idempotency_key) do nothing returning id`, and if no row returns, re-select the existing id (the pattern record_attendance already uses for attendance_event). Then the unique index — not a pre-check — enforces exactly-once even under concurrency. Add a concurrency/regression test.

findings.title: Crew-name slug folds only LOWERCASE accents (translate runs before lower); an uppercase-accented crew name (Ñ/Á…) can collide on the crews PK and abort the whole backfill/seed
findings.location: _backfill_people slug expression line 117: trim(both '-' from regexp_replace(lower(translate(c,'áéíóúñ','aeioun')),'[^a-z0-9]+','-','g'))
findings.problem: `lower(translate(c,'áéíóúñ','aeioun'))` applies translate BEFORE lower, so only the lowercase accented chars á é í ó ú ñ are folded to ASCII. Any UPPERCASE accented char (Á É Í Ó Ú Ñ) survives translate, is lowercased to its accented lowercase form, then STRIPPED by `[^a-z0-9]+`. Two distinct crew names can therefore slug to the same id, and because the crews insert dedupes on `on conflict (name) do nothing` (line 120) — NOT on id — a colliding id raises a duplicate-key error on crews_pkey that aborts _backfill_people() (and thus the migration's own call at line 139 and every seed.sql run that calls it). For this farm, accented/Ngäbe crew names ('Ñurum', 'Ángel', 'Río Sereno') are highly plausible, not exotic.
findings.scenario: A future crew string 'Ñurum Norte' and another 'Nurum Norte' (or 'Ángeles' vs 'Angeles') both reach `select distinct crew from workers`. 'Ñurum Norte' -> translate leaves Ñ -> lower -> 'ñurum norte' -> regexp strips ñ -> 'urum-norte'. 'Nurum Norte' -> 'nurum-norte'. Different here, but 'Ñorte' vs 'Orte', or any name whose only distinguishing char is an uppercase accent, collides to the same id -> second insert hits crews_pkey -> _backfill_people raises -> seed.sql (which calls it at line 58) fails and the whole DB bootstrap aborts. Even a single name that folds to empty (e.g. a standalone 'Ñ') yields id '' and the second such name collides.
findings.fix: Fold accents AFTER lowercasing, covering both cases: `regexp_replace(translate(lower(c), 'áéíóúñ', 'aeioun'), '[^a-z0-9]+','-','g')` (lower first, then translate the now-lowercase accents). Optionally guard against an empty slug (coalesce to a hash of the name) and change the crews insert to also tolerate id collisions, or make the conflict target the id. Add a backfill test with an accented crew name.

findings.title: enroll_crew_member with a backdated occurred_at can violate the crew_memberships left_at>=joined_at CHECK and raise, blocking a legitimate membership correction
findings.location: crew_memberships CHECK line 67 (`check (left_at is null or left_at >= joined_at)`); enroll_crew_member close-membership UPDATE lines 567-569
findings.problem: enroll_crew_member closes the worker's current active membership with `set left_at = p_occurred_at`. The CHECK on crew_memberships requires `left_at >= joined_at`. p_occurred_at comes straight from the caller (the TS validator accepts ANY valid ISO timestamp, including a backdated one), but joined_at on the open row is whatever it was set to earlier (the backfill uses default now(); a prior enroll used that enroll's occurred_at). Nothing in the RPC guarantees p_occurred_at >= the open membership's joined_at, so a backdated enroll fails the CHECK and the whole RPC raises a check_violation instead of doing the move.
findings.scenario: A worker was enrolled into Crew Norte today (joined_at = now()). The supervisor realizes the worker actually moved to Crew Tizingal yesterday and submits an enroll with occurredAt = yesterday to correct the record. The UPDATE tries to set the Norte membership's left_at = yesterday, but joined_at = today -> `left_at >= joined_at` is false -> the RPC raises `new row for relation "crew_memberships" violates check constraint`. The legitimate correction is impossible, and the surfaced error is a raw constraint message, not a friendly one.
findings.fix: Either clamp the close timestamp (`set left_at = greatest(p_occurred_at, joined_at)`), or validate p_occurred_at against the open membership's joined_at inside the RPC and raise a friendly, domain-specific error, or relax the CHECK if backdated closes are intended. Add a test that enrolls then re-enrolls with a backdated occurred_at.

findings.title: Worker-ledger hash chain selects the stream head by device_seq desc, but the actions.ts next_server_seq fallback can inject a Date.now()-sized seq that permanently poisons chain ordering
findings.location: worker_stream_event_set_hash lines 259-278 and attendance_event_set_hash lines 286-305 (head select `order by e.device_seq desc limit 1`); enabling defect in src/app/(app)/crew/actions.ts nextServerSeq fallback lines 78-83
findings.problem: Both worker-ledger BEFORE-INSERT triggers pick prev_hash from `order by device_seq desc limit 1`, and verify_chain-style recomputation orders ascending by device_seq — i.e. the schema assumes device_seq is strictly monotonic per stream in insertion order. That assumption is silently breakable: when next_server_seq() errors, the server action's fallback returns `Date.now()` (~1.7e12) as the device_seq (actions.ts line 81). That value is orders of magnitude larger than the worker_server_seq nextval values (1,2,3,…). An event minted with the fallback seq becomes a permanent maximum, so it stays the chain head forever; a subsequent NORMAL event (seq e.g. 53) is inserted later in real time but sorts BEFORE the fallback event by device_seq, gets its prev_hash from the fallback event, and the by-device_seq recomputation then expects seq-53 to chain from the prior small-seq event — a hash-chain ordering break that makes the stream un-verifiable, undermining the append-only/tamper-evident guarantee the slice is built on.
findings.scenario: During a brief PostgREST/RPC hiccup, recordCertificationAction's nextServerSeq draw errors once and falls back to device_seq=1718900000000. That WORKER_CERTIFIED event lands as the head. The next legitimate enroll draws seq=54. Insert-order is fallback-event then enroll; device_seq-order is enroll(54) then fallback(1.7e12). The enroll's prev_hash now points at the fallback event, but a verify pass that walks by ascending device_seq sees enroll first with a prev_hash that doesn't match the (empty) prior head -> verify fails permanently for that worker's stream.
findings.fix: Don't let a draw failure fabricate an out-of-band seq: surface the next_server_seq error to the caller (fail the action) instead of returning Date.now(). Defense-in-depth at the schema layer: have the trigger pick the head by `recorded_at desc, device_seq desc` (true insertion order) or reject a device_seq that is not exactly previous_head_seq+1 for the 'server' device, so a non-monotonic seq is refused rather than silently poisoning the chain.

findings.title: LogReadingForm reuses one idempotency key across distinct readings; the 2nd+ reading is silently DROPPED and no test catches it
findings.location: line 34 (key minted once per mount) + 42 (carried as hidden field); test src/components/sections/ferment/__tests__/log-reading-form.test.tsx:34-39
findings.problem: `LogReadingForm` mints its exactly-once anchor ONCE per mount via `useState(() => crypto.randomUUID())` and never regenerates it after a successful submit. The form stays mounted while the user logs many readings (pH, then temp, then Brix, then another pH an hour later). Every submit from that mounted form carries the SAME `idempotencyKey`. `record_ferment_reading` short-circuits on `select id ... where idempotency_key = p_idempotency_key` (migration 20260622092000_fermentation.sql) so the SECOND reading dedupes to the FIRST and returns its id — the row is silently dropped, yet the action returns `status:"success"` ("Reading logged."). The live ferment curve — the asset the cut-point and the Best-of-Panama cup are decided on — silently loses readings. The form's own comment (line 19/32) advertises 'a stable idempotency key so a double-submit dedupes to one reading', but the key is TOO stable: it dedupes DISTINCT readings, not just double-submits. The form test (log-reading-form.test.tsx:34) asserts only that a non-empty key renders on a single mount; it never submits twice. The DB 'exactly-once on idempotency_key' test (s_p2s3_fermentation.db.test.ts:260-274) deliberately reuses the SAME key to PROVE dedupe — it structurally tests the opposite scenario and can never catch this. So the highest-value behavior in the slice (a live curve accumulating multiple readings) has zero coverage and is broken.
findings.scenario: Open /ferment on a live JC-800 Geisha batch. Log pH 5.4 (lands, 'Reading logged.'). An hour later, without leaving the page, log pH 5.0 → the action reports success but `record_ferment_reading` returns the first reading's id; ferment_readings still has only ONE row. The cut-point view still reads 5.4, the cut never fires, the ferment over-runs and the lot's cup is damaged — with a green 'Reading logged.' confirmation each time.
findings.fix: Regenerate the idempotency key after each successful submit so distinct readings get distinct keys while a true double-submit (same render) still dedupes: e.g. `const [idempotencyKey, setKey] = useState(() => crypto.randomUUID()); useEffect(() => { if (state.status === 'success') setKey(crypto.randomUUID()); }, [state]);` (key it on a submit nonce, not the action result identity, to avoid loops). ADD the missing test: render LogReadingForm, submit reading A, then reading B from the same mount, and assert the action received TWO calls with DISTINCT idempotencyKey values (or an integration test asserting both rows persist). That test FAILS on today's code.

findings.title: apply_ferment_recipe is granted EXECUTE and listed as a command RPC but its BEHAVIOR is never tested — and it is broken (same-second device_seq collision)
findings.location: apply_ferment_recipe body (device_seq = extract(epoch from clock_timestamp())::bigint); grant-only coverage at src/test/db/s_p2s3_fermentation.db.test.ts:443-459
findings.problem: `apply_ferment_recipe` appends its lot_event with a hardcoded `device_id='server-ferment'` and `device_seq = extract(epoch from clock_timestamp())::bigint` (WHOLE seconds). `lot_event` has `unique (device_id, device_seq)` (migration 20260621092000:223). Two `apply_ferment_recipe` calls within the same wall-second therefore collide on that unique key and the second RAISES (CONFIRMED via throwaway PGlite repro: `duplicate key value violates unique constraint "lot_event_device_id_device_seq_key"`). The shipped suite's ONLY coverage of this RPC is the AD-8 grant assertion (test 'grants EXECUTE on the command RPCs to authenticated', line 443) — it asserts the write door is OPEN but never calls the RPC. So a behaviorally-broken write door passes the slice's tests with a green checkmark: the grant test gives false confidence that the RPC works. This is exactly the 'test that pins the wrong thing' failure mode — it pins access, not behavior.
findings.scenario: If apply_ferment_recipe is ever wired into the UI (it is a designed command — re-binding/rebinding a batch's recipe version), an operator binding then immediately re-binding a recipe (or two batches getting a recipe in the same second) gets a raw 'duplicate key' 500. Today the RPC is dead (not called by any server action), which caps reachability — but the grant test actively masks the defect for whoever wires it next.
findings.fix: Make apply_ferment_recipe accept the client-minted device_id/device_seq/idempotency_key envelope like the other three RPCs (instead of the same-second epoch), matching the offline-replayable contract the migration header claims for 'every RPC'. ADD a real behavior test that calls apply_ferment_recipe twice back-to-back and asserts both succeed and exactly one 'ferment_recipe_applied' event lands per distinct (batch,recipe) — that test FAILS today. Until then, do NOT treat the grant test as coverage.

findings.title: Readings exactly-once is bypassable with a NULL idempotency_key at the RPC layer — untested contract
findings.location: record_ferment_reading / log_mill_water short-circuit `select id ... where idempotency_key = p_idempotency_key`; no NULL-key test in s_p2s3_fermentation.db.test.ts
findings.problem: The exactly-once short-circuit in record_ferment_reading (and log_mill_water) is `where idempotency_key = p_idempotency_key`; with a NULL key `= NULL` is never true, and `idempotency_key text unique` permits multiple NULLs in Postgres. So two NULL-key calls BOTH insert (CONFIRMED via throwaway PGlite repro: 2 rows landed). The shipped 'is exactly-once on idempotency_key' test (line 260-274) only exercises a non-null key, so the RPC's NULL-key dedupe-bypass is an untested branch of the very invariant the test claims to pin. The live wired path is currently protected (withEnvelope in src/app/(app)/ferment/actions.ts:67-70 always defaults a key, and the validators reject empty keys), so this is latent, not reachable-today — but the RPC contract itself is unguarded and unpinned, and the offline outbox (P2-S0) is the kind of caller that could submit a null/blank key.
findings.scenario: A future direct/offline-replay caller (or a refactor that drops the validator) submits two reads with a null idempotency_key → both persist, double-counting the curve. No test fails.
findings.fix: Either reject NULL idempotency_key in the RPC (`if p_idempotency_key is null then raise ...`) or make the unique index NULLS-NOT-DISTINCT. ADD a DB test asserting two NULL-key reading calls either raise or dedupe to one row (asserting the chosen contract).

findings.title: No range guard or test on temp/Brix readings — only pH is bounded; a sensor-fault value lands silently
findings.location: validateFermentReading lines 67-73 (range check only for kind==='ph'); migration ferment_readings has no value CHECK
findings.problem: The reading value has NO bound in the DB (ferment_readings.value is a bare numeric) and the validator range-checks ONLY pH (0-14, line 70). A temp of 9999 °C or a negative Brix lands silently (CONFIRMED via throwaway repro: value 9999 persisted). The cut-point/curve are 'evidence' assets, but a fat-fingered or BLE-probe-fault temp/Brix corrupts the curve with no guard and no test. The slice has a test for the pH 0-14 bound (recordFermentReading.test.ts:66-70) but none asserting temp/Brix sanity — so the asymmetry (pH guarded, temp/Brix wide open) is invisible and the highest-value validation gap is uncovered.
findings.scenario: A worker logs a Brix probe reading while the probe is in air or a temp reading mis-keyed as 9999; it persists and skews the curve and any Phase-3/4 dossier derived from it.
findings.fix: Add reasonable bounds in validateFermentReading per kind (e.g. temp 0-60 °C, Brix 0-40) and/or a DB CHECK, then ADD tests for an out-of-range temp and Brix that assert rejection — mirroring the existing pH-range test. Each new test FAILS on today's code.

findings.title: Cut-point 'latest pH' is never tested against an out-of-order (late-arriving offline) reading — the P2-S0 reorder path is unpinned
findings.location: cut-point describe block lines 297-349 (readings always inserted in occurred_at order); view ordering in migration v_ferment_cutpoint lateral `order by r.occurred_at desc, r.id desc`
findings.problem: v_ferment_cutpoint picks 'latest pH' by `order by occurred_at desc, id desc`. The shipped cut-point tests insert readings in strictly increasing occurred_at order (08:00 → 10:00 → 12:00), so they would pass even if the view ordered by insertion id alone. The whole point of carrying device_id/device_seq + dual clocks (the migration header touts 'every reading is offline-replayable, P2-S0') is that an offline reading can ARRIVE late (high recorded_at) but carry an EARLIER occurred_at — and must NOT be treated as 'latest', or it would resurrect a stale high pH and un-fire the cut. That reorder scenario — the slice's defining offline promise — has zero coverage, so the ordering invariant is not actually pinned. The client mirror projectCutPoint sorts by hoursElapsed and curve-fit.test.ts:68 covers input-order independence, but the SQL view (the source of truth) does not.
findings.scenario: Batch crosses target (pH 4.1 logged at 12:00). A late-syncing offline reading with occurred_at 09:00 (pH 5.6) arrives at 13:00 and inserts with a higher id. If the view ever regressed to id ordering, cut_reached would flip back to false. No test would catch the regression.
findings.fix: ADD a cut-point test that records a pH 4.1 at a LATER occurred_at, then a pH 5.6 at an EARLIER occurred_at inserted AFTERWARD (higher id), and assert cut_reached stays true and latest_ph is 4.1 — pinning occurred_at (not id/insert order) as the 'latest' axis. Also add the no-recipe-bound case (target_ph null ⇒ cut_reached false even at pH 2), which is currently untested though correct.

findings.title: Stray untracked repro test files left in the test tree (scaffolding from prior probing)
findings.location: src/test/db/_s1repro.db.test.ts (untracked); plus _probe_s3.db.test.ts / _zz_repro_devseq.db.test.ts scaffolding observed during review
findings.problem: Throwaway probe/repro test files prefixed `_` sit in src/test/db. They use `expect(true).toBe(true)` + console.log (e.g. the device_seq-collision and NULL-key repros) — i.e. they assert nothing and run as part of `npm run test`, padding the suite with non-assertions and noise. `_s1repro.db.test.ts` is untracked and belongs to a different slice. A reviewer skimming green test counts could mistake these for real coverage; they are the inverse of test efficacy.
findings.scenario: A future agent counts 'N passing ferment tests' including vacuous `expect(true).toBe(true)` probes and over-credits the slice's coverage; or the noisy console.log spam buries a real failure.
findings.fix: Delete the `_`-prefixed repro/probe files (or fold the genuine findings they surfaced — apply_ferment_recipe collision, NULL-key bypass — into REAL asserting tests as recommended above). Per the standing rule, throwaway repros must be removed once their finding is captured.

findings.title: Exactly-once invariant bypassable via NULL idempotency_key in record_maturation_signal (and schedule_pasada/replan_pasada)
findings.location: record_maturation_signal body (the `if exists (select 1 from maturation_signal where idempotency_key = p_idempotency_key)` guard) + the table's `idempotency_key text unique`; same shape in schedule_pasada and replan_pasada
findings.problem: The idempotency guard is `if exists (... where idempotency_key = p_idempotency_key) then return;`. When `p_idempotency_key` is NULL, `idempotency_key = NULL` evaluates to UNKNOWN, so the guard NEVER short-circuits; and a `UNIQUE` column permits unlimited NULLs in Postgres, so the unique constraint does not catch it either. The append-only/exactly-once ledger invariant is therefore bypassable: an authenticated caller hitting the RPC directly via PostgREST with a null key writes a brand-new ledger row (and a new phenology upsert / a new pasada plan + a new fired task) on EVERY call. The Server Action always mints `crypto.randomUUID()`, so the /plan UI is safe, but the SECURITY DEFINER RPC is granted to `authenticated` and is callable directly outside the action — this is exactly the 'untrusted-driven write door' the dimension asks to harden.
findings.scenario: REPRODUCED in PGlite against the real migration: `select record_maturation_signal('p-x','2026-01-01',1000,null,now(),'d',1,null);` then the same with seq=2 and key=null again → `select count(*) from maturation_signal where plot_id='p-x'` returns 2. Two ledger rows from two 'replays' that should have been idempotent. Same for schedule_pasada (two plans + two fired tasks) with a null key.
findings.fix: Make the key mandatory and non-coalescible: add `not null` to `idempotency_key` on all three command tables (it is already `unique`), and/or guard at the top of each RPC with `if p_idempotency_key is null then raise exception 'idempotency_key required' using errcode='23502'; end if;`. With a NOT NULL + UNIQUE key the `on conflict (idempotency_key) do nothing` and the `if exists` guard both become real exactly-once gates.

findings.title: No uniqueness on active (plot_id, pasada_number): duplicate active pasada plans accumulate and replan_pasada only supersedes ONE
findings.location: pasada_schedule table (no partial unique index on (plot_id, pasada_number) where status<>'superseded'); schedule_pasada (unconditional insert); replan_pasada (`select id ... where ... status <> 'superseded' order by recorded_at desc limit 1` then supersede only that one)
findings.problem: The append-only/superseded chain assumes exactly one ACTIVE plan per (plot, pasada), but nothing enforces it. `schedule_pasada` inserts unconditionally, so calling it twice for the same (plot, pasada) creates two active plans and fires two tasks. `replan_pasada` then supersedes only the single most-recent active row (`order by recorded_at desc limit 1`), so the older duplicate active plan(s) are never superseded — they live forever in `v_pasada_calendar`, which filters only on `status<>'superseded'`. The 'one morning plan per pasada' invariant the slice advertises is not a data-layer guarantee.
findings.scenario: REPRODUCED in PGlite: two `schedule_pasada('p-x','2026',1,...)` calls (distinct idempotency keys) → `count(*) where pasada_number=1 and status<>'superseded'` = 2 active plans + 2 fired tasks. A subsequent `replan_pasada('p-x','2026',1,...)` raises the active count to 3 (it adds its own and supersedes only one), and v_pasada_calendar shows multiple conflicting 'active' passes for the same plot+pasada. The picker board then carries two/three contradictory harvest-pass tasks for the same pass.
findings.fix: Add `create unique index pasada_schedule_one_active on pasada_schedule (plot_id, pasada_number) where status <> 'superseded';` so a second active plan for the same (plot, pasada) is rejected at the data layer (force callers through replan_pasada). Have schedule_pasada raise a friendly 'already planned — re-plan instead' on the conflict, and have replan_pasada supersede every active row for the (plot, pasada), not just the latest.

findings.title: Planner write door is fail-closed-fragile: constant device_id + millisecond device_seq collides on the UNIQUE(device_id,device_seq), unlike the crew slice's next_server_seq fix
findings.location: writeEnvelope() lines 36-45: `PLANNER_DEVICE_ID = "planner-console"` (const) + `p_device_seq: now.getTime()` (ms); against `pasada_schedule`/`maturation_signal` `unique (device_id, device_seq)`
findings.problem: Every planner write uses the SAME device_id and a millisecond-resolution seq. Two writes in the same millisecond (rapid double-click, two managers, or any environment where the wall clock does not advance between calls) produce the same (device_id, device_seq) and the second INSERT throws `duplicate key value violates unique constraint`. The crew slice explicitly documented and fixed this exact anti-pattern with a SECURITY DEFINER `next_server_seq()` draw (src/app/(app)/crew/actions.ts lines 65-84: 'a constant collides on the second write system-wide'); S8 reintroduces the bug the rest of the codebase already solved. friendlyError() maps the raw unique-violation to the generic 'We couldn't save that plan' so the manager just sees a mysterious failure on a legitimate action.
findings.scenario: REPRODUCED in PGlite: `schedule_pasada(...,'planner-console',1700000000000,'s1')` succeeds; a second `schedule_pasada(...,'planner-console',1700000000000,'s2')` (same seq, the collision a same-millisecond submit produces) throws `duplicate key value violates unique constraint "pasada_schedule_device_id_device_seq_key"`. Because device_seq is shared across BOTH tables for this device_id... (note: tables have separate constraints, so the collision is per-table, but a same-ms double-submit to one table is enough).
findings.fix: Mirror the crew slice: draw device_seq from the existing `next_server_seq()` SECURITY DEFINER sequence (strictly increasing, collision-proof) instead of `now.getTime()`, keeping a constant device_id. This makes (device_id, device_seq) unique forever and the offline-replay envelope honest.

findings.title: ALTER TYPE ADD VALUE runs outside begin/commit but Supabase wraps each migration file in one transaction — the only enum-extension in the repo, no precedent, deploy-time portability risk
findings.location: line ~`alter type task_category add value if not exists 'Harvest';` placed before the `begin;` block, with a comment asserting it 'runs OUTSIDE the begin/commit block (autocommit)'
findings.problem: This is the ONLY `ALTER TYPE ... ADD VALUE` in the entire migration set (grep across supabase/migrations confirms no other precedent), so the deploy path is untested by sibling slices. The file places the statement before `begin;`, but the Supabase migration runner / `supabase db push` executes each migration file inside a single wrapping transaction regardless of in-file `begin;`/`commit;` markers. On that path the bare `ALTER TYPE ADD VALUE` can fail with 'ALTER TYPE ... ADD VALUE cannot be executed inside a transaction block' (or the new value being unusable in the same txn). PGlite's `exec` tolerates it (the harness comment notes it runs autocommit), so the test suite is GREEN and masks the deploy-time hazard — i.e. the guard passes locally but the real `db push` may not.
findings.scenario: On real Postgres via `supabase db push`, if the runner wraps the file in BEGIN…COMMIT, the migration aborts at the ALTER TYPE line; the slice never lands. Even where PG12+ permits ADD VALUE in a txn, the value cannot be referenced in the same txn — not hit here because schedule_pasada only references 'Harvest' at call time, but it is one refactor away from biting.
findings.fix: Split the enum extension into its own dedicated migration file that contains ONLY `alter type task_category add value if not exists 'Harvest';` (and no begin/commit), or confirm the project's runner does not wrap files and document it. Add a deploy-path test (not just PGlite) or a note that this file must be applied with the transaction wrapper disabled.

findings.title: task_category TS union omits 'Harvest' while S8 now writes category='Harvest' into the tasks board the /tasks UI reads
findings.location: TaskCategory union lines 92-99 ('Pruning'|'Fertilizing'|'Pest Control'|'Weeding'|'Planting'|'Irrigation'|'Soil') — no 'Harvest'; consumed by FarmTask.category (line 106) and tasks_view
findings.problem: schedule_pasada/replan_pasada insert `category = 'Harvest'` into the real phase-1 `tasks` table, and `tasks_view.category` flows into the typed `TaskCategory` union. 'Harvest' is NOT a member of that union, so the data-layer truth and the TypeScript contract have drifted. Any /tasks UI code that exhaustively switches on TaskCategory (badge color, icon, filter) will have no case for harvest tasks — they render with a fallback/blank, or a `never`-exhaustiveness check silently misses them. This is the cross-slice integration seam the migration's own FLAG comment calls out ('the one place the slice extends a shared phase-1 domain type') but the TS side was never updated.
findings.scenario: A fired pasada task appears on /tasks with `category:'Harvest'`; the tasks board's category badge/legend (built from the TaskCategory union) has no styling or filter entry for it, so the harvest pass shows up uncategorized/unstyled and is invisible to a 'filter by category' control.
findings.fix: Add `| "Harvest"` to the TaskCategory union in src/lib/types.ts (with a render/smoke test asserting a Harvest-category task renders on the board), keeping the TS contract in lockstep with the enum the migration added.

findings.title: Stray untracked repro test file left in the DB test tree (cross-agent hygiene)
findings.location: untracked file matching the `src/**/*.db.test.ts` glob, so it runs as part of `npm run test`
findings.problem: An untracked throwaway repro (`_s1repro.db.test.ts`, an S1-slice probe — not part of S8) sits in the db-test directory and is picked up by the test runner's `src/**/*.db.test.ts` include. A leftover ad-hoc repro in the suite dilutes the green-suite signal (it asserts whatever a prior agent was probing, not a slice invariant) and can mask or muddy real failures. Per the repo's own discipline (delete throwaway repros), it should not be committed.
findings.scenario: `npm run test` (the local quality gate that replaces CI) executes this stray file alongside the real S8 db tests; if it ever goes red for an unrelated reason it blocks the gate, and if green it pads the count with a non-invariant assertion.
findings.fix: Delete src/test/db/_s1repro.db.test.ts (or fold any genuine assertion it makes into the proper S1 db test). Out of S8 scope — flagged, not fixed.

findings.title: The /plan UI is read-only — none of the three promised dogfood actions (schedule, re-plan-around-rain, log bloom) are reachable; the Server Actions are dead code wired to no button
findings.location: page.tsx:19-30 + harvest-planner.tsx (whole) + actions.ts:76,105,133
findings.problem: DESIGN P2-S8 (line 285) requires /plan to be an interactive cockpit: '...and a re-plan around rain action when the forecast shifts.' The dogfood moment (line 293) says picking 'becomes a model the dispatch card runs on' — which requires the family to actually CREATE plans. But the entire rendered tree (PlanPage -> HarvestPlanner -> ReadinessList + PasadaTimeline) is pure read-only Server Components: zero 'use client', zero <button>, zero Dialog, zero onClick (grep across the whole slice returns nothing). The three Server Actions that do the writing — schedulePasada, replanPasada, recordMaturationSignal — are imported by NOTHING except their own test file (grep confirms: only src/app/(app)/plan/__tests__/actions.test.ts references them). So the 're-plan around rain' action, the schedule-a-pasada action, and the log-a-bloom action all exist in the data layer and are exercised only by unit tests; a real user on /plan has no way to invoke any of them. The build delivers a viewer, not the planner the spec describes.
findings.scenario: The family opens /plan in production. They see a readiness list and a (likely empty) pasada timeline. There is no 'Schedule pasada' button, no 're-plan around rain' control, no 'log bloom' form. They cannot create a single plan or fire a single task from the UI. The capability the slice is named for (a 'pasada scheduler' that 're-plans when rain is forecast') is unreachable.
findings.fix: Add a client island (e.g. PlanActions.client.tsx) that renders, per readiness row / per active pasada, the schedule + re-plan + log-bloom controls in the shared portal-fixed Dialog (src/components/ui/dialog.tsx), each calling the existing Server Actions. Wire 're-plan around rain' to replanPasada with a reason, schedule to schedulePasada, and a bloom/GDD/NDVI form to recordMaturationSignal. Add an interaction test (the slice's test-first rule) covering a schedule + a replan round-trip through the UI.

findings.title: No readiness-tinted map — the spec's headline dogfood visual ('the Phase-1 map tinted by predicted readiness') is missing; the planner ships a list instead of the map
findings.location: harvest-planner.tsx:40-92 (renders ReadinessList + PasadaTimeline only; no FarmMap import)
findings.problem: DESIGN P2-S8 (line 285) defines the /plan UI as 'a harvest-readiness map+calendar: the Phase-1 map tinted by predicted readiness (which plots to pick this week)...'. The dogfood moment (line 293) leads with 'the family opens /plan and sees their farm tinted by when each plot will be ready'. The slice's own world-class-UI line (design 342) names 'the readiness map (S8)' as the Apple-grade surface this slice must ship. The build ships NO map: HarvestPlanner renders only a ReadinessList (a tinted text list) and a PasadaTimeline. The reusable Phase-1 map (src/components/islands/FarmMap.client.tsx) already exists, already tints plot polygons by a per-feature property via a MapLibre 'match' paint expression (STATUS_FILL_EXPR), and the spec explicitly says to reuse it — but the planner never imports it and never feeds it a readiness tint. The signature dogfood visual ('their farm tinted by when each plot will be ready, the 1,400m plots ready next week, the 1,700m Geisha two weeks out') is absent.
findings.scenario: The family opens /plan expecting to see their actual farm map tinted green/amber where it's ready vs early. They get a vertical list of plot cards instead. The 'which plots to pick this week, at a glance on the map' moment — the whole point of the slice — never renders.
findings.fix: Reuse FarmMap.client.tsx in HarvestPlanner: build a readiness FeatureCollection (plot geometry from the existing geo port + each plot's readinessTone) and pass a readiness-based fill expression so polygons tint by tone (ready=forest, approaching=honey, early=sky), matching the existing ReadinessList palette. Add a render/smoke test that the map island mounts with the readiness tint. Keep the list as a secondary view, not the primary surface.

findings.title: GDD model is never fed: nothing computes growing-degree-days from the weather feed into plot_phenology, and the weather table has no dated daily temperature series to accumulate from
findings.location: actions.ts:21-25 (claims it 'consumes the cached weather table') + gdd.ts:58 accumulateGdd + supabase/migrations/20260620120000_init.sql:118 weather table
findings.problem: DESIGN P2-S8 (line 277, 291) says plot_phenology.gdd_accumulated is 'fed by the Phase-1 weather feed (GDD from Open-Meteo temps)' and lists the Open-Meteo weather feed as a HARD dependency. The actions.ts docstring (line 21-25) explicitly claims 'The GDD/readiness model consumes the cached weather table (the same feed the dashboard reads).' This is not true. (a) gddAccumulated is a caller-supplied parameter to recordMaturationSignal (actions.ts:124) — there is no code that reads the weather table, accumulates GDD, and writes it. (b) The pure accumulator gdd.ts:accumulateGdd / dailyGdd has ZERO non-test callers (grep confirms). (c) The weather table (init.sql:118) holds only 7 forecast rows keyed by a label 'day' ('Today','Mon','Tue' — seed.sql:152-157), not a dated historical hi/lo series, so even a future job has no real temperature history to accumulate bloom→today GDD from. The 'transparent GDD model calibrated by the family's bloom/harvest dates' (the de-risk plan, design line 295) therefore cannot run: gdd_accumulated stays at its default 0 forever, so readiness = clamp(0/2200) = 0 and the predicted ready date is the bloom date + (2200/50) days flat, ignoring actual accumulated heat.
findings.scenario: Even after the family logs a bloom date, gdd_accumulated never advances (no feeder exists), so every plot's readiness meter sits near 0% and the predicted date is a static bloom+44-days offset that never moves as the season warms or cools. The model the spec promised — readiness that climbs with accumulated heat — is inert. The actions.ts comment actively misrepresents this as working.
findings.fix: Either (1) build the feeder: a path that reads a dated daily-temp series, runs accumulateGdd, and calls record_maturation_signal with the real gdd_accumulated — which requires extending the weather table to a dated daily-temperature history (the current 7-label forecast is insufficient); or (2) if v1 is honestly bloom-date-only, correct the actions.ts docstring to stop claiming it consumes the weather feed, surface the limitation in the confidence note, and flag the GDD feeder as the explicit follow-up. Do not ship a comment that says the GDD model runs when no code accumulates GDD.

findings.title: Pure agronomy module re-implemented in SQL instead of shared — gdd.ts is dead code, breaking the 'one set of numbers' contract it was built to guarantee
findings.location: gdd.ts:5-8 (stated purpose) vs migration v_harvest_readiness:212-235 (re-implemented inline)
findings.problem: DESIGN P2-S8 (line 283) calls for 'A pure src/lib/agronomy/gdd.ts (GDD accumulation + bloom→cherry phenology, tested, no DB)', and the module's own header (gdd.ts:5-8) states its raison d'être: 'The whole module is pure functions so the v_harvest_readiness SQL view, the /plan UI, and S5 dispatch can all reason about the same numbers.' In practice the readiness math is implemented TWICE and shared by no one: the SQL view (migration lines 212-235) hand-codes the GDD spine, the NDVI nudge (centre 0.6, ±0.15), the stagger, and the predicted-ready-date formula inline; and gdd.ts implements the same thing in TS — but readinessScore / predictReadyDate / rankByReadiness / staggerOffsetDays / readinessConfidence have ZERO non-test callers (grep confirms). The UI reads the SQL-computed numbers off the view and never calls gdd.ts. So the two implementations are free to drift, and one concrete drift already exists: predictReadyDate (gdd.ts:87) divides by a caller-supplied gddPerDay, while the SQL (line 233) hardcodes a '/ 50.0' nominal accrual — different formulas for the same predicted date. The de-risk promise of 'one transparent model everyone reasons about' is unmet.
findings.scenario: A future change tweaks the NDVI nudge or the GDD-to-cherry constant in gdd.ts (the 'family-tunable calibration' the comments invite) and the unit tests pass — but the view, which is what the UI and S5 dispatch actually read, is unchanged, so the running app shows the old numbers. The calibration knob the spec promised is illusory: tuning the TS constants changes nothing a user sees.
findings.fix: Make the SQL view the single source and have gdd.ts either (a) be the codegen source for the view's expressions, or (b) be deleted in favor of the view if it truly cannot be shared server-side; OR feed the view's inputs through gdd.ts on the read path. At minimum, reconcile the predicted-ready-date formula (the /50 nominal in SQL vs gddPerDay in TS) and add a test asserting the two agree on a fixture, so the 'same numbers' contract is enforced rather than asserted in a comment.

findings.title: Field-facing planner is English-only — violates the slice's explicit bilingual requirement for the ~90% Ngäbe-Buglé/Spanish-speaking crew context
findings.location: readiness.ts:27-48 (English-only labels) + readiness-list.tsx + pasada-timeline.tsx + harvest-planner.tsx (all English)
findings.problem: DESIGN line 342 lists 'the readiness map (S8)' among the slices that each ship 'bilingual where field-facing'. /plan is the surface that feeds S5's morning dispatch card to the crew lead, and it carries picker-facing phrases. Every user-visible string is English-only: readinessLabel ('Ready to pick' / 'Approaching — days out' / 'Developing — weeks early', readiness.ts:27-36), confidenceLabel ('High confidence — bloom logged + corroborated', etc., readiness.ts:39-48), the PasadaTimeline 'peak ripe'/'ripe'/'early pass' chips (pasada-timeline.tsx:21-25) and 're-planned · {reason}', the empty states ('No plots to plan yet', 'Schedule a pasada from a ready plot...'), and the page header. No es:/en: pairs, no locale switch — grep finds no i18n anywhere in the slice. The reposo gate and processing surfaces in this codebase already carry bilingual treatment for exactly this crew, so the precedent exists and the spec demands it here.
findings.scenario: The pasada plan that drives the crew lead's morning dispatch is shown only in English. The lead (Spanish/Ngäbere-speaking) reading 'Approaching — days out' or 'peak ripe' on the plan that routes his crew has no Spanish rendering — the bilingual contract the spec set for field-facing surfaces is broken at the exact handoff into the dispatch card.
findings.fix: Add Spanish to the readiness/confidence/ripeness labels and the timeline/empty-state copy (es/en pairs or the codebase's locale mechanism), at minimum for the strings that flow into the dispatch handoff. Add a render test asserting the Spanish strings render under the es locale, per the slice's test-first rule.

findings.title: verify_chain only scans lot_event, so the attendance "Chain verified" badge verifies NOTHING and is vacuously always-green
findings.location: people.ts:379-387 (verifyAttendanceChain) calls verify_chain('attendance:<id>'); verify_chain is defined at 20260621092000_event_log_units_lot_graph.sql:297-330 and its loop is `for r in select * from lot_event e where e.stream_key = verify_chain.stream_key`. The S1 migration 20260622090000_people_system.sql adds NO attendance-aware verify_chain overload (grep confirms zero references).
findings.problem: The P2-S1 spec (DESIGN line 99) and the people.ts contract promise a CHAIN-VERIFIED badge sourced from verify_chain over the attendance ledger ('attendance:<id>' stream) — the same audit-drawer integrity affordance Phase-1 ships. But the only verify_chain on disk queries the Phase-1 `lot_event` table EXCLUSIVELY. The new append-only attendance hash chain lives in a SEPARATE table, `attendance_event`. So verify_chain('attendance:w-06') scans lot_event for stream_key='attendance:w-06', finds zero rows, the loop body never runs, and it returns the default `true`. The WorkerProfileSheet therefore renders a green 'Chain verified' badge for EVERY worker no matter what the attendance ledger contains — including when the ledger is empty or corrupted. The integrity feature the badge advertises does not exist for attendance; the badge is a false assurance. (worker_stream_event has the same problem — nothing verifies it either.)
findings.scenario: Open /crew, click any returning-partner card -> the profile sheet shows 'Chain verified' (forest badge). Tamper the attendance_event hashes via direct table write (the exact scenario the badge claims to detect) and re-open: still 'Chain verified', because verify_chain never looks at attendance_event. No test catches this: people.test.ts stubs the rpc boolean, and the DB test (p2s1_people.db.test.ts:399-422) checks prev_hash->hash by reading the table columns directly and NEVER calls verify_chain('attendance:...'), so the production path is untested.
findings.fix: Make verify_chain attendance-aware. Either (a) add an overloaded/branching verify_chain in the S1 migration that dispatches on the stream_key prefix ('attendance:' -> attendance_event, 'worker:' -> worker_stream_event, else lot_event) recomputing each table's hash with its own canonical-bytes call (attendance folds event_kind into the kind slot; see attendance_event_set_hash at people_system.sql:286-305), or (b) add a dedicated verify_attendance_chain(text) RPC and point people.ts at it. Then add a DB test that records two attendance events, calls verify_chain/verify_attendance_chain('attendance:w-06')=true, mutates a stored hash, and asserts it flips to false.

findings.title: Dogfood moment unreachable: seed has only ONE worker with a cert+por-obra and ZERO attendance events, so the rehire/profile experience is empty
findings.location: seed.sql:58-65 (the entire P2-S1 dignity seed block) and the absence of any record_attendance call (grep 'record_attendance' supabase/seed.sql returns nothing).
findings.problem: DESIGN line 108 defines the dogfood moment precisely: the family opens /crew and one-taps rehire 'the eight pickers who came back — each carrying their identity, languages, and still-valid pesticide cert.' The seed delivers almost none of it: only w-06 (Lucía) gets a pesticide cert + a por-obra contract; w-08 and w-13 get languages/comarca but NO cert and NO contract; the other 11 workers get nothing beyond the Spanish-default identity shell. Worse, ZERO attendance events are seeded (record_attendance is never called in seed.sql), so every WorkerProfileSheet renders 'No attendance recorded yet,' the roster attendance dots are driven only by the static workers.attendance snapshot, and worker_attendance_today is empty on a fresh boot. The promised 'eight returning pickers each with a still-valid cert' tableau does not exist — the dogfood demo shows one populated card and thirteen hollow ones.
findings.scenario: Run supabase db reset (migrations + seed), open /crew. The 'Returning partners' strip shows 14 cards but only w-06 has a cert badge and a non-empty profile sheet; opening any other worker's sheet shows empty attendance, no contracts, no certs. The narrative 'last season's Ngäbe-Buglé crew as named returning partners carrying still-valid certs' is not demonstrable.
findings.fix: Seed the dogfood narrative: pick ~8 picker workers, set comarca_origin='Ngäbe-Buglé'+languages={es,ngäbere}, give each a still-valid pesticide-handling cert via record_certification(...) and a por-obra picking contract via sign_por_obra_contract(...), and seed a few attendance events per worker via record_attendance(...) (clock-in/clock-out at now()-ish so worker_attendance_today and the timeline populate, and the chain badge has something to verify once finding #1 is fixed).

findings.title: Every worker defaults rehire_eligible=true, so non-pickers (supervisor, agronomist, driver, mill ops) all appear as "Returning partners" — diluting the dignity concept
findings.location: people_system.sql:88 `rehire_eligible boolean not null default true`; seed.sql sets it false for nobody (grep count = 0); page.tsx:55 `const eligible = roster.filter((m) => m.rehireEligible)` feeds CrewRehireStrip members.
findings.problem: The DESIGN frames rehire-eligibility and the 'Returning partners' strip as the season-start dignity moment for the returning Ngäbe-Buglé PICKING crew (lines 88-89, 108: 'last season's crew', 'the eight pickers who came back'). But because worker_identity.rehire_eligible defaults to true and the seed never narrows it, ALL 14 seeded workers — including Miguel Janson (Supervisor), Janette Janson (Agronomist), Raúl Santamaría (Driver), and the two Mill Operators — are surfaced as eligible returning partners with a one-tap Rehire button. The strip becomes 'rehire literally everyone' rather than 'welcome back the returning pickers,' undercutting the named-partner narrative and showing a Rehire CTA on the owners themselves.
findings.scenario: Open /crew -> 'Returning partners' header reads '14 eligible' and shows Rehire buttons on the supervisor and agronomist (the family). The intended scene (the returning field crew) is buried among staff who are not 'rehired' seasonally.
findings.fix: Either narrow the seed (set rehire_eligible=false for non-seasonal staff, or only flag the returning seasonal pickers as eligible), or scope the strip to seasonal/picker roles (e.g. the strip filters to role='Picker' and rehireEligible, or to workers whose latest membership ended). Lowest-risk: in seed.sql set rehire_eligible=false for the year-round staff (w-01,w-02,w-10,w-11,w-12) so only the returning field crew populates the dignity strip.

findings.title: Bilingual ngäbere field labels are admitted placeholders, leaving the bilingual-field requirement substantively unmet
findings.location: labels.ts:4-9 (the 'PLACEHOLDER TRANSLATIONS … MUST be reviewed by a native Ngäbe-Buglé speaker before this reaches the field … Do not ship to production copy as-is' banner) and the EVENT_KIND_LABELS/ATTENDANCE_LABELS/TERMS maps it covers (lines 24-44). Note also line 34 ng value ' neme sribire' has a leading space.
findings.problem: DESIGN line 99 requires 'Bilingual labels (es / ngäbere) on the field-facing surfaces,' and the dogfood/field rationale is that much of the ~90% Ngäbe-Buglé crew reads ngäbere, not Spanish, in the field. The build ships the bilingual AFFORDANCE (the 'es · ngäbere' rendering plumbs through), but the actual ngäbere strings are self-described unreviewed placeholders the file itself says must not ship to production copy. So the field-facing bilingual requirement is structurally present but substantively a stub: the crew sees machine/best-effort ngäbere that the code flags as unverified. This is the 'degraded/stub where the spec wants real' gap. (Minor data-quality tell: the clock-out ngäbere value has a stray leading space, ' neme sribire', which would render mis-aligned.)
findings.scenario: A returning Ngäbe-Buglé picker views their profile sheet; the attendance timeline shows 'salida ·  neme sribire' — copy the codebase explicitly marks as not-for-field and which carries a formatting artifact. The bilingual promise that justified the feature is not actually delivered to the user it targets.
findings.fix: Get the ngäbere terms reviewed by a native Ngäbe-Buglé speaker and replace the placeholders (the standing $0/no-paid-service constraint doesn't block a one-time human review), trim the leading space in EVENT_KIND_LABELS['clock-out'].ng, and drop the 'do not ship as-is' banner once verified. Until then the requirement should be flagged as not-met rather than presented as complete.

findings.title: apply_ferment_recipe uses a 1-second-resolution epoch as device_seq, so two recipe-binds in the same second collide on lot_event's UNIQUE(device_id, device_seq) and abort
findings.location: lines 213-218 (apply_ferment_recipe -> record_lot_event call); collides with lot_event UNIQUE(device_id,device_seq) defined at 20260621092000_event_log_units_lot_graph.sql:223
findings.problem: Unlike every other write door in the codebase, apply_ferment_recipe does NOT pass a client-minted device_seq into record_lot_event. It hardcodes `now(), 'server-ferment', extract(epoch from clock_timestamp())::bigint`. That epoch is whole-second resolution, and the device_id is the shared constant 'server-ferment'. record_lot_event inserts into lot_event with `on conflict (idempotency_key) do nothing` — that conflict clause ONLY covers the idempotency_key constraint, NOT the separate UNIQUE(device_id, device_seq). So two apply_ferment_recipe calls for DIFFERENT batches (different idempotency keys: `apply-recipe:<batch>:<recipe>`) that land in the same wall-clock second produce two lot_event inserts with the same ('server-ferment', <epoch-second>) tuple. The second violates lot_event_device_id_device_seq_key, raising 23505, and since record_lot_event's ON CONFLICT does not catch it, the WHOLE apply_ferment_recipe transaction aborts. This is the ONLY record_lot_event call site in the entire migration tree that uses an epoch device_seq (every other slice passes a client device_seq); it is an S3-specific defect. apply_ferment_recipe is granted EXECUTE to authenticated, so it is reachable via the REST API even though no UI wires it yet.
findings.scenario: REPRO (run in PGlite harness, CONFIRMED): seed lots JC-800/JC-801, start a recipe-less batch on each, then within one second call apply_ferment_recipe(batch1,'rec-geisha-anaerobic-v1') and apply_ferment_recipe(batch2,'rec-caturra-honey-v1'). Observed: the second call throws `duplicate key value violates unique constraint "lot_event_device_id_device_seq_key"`; only 1 of the 2 expected ferment_recipe_applied events is persisted and batch2's recipe bind is rolled back. A retry within the same second keeps colliding until the clock ticks over.
findings.fix: Stop hardcoding an epoch device_seq. Either (a) add p_device_id/p_device_seq/p_idempotency_key parameters to apply_ferment_recipe and pass the client-minted (server-ferment, randomDeviceSeq) values like start_ferment_batch does, or (b) at minimum mint a collision-free seq inside the RPC (e.g. nextval of a dedicated sequence, or `extract(epoch from clock_timestamp())*1e6` microseconds) AND make record_lot_event's insert tolerate the (device_id,device_seq) constraint (it currently only does ON CONFLICT (idempotency_key)). Add a regression test that fires two applies in the same second and asserts both events land.

findings.title: ferment_readings.value has no DB-layer range check, so a direct RPC call can persist an out-of-range pH that poisons the cut-point invariant (the signal that defines the cup)
findings.location: ferment_readings.value column def line 121 (no CHECK); record_ferment_reading lines 273-320 (no p_value/p_kind validation); v_ferment_cutpoint cut logic lines 404-405; client-only check at src/lib/db/commands/recordFermentReading.ts:70-73
findings.problem: The only pH range validation (0-14) lives in the TypeScript validator validateFermentReading (recordFermentReading.ts:70-73). The DB column ferment_readings.value is a bare `numeric not null` with NO CHECK, and the record_ferment_reading RPC does no value validation before insert. Per ADR-002 the SQL is supposed to be the real enforcement and the TS is just the friendly-error seam — but here there is NO SQL enforcement of the value at all. Any authenticated user calling record_ferment_reading directly over the REST/RPC API (bypassing the form/validator) can write reading_kind='ph' with value=-999 or value=14 (CONFIRMED accepted in PGlite). Because v_ferment_cutpoint fires cut_reached on `latest_ph <= target_ph`, a forged low pH falsely signals 'cut the ferment now' and a forged high pH suppresses a real cut signal. For this farm the cut-point IS the make-quality decision (the reposo/window that defines the Geisha cup), so a poisonable, un-range-checked pH is a real data-integrity hole on the slice's named invariant, not just cosmetic.
findings.scenario: Authenticated user (or a buggy/offline client) POSTs to the record_ferment_reading RPC with {p_kind:'ph', p_value:-999} or {p_kind:'ph', p_value:99}. The validator is never on this path (it only runs inside the Server Action). The row is accepted (no CHECK), appended immutably to the evidence ledger, and v_ferment_cutpoint now reports cut_reached based on a physically impossible pH — the cutpoint alert in the UI flips on/off on garbage.
findings.fix: Move the range invariants into the DB. Add a CHECK to ferment_readings, e.g. `check ((reading_kind='ph' and value >= 0 and value <= 14) or (reading_kind='temp' and value > -10 and value < 80) or (reading_kind='brix' and value >= 0 and value <= 50))` (tune bounds with the family), OR validate p_value per p_kind inside record_ferment_reading and raise check_violation. Keep the TS validator as the friendly seam but make SQL the enforcement, per ADR-002. Add a DB test asserting a -999 pH is rejected.

findings.title: Append-only evidence ledgers ferment_readings and mill_water_log are missing `force row level security`, deviating from the lot_event / people_system hardening pattern
findings.location: RLS block lines 434-442 (enable RLS + read policy only, no force); contrast lot_event force at 20260621092000_event_log_units_lot_graph.sql:516 and the 4 people_system ledgers at 20260622090000_people_system.sql:778-781
findings.problem: The migration header (lines 19-20, 132-133) frames ferment_readings/mill_water_log as append-only evidence ledgers ('A ferment curve is evidence'), exactly the role lot_event and the people_system event tables (worker_stream_event, attendance_event, por_obra_contracts, worker_certifications) play. Every one of those peers gets an explicit `alter table ... force row level security` so the table OWNER is also governed by policy. S3's two ledgers get `enable row level security` + a read policy but NO `force`. This is a defense-in-depth deviation from the established, documented posture. It is NOT exploitable today: the block triggers (lines 134-144, 164-174) stop owner UPDATE/DELETE regardless of RLS, and there is no INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE grant to authenticated, so the only writer is the SECURITY DEFINER RPC path (insert, which the triggers allow). But the inconsistency means a future policy/grant change here lacks the owner-level backstop its siblings have.
findings.scenario: No reachable exploit today. The risk is latent: if a later slice adds an owner-context maintenance routine or a write policy to these tables, lot_event/people_system are protected by force-RLS while these two are not, so the same change would silently behave differently on the ferment ledgers.
findings.fix: Add `alter table ferment_readings force row level security;` and `alter table mill_water_log force row level security;` (and optionally ferment_batches) to match lot_event and the people_system ledgers, for posture consistency and future-proofing.

findings.title: ferment_recipes immutability/append-only trigger blocks UPDATE only, not DELETE (lot_event blocks both)
findings.location: trigger def lines 77-79 `before update on ferment_recipes` (UPDATE only); contrast lot_event_block_mutation `before update or delete` at 20260621092000_event_log_units_lot_graph.sql:286-288
findings.problem: ferment_recipes is documented as an append-only versioned library whose target curve is immutable (a batch's recorded curve must stay forever comparable to the exact recipe version it ran against). The block trigger ferment_recipes_block_curve_edit only fires `before update`, so a DELETE of a recipe row is NOT blocked at the trigger layer (CONFIRMED in PGlite: deleting rec-pacamara-natural-v1 as owner succeeds). lot_event and the readings/water ledgers all use `before update or delete`. Deleting a recipe a batch was bound to would orphan that batch's target-curve reference (ferment_batches.recipe_id FK is `references ferment_recipes(id)` with no ON DELETE — so the FK would actually block deleting a referenced recipe, but an UNreferenced seeded recipe could be deleted, erasing a versioned asset and breaking the 'supersede, never remove' invariant). This is NOT reachable by authenticated: ferment_recipes has only a SELECT grant (line 450), no DELETE grant, so a REST caller cannot delete. It is an owner-path / defense-in-depth gap and an inconsistency with the immutable-ledger pattern.
findings.scenario: Owner-context only (e.g. a future seed/maintenance script, or an accidental admin DELETE) can remove an unreferenced recipe version, contradicting the append-only-versioned guarantee the slice advertises. Authenticated REST users cannot trigger it (no DELETE grant).
findings.fix: Change the trigger to `before update or delete on ferment_recipes` and have ferment_recipes_block_curve_edit raise on DELETE too (mirroring lot_event_block_mutation), so the append-only-versioned invariant is enforced at the data layer even on the owner path.

findings.title: por_obra supersede is window-blind: signing any contract silently nukes ALL open contracts for the worker+task, erasing the rate a worker agreed to (resolver returns nothing)
findings.location: sign_por_obra_contract, supersede UPDATE at lines 637-640
findings.problem: The supersede UPDATE is `update por_obra_contracts set superseded_by = new_id where worker_id = p_worker_id and task_kind = p_task_kind and id <> new_id and superseded_by is null`. It supersedes EVERY non-superseded contract for that worker+task with ZERO regard for effective_from/effective_to windows. The slice's stated invariant (migration header lines 213-216: "the rate a worker agreed to on a given day is forever auditable"; test file lines 10-13: por-obra "supersedes the prior open contract... v_active_por_obra resolves the effective rate on a date") is bypassable. Because v_active_por_obra (lines 456-477) filters `superseded_by is null`, a wrongly-superseded contract is dropped from the resolver entirely — payroll then cannot resolve the rate the worker actually signed. This is money/compliance-wrong. Two concrete corruptions confirmed via PGlite repro: (1) PARTIAL-WINDOW GAP — sign an open-ended June contract (rate 3.0, from 2026-06-01), then sign a July-only contract (rate 5.0, 2026-07-01..07-31); the June contract is superseded, and v_active_por_obra('w-06','picking','2026-06-15') now returns [] (the June rate the picker agreed to is unresolvable). (2) FUTURE-CONTRACT WIPE — pre-sign next season's rate (5.0, 2027-01-01..2027-12-31, a real farm practice), then sign this season's rate (3.5, 2026); the 2027 contract gets superseded_by=2 even though its window does not overlap 2026 at all, and v_active_por_obra(...,'2027-06-15') returns [] — the pre-agreed 2027 rate is gone.
findings.scenario: On a tablet, the supervisor signs Eduardo's picking rate for the upcoming 2027 season in advance (effective 2027-01-01..2027-12-31). Later the same week he records the current 2026 season rate (effective 2026-06-01..2026-12-31). The 2027 row is silently flagged superseded_by the 2026 row despite non-overlapping windows. When 2027 payroll runs, v_active_por_obra('w-06','picking','2027-06-15') returns no row — the rate Eduardo signed and agreed to has vanished from the only resolver payroll consults. The por_obra_contracts row still physically exists (append-only), but it is invisible to every read path. Equivalent gap occurs any time two windows for the same task don't fully overlap.
findings.fix: Scope the supersede to the OVERLAPPING window only, not the whole worker+task. Replace the WHERE with one that intersects effective ranges: `... and superseded_by is null and effective_from <= coalesce(p_effective_to, 'infinity'::date) and coalesce(effective_to, 'infinity'::date) >= p_effective_from`. Better still, only supersede the single prior contract whose window contains/overlaps the new one's effective_from (the rate it is actually replacing), and add a DB test asserting that a non-overlapping future contract is NOT superseded and remains resolvable. Add a uniqueness/exclusion constraint (e.g. an `tstzrange`/`daterange` GIST EXCLUDE on (worker_id, task_kind, range) WHERE superseded_by IS NULL) so two open contracts can never silently overlap in the first place.

findings.title: Attendance/worker hash-chain forks across devices: head selection orders by per-device device_seq, so a second device on the same worker stream branches the chain (and there is NO verify function to detect it)
findings.location: attendance_event_set_hash (lines 286-309) and worker_stream_event_set_hash (lines 259-282); head SELECT orders `by e.device_seq desc`
