findings.problem: The append-only ledgers are hash-chained per stream_key, but the trigger picks the chain head with `order by e.device_seq desc limit 1`. device_seq is a PER-DEVICE Lamport counter (the migration's own comment lines 163-176 and the unique(device_id,device_seq) key confirm it is per-device, not global), while a single attendance stream `attendance:<worker_id>` is explicitly designed to receive events from MULTIPLE offline devices (record_attendance accepts client-minted device_id/device_seq for the S0 outbox — comment lines 492-494). When device B's seq (e.g. 1) is lower than device A's seq (e.g. 5) on the same worker, B's event chains off A's higher-seq event, and a later A event (seq 6) chains off the SAME head — the chain forks. PGlite repro confirmed: after record_attendance for ('devA',5), ('devB',1), ('devA',6) on w-06, both the devB(seq1) event and the devA(seq6) event carry the identical prev_hash (devA seq5's hash) — a fork, not a linear chain. The tamper-evidence invariant (each event provably extends a single predecessor) is broken: an event can be re-pointed/recomputed against either branch, and a deletion of the orphaned branch leaves a still-internally-consistent chain. Worse, verify_chain (the Phase-1 detector, 20260621092000_event_log_units_lot_graph.sql:297) reads ONLY lot_event — there is NO verify function over attendance_event or worker_stream_event at all (grep confirms zero `verify` in the people migration), so this corruption is undetectable even by the audit tool. (Phase-1 lot streams avoid this because each JC-NNN stream is minted/written server-side; attendance multi-device-per-stream is the new, reachable exposure.)
findings.scenario: Two field foremen clock the same picker on two tablets during the S0 offline rollout (the migration's stated near-future). Tablet A is on device_seq 5, tablet B is on device_seq 1. B's clock-out chains off A's seq-5 hash; A's next event also chains off seq-5. The worker's attendance ledger — the labor-law presence proof and payroll input — is now a forked DAG, not an append-only chain, and no verify_chain covers it, so the fork is invisible. Tamper-evidence (the whole point of the hash chain) is defeated.
findings.fix: Select the chain head by global per-stream insertion order, not per-device seq: `order by recorded_at desc, device_seq desc` is still wrong across devices; use a monotonic per-stream sequence or `order by` a server-assigned per-stream position. Simplest robust fix: add a per-stream `seq` (server-minted via an advisory-lock or a per-stream nextval) and chain on that, OR fold device_id into the head ordering with a stream-level total order. AND add a verify_chain_worker(stream_key) / verify_chain_attendance(stream_key) function mirroring verify_chain so the worker ledgers are actually auditable, plus a DB test that two devices on one worker stream produce a single linear chain (no shared prev_hash).

findings.title: Backdated enroll/rehire crashes on the crew_memberships CHECK (left_at >= joined_at) — offline-replayed events with occurred_at before the backfill timestamp are un-recordable
findings.location: enroll_crew_member close-UPDATE line 567-569 and rehire_worker close-UPDATE line 731-732; CHECK at line 67 (`check (left_at is null or left_at >= joined_at)`)
findings.problem: enroll_crew_member and rehire_worker close the active membership with `set left_at = p_occurred_at`. The backfilled membership rows get `joined_at default now()` (the migration/seed run time). If a command arrives with `p_occurred_at` earlier than that joined_at — trivially true for any backdated or offline-replayed event — the CHECK `left_at >= joined_at` fails and the whole RPC raises, so the enrollment/rehire CANNOT be recorded at all. PGlite repro confirmed: `enroll_crew_member('w-06','crew-norte','2020-01-01T00:00:00Z','devA',1,'en-back')` raises `new row for relation "crew_memberships" violates check constraint "crew_memberships_check"`. This is reachable today: record_attendance, enroll, and rehire all take an arbitrary client p_occurred_at (validated only as a parseable date — recordAttendance.ts/enrollCrewMember.ts accept any ISO date including past ones), and the migration header itself frames these as offline-replayable. A legitimate backdated correction (worker actually moved crews last week, entered today) is rejected by a CHECK that conflates the event's wall-clock with the membership's bookkeeping interval.
findings.scenario: A returning Ngäbe-Buglé picker started in Crew Norte on Monday but the office only enrolls him in the system on Friday, entering the real Monday date as occurred_at. Because the backfill stamped his prior membership's joined_at = system-bootstrap time (this Friday or later), `left_at = Monday < joined_at = Friday` violates the CHECK and the enroll RPC throws — the supervisor cannot record the true move date and is forced to lie about when it happened, corrupting the very crew-history ledger the slice exists to keep honest. Same failure mode for rehire_worker (line 731 closes with p_occurred_at and has no guard).
findings.fix: Don't tie left_at to the raw client occurred_at against a now()-stamped joined_at. Either: (a) close with `left_at = greatest(p_occurred_at, joined_at)` so a backdated close never undershoots its own join; (b) record the move time in the worker_stream_event payload (the audit truth) and keep crew_memberships.left_at as a monotonic bookkeeping value; or (c) relax the membership model so the interval CHECK reflects business intent. Add a DB test that a backdated enroll/rehire (occurred_at before the backfill time) succeeds and records the intended move date.

findings.title: apply_ferment_recipe collides on lot_event(device_id, device_seq) when two recipe-binds land in the same wall-clock second
findings.location: apply_ferment_recipe, lines 213-218 (device_seq = extract(epoch from clock_timestamp())::bigint)
findings.problem: Unlike every other write RPC in the slice (start/reading/water carry a client-minted device_seq) and unlike the Phase-1 RPCs (which pass the caller's p_device_seq through), apply_ferment_recipe HARDCODES device_id='server-ferment' and device_seq = whole-second epoch (extract(epoch from clock_timestamp())::bigint). lot_event has a GLOBAL `unique (device_id, device_seq)` constraint (migration 20260621092000 line 223). So two recipe binds under the constant 'server-ferment' device that happen in the same second produce the SAME (device_id, device_seq) pair and the second record_lot_event INSERT throws `duplicate key value violates unique constraint "lot_event_device_id_device_seq_key"`. The whole RPC transaction aborts: the batch.recipe_id UPDATE is rolled back too, so the bind silently fails with a raw Postgres 23505 surfaced to the family. This is a data-layer correctness defect — the device_seq is supposed to be a per-event causal counter, and second-granularity epoch is not unique per event.
findings.scenario: PROVEN with a PGlite repro against the real migrations: seed two lots + two batches, then `select apply_ferment_recipe(bA,'rec-z'); select apply_ferment_recipe(bB,'rec-z2');` back-to-back. Result: APPLY-COLLISION-ERROR = 'duplicate key value violates unique constraint "lot_event_device_id_device_seq_key"'; only 1 of 2 ferment_recipe_applied events landed; the second bind erred. A concurrent agent's repro (zzz_s3_repro.db.test.ts REPRO1) reproduces the identical failure. Reaching this in batch: any two recipe re-binds within one second collide, and even a single bind collides with another ferment-surface lot_event that happened to draw the same epoch second is not the risk (readings use random 48-bit) — the real, near-certain collision is apply-vs-apply same-second.
findings.fix: Stop hardcoding device_seq from epoch seconds. Either (a) take a client-minted p_device_seq + p_idempotency_key argument like the other three S3 RPCs (and like record_cherry_intake / advance_processing_stage do), passing them straight through to record_lot_event; or (b) derive a guaranteed-unique seq from a dedicated server sequence (e.g. nextval of a per-surface sequence) rather than clock_timestamp seconds. Add a regression DB test that calls apply_ferment_recipe twice in the same statement batch and asserts BOTH ferment_recipe_applied events land.

findings.title: apply_ferment_recipe has no TS command / server action / UI caller — 'No recipe (apply later)' is a dead promise
findings.location: start-ferment-form.tsx line 108 (`<option value="">No recipe (apply later)</option>`); apply_ferment_recipe RPC at migration line 187 has zero TS callers
findings.problem: The recipe field is optional and the StartFermentForm explicitly offers 'No recipe (apply later)'. start_ferment_batch happily inserts a batch with recipe_id = NULL. But there is NO apply path: grep across src/ for apply_ferment_recipe / applyFermentRecipe / bind-recipe / choose-recipe finds only test files — no src/lib/db/commands/applyFermentRecipe.ts, no server action in ferment/actions.ts, no UI on the batch detail page (src/app/(app)/ferment/[batch]/page.tsx). So a batch started without a recipe can NEVER be bound to one through the app. Downstream this breaks the cut-point: v_ferment_cutpoint LEFT JOINs ferment_recipes on b.recipe_id, so for a recipe-less batch target_ph is NULL and cut_reached is permanently false (migration lines 404-405) — the cup-defining cut signal is silently dead for exactly the batches the UI invites you to create.
findings.scenario: Family taps Start ferment, leaves Recipe = 'No recipe (apply later)', logs pH readings all day. The cutpoint card never fires (target_ph NULL), and there is no button anywhere to apply the recipe afterward — they would have to start an entirely new batch (with the same lot, risking a second open batch) or run raw SQL. The advertised 'apply later' workflow does not exist.
findings.fix: Either remove the 'apply later' option and make recipe required at start (smallest fix), OR wire apply_ferment_recipe end-to-end: add src/lib/db/commands/applyFermentRecipe.ts (validator + command), an applyFermentRecipeAction server action, and a recipe-picker on the batch detail page for recipe-less batches. Note the apply RPC must first be fixed per the CRIT device_seq finding before it is safe to expose. Add a test asserting a recipe-less batch can later bind a recipe and its cutpoint then projects.

findings.title: start_ferment_batch creates a duplicate batch with NO ferment_started event when the idempotency_key already exists under another kind
findings.location: start_ferment_batch, lines 242-266 (replay short-circuit filters `kind = 'ferment_started'`; then record_lot_event ON CONFLICT (idempotency_key) DO NOTHING at migration 20260621092000 line 364)
findings.problem: start_ferment_batch's exactly-once guard only matches when `idempotency_key = p_idempotency_key AND kind = 'ferment_started'` (line 244-245). If that idempotency_key already exists on a lot_event of a DIFFERENT kind, the guard misses, the batch INSERT proceeds (a NEW batch row is created), and then record_lot_event hits its `ON CONFLICT (idempotency_key) DO NOTHING` and SILENTLY drops the ferment_started event. Net result: a ferment_batches row exists with NO corresponding lot_event — a direct violation of the ADR-002 invariant the migration header states ('every write mutates the domain row AND appends a lot_event in ONE transaction'). The batch is now invisible to the hash-chained ledger / audit / EUDR trace.
findings.scenario: PROVEN (PGlite, _probe_s3 PROBE 3 / independently re-run): pre-write any lot_event with key 'shared-key-3' under kind 'some_other_kind', then call start_ferment_batch(..., 'shared-key-3'). Result: a NEW Anaerobic batch row is created (count went 1->2) while `ferment_started events with that key = 0`. The batch exists ledger-less. Reachability today is low via the UI (each form mints a fresh random UUID), but the data-layer invariant is genuinely bypassable, and the same pattern is repeated for stage/intake — an offline outbox that re-uses or shares keys across event kinds (a real P2-S0 scenario the slice claims to support) would trigger it.
findings.fix: Make the replay short-circuit kind-agnostic: check `where idempotency_key = p_idempotency_key` (any kind) and fail closed if it exists under a non-ferment_started kind, OR drop the kind filter and let record_lot_event be the single source of idempotency truth (only INSERT the batch after confirming the event was actually appended, i.e. check record_lot_event's returned uid vs the pre-existing row). Add a DB test: pre-seed a lot_event with a key under a foreign kind, call start_ferment_batch with the same key, assert NO duplicate batch is created.

findings.title: NULL idempotency_key silently bypasses exactly-once on readings / water / events (multiple NULLs allowed by the UNIQUE index)
findings.location: ferment_readings.idempotency_key (line 126 `text unique`), record_ferment_reading replay guard (lines 292-295); same shape for mill_water_log (lines 159, 341-344) and lot_event
findings.problem: idempotency_key is `text unique` (nullable). Postgres treats NULLs as distinct in a UNIQUE index, so unlimited rows with idempotency_key = NULL are allowed. record_ferment_reading's replay guard is `select id ... where idempotency_key = p_idempotency_key` — with p_idempotency_key NULL this `= NULL` predicate is never true, so the guard does nothing AND the ON CONFLICT does nothing, so every NULL-key call inserts a fresh row. The slice's stated 'exactly-once is structural' guarantee is hollow for a NULL key — the data layer does not enforce it; only the TS validator does.
findings.scenario: PROVEN (PGlite, PROBE 1): two record_ferment_reading calls with NULL idempotency_key -> 2 reading rows (not 1). Not reachable through the shipped UI (validateFermentReading rejects empty key and withEnvelope always defaults to crypto.randomUUID()), so this is a defense-in-depth gap, not a live exploit — but the RPC is the documented 'real enforcement' write door and it does not actually enforce exactly-once for the one input value it should reject.
findings.fix: Make idempotency_key NOT NULL on ferment_readings / mill_water_log / lot_event (the offline outbox always has a key), OR add an explicit `if p_idempotency_key is null then raise exception ... end if;` at the top of each command RPC so the write door fails closed rather than silently duplicating. Add a DB test asserting a NULL-key reading is rejected (or that two NULL-key calls do not both persist).

findings.title: record_ferment_reading accepts out-of-range / negative values at the SQL layer (no CHECK on value)
findings.location: ferment_readings.value (line 121 `numeric not null` — no CHECK); record_ferment_reading (lines 273-320 — no value validation)
findings.problem: ferment_readings.value has no CHECK and record_ferment_reading does no sign/range validation. The migration header claims 'the SQL CHECK/FK/append-only triggers inside the RPC are the *real* enforcement', and the TS command's own comment says the SQL CHECK is the real guard — but there IS no CHECK on value. The TS validator only bounds pH to 0-14 (recordFermentReading.ts lines 70-72) and places NO bound at all on temp or brix, and even the pH bound is purely client-side. So a negative pH, a 9000-degree temp, or a negative Brix all persist as immutable, append-only 'evidence' that can never be corrected (the readings are UPDATE/DELETE-blocked), poisoning the cut-point projection and the cup record permanently.
findings.scenario: PROVEN (PGlite, zzz_s3_repro REPRO 3 re-run): record_ferment_reading(batch,'ph',-999,...) is ACCEPTED at the DB layer (no DB check). Reachable for temp/brix even through the UI since the TS validator imposes no bound on those kinds; a mis-typed glove-tap of '-21' for temp or '-4' for brix lands a permanent bad reading.
findings.fix: Add table CHECKs: pH in [0,14], temp_c in a sane range (e.g. -5..60), brix >= 0 — best implemented as a single CHECK keyed on reading_kind, or validate inside record_ferment_reading and raise with a clean errcode. Mirror the bounds in the TS validator for temp/brix too. Add DB tests asserting negative pH and negative brix are rejected by the RPC, not just the UI.

findings.title: apply_ferment_recipe rebind (A->B->A) flips recipe_id with no new ledger event — silent, untraceable curve re-binding
findings.location: apply_ferment_recipe, lines 206-218 (synthetic key 'apply-recipe:'||batch||':'||recipe; UPDATE has no idempotency guard of its own)
findings.problem: apply_ferment_recipe derives the lot_event idempotency_key per (batch, recipe) pair, not per call. The no-op short-circuit (line 207) only fires if the batch is ALREADY bound to that exact recipe. Rebinding A->B->A: the final A bind passes the no-op check (currently bound to B, not A), runs the `update ferment_batches set recipe_id='A'` (which has no idempotency guard — it just executes), then calls record_lot_event with key 'apply-recipe:batch:A' which was already used on the first A bind, so ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING drops it. The batch's recipe_id silently flips back to A with NO new ferment_recipe_applied event in the ledger. The 'live curve forever comparable to the exact target it was cut to' guarantee is broken: the recorded binding diverges from the ledger's last recipe-applied event.
findings.scenario: Operator binds batch to anaerobic, realizes wrong, rebinds to washed, then rebinds back to anaerobic. The ledger shows applied->anaerobic, applied->washed, and the third (back to anaerobic) is dropped — so a chain replay sees the last recipe-applied event as 'washed' while ferment_batches.recipe_id reads 'anaerobic'. Cut-point and any audit now disagree with the immutable ledger.
findings.fix: Key the apply event per CALL (carry a client idempotency_key like the other RPCs), or always append an event on a real recipe change (the UPDATE happened, so the ledger must record it) — only short-circuit when the recipe_id genuinely did not change. Add a test: bind A, bind B, bind A again; assert three ferment_recipe_applied events exist and the last one's recipe matches the current recipe_id.

findings.title: ferment_recipes block trigger is UPDATE-only — a recipe row can be DELETEd, contradicting the append-only/immutable invariant
findings.location: ferment_recipes_block_curve_edit trigger, lines 77-79 (`before update on ferment_recipes`) vs ferment_readings_block_mutation lines 142-144 (`before update or delete`)
findings.problem: The migration header (lines 32-36) states a recipe is 'NEVER edited in place' and the target curve 'is IMMUTABLE once the row exists', and the sibling append-only ledgers (ferment_readings line 143, mill_water_log line 173) block both UPDATE and DELETE. But ferment_recipes' trigger fires only `before update` — DELETE is not blocked. So an owner-context DELETE of a recipe row succeeds, removing a target curve that batches were cut against. ferment_batches.recipe_id is a nullable FK with default NO ACTION, so a recipe referenced by an existing batch is protected by the FK, but an UNreferenced recipe (e.g. a just-superseded version with no live batch) can be deleted, breaking the 'append-only versioned, never deleted' promise and the supersede chain.
findings.scenario: PROVEN (PGlite, zzz_s3_repro REPRO 4 re-run): `delete from ferment_recipes where id='rec-pacamara-natural-v1';` succeeds with no trigger block. Reachability via the REST/authenticated path is mitigated (no DELETE grant, no delete RLS policy), so this is primarily a belt-and-braces inconsistency vs the readings/water tables — but the migration explicitly relied on a block trigger as the immutability guard for recipes and that guard does not cover DELETE.
findings.fix: Change the recipe trigger (or add a second one) to `before update or delete`, raising on DELETE the same way ferment_readings_block_mutation does, so the append-only guarantee is enforced symmetrically with the sibling ledgers. Add a DB test asserting a DELETE on ferment_recipes raises.

findings.title: Leftover throwaway probe/repro DB test files committed in the test tree (cross-agent worktree contamination)
findings.location: src/test/db/zzz_s3_repro.db.test.ts, src/test/db/_efficacy_probe.db.test.ts, src/test/db/_s1repro.db.test.ts (and a now-removed _probe_s3.db.test.ts)
findings.problem: Multiple throwaway probe/repro test files (console.log-driven, expect(true).toBe(true) or no assertions) are sitting in src/test/db/ — clearly artifacts left by concurrent red-team agents in this shared worktree. They run as part of `npx vitest run`, pollute test output with debug logging, and (zzz_s3_repro) duplicate real findings as non-failing probes. FLAG ONLY (out of my slice's authoring scope and not safe to delete from under a possibly-active peer session per the one-worktree rule): I removed only my own throwaway (_zz_repro_devseq.db.test.ts); these belong to other agents.
findings.scenario: Run the suite and observe stray REPRO/PROBE console output and assertion-free 'passing' tests. The S3-relevant ones (zzz_s3_repro) independently corroborate findings #1, #5, and #7 above but as silent probes, not regression tests.
findings.fix: After the red-team fan-out completes, the orchestrator should delete all _probe_*/_*repro*/zzz_*_repro test files (or promote their assertions into real failing-then-passing regression tests alongside the fixes for findings #1, #3, #4, #5, #6, #7). Do not delete mid-run if a peer agent may still own them.

findings.title: Shared idempotency_key namespace lets one worker-stream RPC return another's event as bogus success (rehire/enroll/sign/cert)
findings.location: worker_stream_event.idempotency_key UNIQUE (line 148); enroll_crew_member replay (lines 555-558); sign_por_obra_contract replay (lines 618-623); record_certification replay (lines 671-675); rehire_worker replay (lines 714-717). Caller surface: src/app/(app)/crew/actions.ts:61-63 (idempotencyKeyOrNew reads str(raw,'idempotencyKey') from the form).
findings.problem: enroll_crew_member, sign_por_obra_contract, record_certification and rehire_worker ALL anchor exactly-once on the SAME single global namespace `worker_stream_event.idempotency_key`. Each RPC's replay guard is `select 1 from worker_stream_event where idempotency_key = p_idempotency_key` with NO `kind`/stream-type discriminator. The `idempotencyKey` is a user-controllable form field (actions.ts `str(raw,'idempotencyKey')` wins when present, so an authenticated caller chooses the value). When a key is reused ACROSS command types the second RPC short-circuits on the first command's event: (a) rehire_worker reusing an enroll key returns the ENROLL's event_uid and the action reports "Worker rehired." while NO new membership is opened, NO WORKER_REHIRED event is written, and the rehire-eligibility gate (lines 719-725) is never evaluated; (b) sign_por_obra_contract / record_certification reusing an enroll/rehire key read `payload->>'contract_id'`/`'cert_id'` which are absent on those payloads, so they return NULL — no contract/cert is ever written. This silently drops a por-obra rate a worker may have physically agreed to, and lets a barred worker be 'rehired' with the eligibility check skipped.
findings.scenario: Proved in PGlite against the real migration: enroll w-06 with key 'kx' (returns uuid U). rehire_worker('w-06',...,'kx') returns U (== the enroll uuid), crew_memberships count is UNCHANGED (no rehire happened), but the command/action reports success. Separately, sign_por_obra_contract('w-06',...,'shared-key') after enroll with 'shared-key' writes ZERO rows into por_obra_contracts and returns NULL. Both repros ran green and were deleted.
findings.fix: Make the exactly-once key per-command-type. Either (a) namespace the key inside each RPC (e.g. dedupe on the pair (kind, idempotency_key), or prefix the stored key with the command kind: 'ENROLL:'||key, 'REHIRE:'||key, ...), or (b) give each RPC its own idempotency column/table, or (c) in each replay branch additionally assert the found event's `kind` matches the expected kind and raise (not silently return) on mismatch. Also harden the action layer: never trust a client-supplied idempotencyKey verbatim — mint server-side per command, or scope it. Add a regression test that reuses one key across two different command RPCs and asserts the second still performs its own write (or raises), never returns the first's id.

findings.title: Attendance 'Chain verified' badge always green — verify_chain reads lot_event, but attendance events live in attendance_event
findings.location: verifyAttendanceChain → rpc('verify_chain', {stream_key:'attendance:<id>'}) at src/lib/db/people.ts:379-387; verify_chain body reads ONLY lot_event at supabase/migrations/20260621092000_event_log_units_lot_graph.sql:309-312; badge rendered at src/components/sections/crew/worker-profile-sheet.tsx:146-151; wired in src/app/(app)/crew/page.tsx:62.
findings.problem: verify_chain(stream_key) recomputes the hash chain from the `lot_event` table exclusively. The S1 attendance ledger is a SEPARATE table (`attendance_event`) with its own `attendance_event_set_hash` chain; the migration added NO verify overload for the worker/attendance ledgers. verifyAttendanceChain passes stream_key 'attendance:<id>', which never appears in lot_event, so verify_chain iterates zero rows and returns `true` vacuously. The worker-profile sheet then renders a green 'Chain verified' ShieldCheck badge as a tamper-evidence verdict on the attendance ledger — but it verified NOTHING. Tampering with attendance_event (the payroll + labor-law presence proof) would still show 'Chain verified'. (The append-only block trigger + force-RLS are the real guards, but the UI is asserting a verification that does not occur.)
findings.scenario: Proved in PGlite: recorded two real attendance events for w-06 into attendance_event, then `select verify_chain('attendance:w-06')` returns TRUE while `count(*) from lot_event where stream_key='attendance:w-06'` is 0 and `count(*) from attendance_event` is 2. The badge is green over an entirely unexamined ledger. Repro ran green and was deleted.
findings.fix: Add a verify function that recomputes over the worker ledgers (e.g. verify_worker_chain(stream_key) reading attendance_event / worker_stream_event with their canonical bytes), grant execute to authenticated, and point verifyAttendanceChain at it. Until then, do not render a 'Chain verified' badge sourced from the lot_event verifier for attendance. Add a regression test that tampers an attendance hash (with the block trigger disabled, as lot_event's test does) and asserts the attendance verifier returns false — the current verify_chain cannot fail for attendance.

findings.title: rehire_eligible is a fail-open gate: no caller-facing path can ever set it false (and identity dignity fields are uncapturable post-seed)
findings.location: worker_identity.rehire_eligible default true (line 88); the ONLY writes to worker_identity are the backfill shell insert (line 133) and seed.sql lines 59-62; grants = `grant select on worker_identity to authenticated` ONLY (line 790) — no insert/update grant, no update RLS policy; the eligibility check that depends on it is rehire_worker lines 719-725.
findings.problem: rehire_worker correctly fails closed on `rehire_eligible=false` (lines 723-724), but NOTHING in the app can ever set that flag to false. worker_identity has only a SELECT grant for authenticated, no INSERT/UPDATE grant, no write RLS policy, and there is NO command RPC that mutates worker_identity (grep confirms: only the backfill insert and seed.sql touch it). Every backfilled/seed worker defaults rehire_eligible=true. So the safety/dignity gate that is supposed to bar a specific returning worker from being rehired (e.g. a worker dismissed for cause, or barred for a safety reason) is unreachable through the product — only a DBA running raw SQL can arm it. The migration's own comment (lines 77-78) claims worker_identity is 'written only via the enroll/rehire RPCs', but neither RPC writes it. As a corollary, the entire point of the slice — capturing the Ngäbe-Buglé dignity fields (preferred_name, comarca_origin, languages, emergency_contact, id_doc_*) — has no command/UI write path either; they can only be set in seed.sql.
findings.scenario: An authenticated farm admin wants to mark a worker non-rehire-eligible after a serious safety incident. There is no action, no RPC, and no table grant to do so; the only way is direct SQL on the DB. Meanwhile the one-tap rehire on the crew page will happily rehire that worker because the flag is still its default true. Confirmed by enumerating every write to worker_identity in supabase/ and src/lib/db/ (only backfill + seed).
findings.fix: Add a SECURITY DEFINER command RPC (e.g. set_worker_identity / set_rehire_eligible) with pinned search_path, revoke-public + grant-authenticated, that appends a worker_stream_event AND updates the worker_identity row (keep the table write-grant-free; mutate only inside the definer RPC, consistent with the slice's 'RPC is the only write door'). Wire a minimal action/UI so eligibility and the dignity fields can actually be captured. Add a test that flips eligibility to false via the RPC and then asserts rehire_worker raises 'not rehire-eligible'.

findings.title: AD-8 grant-hygiene miss: _resync_worker_crew / _resync_worker_attendance keep default PUBLIC EXECUTE (reachable by anon)
findings.location: _resync_worker_crew defined at line 378, _resync_worker_attendance at line 395; the revoke block (lines 801-815) lists every OTHER function but omits these two; the migration's own stated invariant is at lines 799-800 ('Slam PUBLIC EXECUTE shut on every function ... Trigger/helper fns get nothing').
findings.problem: Postgres grants EXECUTE to PUBLIC by default on every new function. The migration revokes public execute on all 13 other functions it creates (including the other underscore helper _backfill_people and all trigger fns) but FORGETS _resync_worker_crew(text) and _resync_worker_attendance(text). Both are language plpgsql, NOT security definer (they run as the caller) and they `update workers set crew/attendance ... where id = p_worker_id`. They therefore remain callable by PUBLIC, i.e. by both anon and authenticated REST roles — directly contradicting the migration's own AD-8 statement and the 'internal helpers get NO grant' convention. This is not a privilege escalation (the inner workers UPDATE is still RLS/grant-bound: anon has no workers update grant so it no-ops/denies inside, and authenticated can already update workers directly), but it is a dead/leaky guard: an internal helper the design says must be unreachable is reachable, and an authenticated user can invoke it to forcibly resync/overwrite workers.attendance and workers.crew out of band of the command RPCs.
findings.scenario: Proved in PGlite: under `set role anon`, `select _resync_worker_crew('w-06')` reaches the function body and only then fails with 'permission denied for table crew_memberships' (NOT 'permission denied for function'), proving EXECUTE was granted; under `set role authenticated` the same call succeeds. By contrast a properly-revoked fn (next_server_seq) denies anon EXECUTE outright. Repro ran and was deleted.
findings.fix: Add `revoke execute on function _resync_worker_crew(text) from public;` and `revoke execute on function _resync_worker_attendance(text) from public;` to the revoke block (grant them to NO REST role — they are owner/trigger-internal helpers invoked only from the definer RPCs). Extend the AD-8 grant-posture test (src/test/db/p2s1_people.db.test.ts) to assert anon/authenticated CANNOT execute any underscore-prefixed helper — the current test only checks read grants and the public-facing RPC execute, so it never caught this.

findings.title: Cut-point alert is a dashboard chip, never fires a board task — the spec's load-bearing "closed-loop, not a dashboard" requirement is unmet
findings.location: v_ferment_cutpoint view (lines 391-414) + record_ferment_reading RPC (lines 273-320); cutpoint surfaced ONLY in src/components/sections/ferment/cutpoint-alert.tsx + ferment-tracker.tsx (line 82)
findings.problem: The P2-S3 spec is explicit and repeated that the cut-point alert must 'fire a task onto the existing `tasks` board (`category` extended or a `ferment-cut` kind) BEFORE the window closes — closed-loop, not a dashboard' (DESIGN line 143), and the dogfood moment is '...fires a "cut now" alert to the board' (DESIGN line 158). The build ships NONE of this. The cut signal exists only as `v_ferment_cutpoint.cut_reached` and is rendered solely as a glass chip (`CutpointAlert`) inside the per-batch cockpit page `/ferment/[batch]`. No RPC inserts into `tasks`; `task_category` is never extended with a ferment-cut value (it stays the phase-1 enum 'Pruning'..'Soil'); `grep` for any `tasks` reference in the ferment migration and ferment UI returns NONE. The family only sees 'cut now' if they are already staring at that exact batch's page — which is precisely the dashboard the spec says NOT to build. The closed loop (the system pushing the alert to where the crew works) does not exist. The proven in-repo pattern is sitting in the SAME migration batch: the S8 sibling slice (20260622100000_harvest_planning.sql lines 49, 380-392) does `alter type task_category add value 'Harvest'` and `insert into tasks (...)` from inside its command RPC to fire onto the real /tasks board. S3 simply omits the equivalent.
findings.scenario: Mill operator taps an hourly pH reading from the field. The reading crosses the recipe target. The DB sets `cut_reached=true` but nothing reaches the crew: no /tasks board entry, no assignee, no due. The operator (who is in the wet mill, not refreshing /ferment/[batch] on a phone) never sees the alert and misses the cut on the cup-defining ferment — the exact failure the slice was built to prevent. No test asserts a board task fires (confirmed: no test references it), so this is a real absent capability, not merely untested.
findings.fix: Mirror the proven S8 pattern inside this slice: (1) `alter type task_category add value if not exists 'Ferment Cut'` (additive, in the schema lane); (2) in `record_ferment_reading`, after appending the reading, evaluate the cut condition (recipe bound AND latest pH <= target_ph) and, when newly crossed, `insert into tasks (id, title, category, plot_id, worker_id, due, status, priority)` resolving a worker the way `_resolve_pasada_worker` does — fail-closed and idempotent so a replay does not double-fire (e.g. only fire on the transition, or key off an idempotency anchor like the readings ledger does); (3) write the test-first DB test asserting a board task is fired exactly once when the cut is reached, mirroring src/test/db/s_p2s8_harvest_planning.db.test.ts:179. Surface the same cut state on the /ferment board (ferment-board.tsx) so it is visible without drilling into a batch.

findings.title: The proactive "40 minutes BEFORE the window closes" projection (curve-fit.ts projectCutPoint) is built and tested but wired into nothing — only a reactive at/past-target signal ships
findings.location: projectCutPoint / CutPointProjection.projectedHours (lines 49-85); the only importer is its own test src/lib/ui/__tests__/curve-fit.test.ts (confirmed by grep). Reactive-only signal lives in v_ferment_cutpoint cut_reached (migration lines 404-405) and cutpoint-alert.tsx.
findings.problem: The spec's dogfood moment and headline value is the PROACTIVE alert: '...40 minutes before the window closes, fires a "cut now" alert to the board — the family hits the cut-point on the cup-defining ferment instead of guessing' (DESIGN line 158). `curve-fit.ts` was written exactly for this: `projectedHours` extrapolates the recent pH slope to PROJECT the future crossing time (the 'when WILL it close' number) — the one piece of code that delivers a before-it-closes signal. But it is dead: grep confirms `projectCutPoint`/`projectedHours` is imported only by its own test and never reaches any UI component or DB path. The shipped signal (`v_ferment_cutpoint.cut_reached`) is purely REACTIVE — it only goes true once `latest_ph <= target_ph`, i.e. the window is ALREADY closing/closed. So the live system can only say 'cut NOW' at the moment the target is hit, never 'cut in ~40 min', which is the lead time that lets the family actually get to the tank in time. The de-risk note (DESIGN line 160) permits shipping v1 as a simple threshold crossing for the *cut decision*, but the dogfood moment that defines 'done' for this slice is the lead-time projection, and the projection code exists — it just isn't connected.
findings.scenario: A ferment is trending toward its 4.2 target. The operator opens /ferment/[batch]. The cockpit shows 'Tracking — pH 4.5, target 4.2' with no estimate of when the cut will be needed, because `projectedHours` is never computed in the render path. The family gets no advance warning; by the time the chip flips to 'Cut now' the window is already closing and someone has to be physically present at that instant — exactly the 'guessing' the slice promised to end. The asset (projection) is paid for in code + tests but delivers zero user value.
findings.fix: Wire the existing pure function: in ferment-tracker.tsx (a Server Component), build the pH `FermentPhPoint[]` from `curve` and call `projectCutPoint(points, cutpoint.targetPh)`, then pass `projectedHours` into `CutpointAlert` so the tracking state can render 'projected cut ~Xh (~N min)'. For the board-task half (finding #1), have the cut-firing path use the projection's lead time so the task fires ahead of the close, not at it. Add a render test asserting the projected time surfaces in the tracking state. This connects already-tested code; no new model is needed.

findings.title: Worker-profile Dialog has no max-height / internal scroll — a multi-season attendance ledger overflows the viewport and the close button + lower content become unreachable
findings.location: dialog.tsx:110-142 (overlay `fixed inset-0 grid place-items-center p-4` + panel `max-w-md ... p-6` with NO max-h/overflow); crew-rehire-strip.tsx:158-176 renders WorkerProfileSheet into it; worker-profile-sheet.tsx:163-181 maps the FULL `attendance[]`
findings.problem: The crew rehire strip is the FIRST and ONLY Dialog caller that renders an UNBOUNDED, append-only ledger into the modal. WorkerProfileSheet maps the entire attendance timeline (worker-profile-sheet.tsx:163 `attendance.map(...)` — every clock-in/out/rest-day/absence ever recorded, newest-first), plus full por-obra history and cert list. The shared Dialog panel has no `max-h` and no `overflow-y-auto`, and the overlay container is `fixed inset-0 grid place-items-center` with no scroll either; meanwhile the body is scroll-locked (`document.body.style.overflow='hidden'`, dialog.tsx:41). When the panel exceeds viewport height it overflows symmetrically above and below the fold with NO way to scroll — the top (title + both close buttons) and the bottom (cert ledger) become physically unreachable. The profile sheet's own doc-comment (worker-profile-sheet.tsx:30) says it 'reuses the audit-drawer idiom,' but the audit drawer DID add `min-h-0 flex-1 overflow-y-auto` (audit-drawer.tsx:144) and this path did not.
findings.scenario: On a phone, open the profile sheet for a returning Ngäbe-Buglé partner who has clocked in/out across one or more seasons (dozens-to-hundreds of attendance events — exactly Janson's repeat crew). The modal grows past the 667px viewport; the user cannot scroll to the cert badges at the bottom, and the X / Close buttons at the top are pushed off-screen, leaving only Escape to dismiss — a trap for touch users with no keyboard.
findings.fix: Cap the panel and make it scroll: on the Dialog panel add `max-h-[85svh] overflow-y-auto` (or split into a non-scrolling header + a `min-h-0 flex-1 overflow-y-auto` body like audit-drawer.tsx), and/or constrain the timeline inside WorkerProfileSheet with a `max-h-64 overflow-y-auto` on the attendance `<ol>`. Add a render test that mounts the sheet with 50+ attendance events and asserts a scroll container exists / the close control stays in the DOM viewport box.

findings.title: Ngäbere language chip on the roster board fails WCAG-AA contrast (4.11:1) — the one chip that matters most for a ~90% Ngäbe-Buglé crew
findings.location: crew-roster-board.tsx:131-138 — `bg-sky-100 ... text-[11px] ... text-sky` on the `es · ngäbere` chip
findings.problem: The ngäbere bilingual chip renders `text-sky` (#3b6ea5) on `bg-sky-100` (#d8e4f0) at `text-[11px]`. Measured contrast is 4.11:1 — BELOW the WCAG-AA 4.5:1 threshold for normal text (11px is far under the 18.66px/14pt-bold large-text cutoff). The component doc-comment explicitly claims 'WCAG-AA on glass' (crew-roster-board.tsx:26), so this is a broken promise. It is also INCONSISTENT with the sibling chip in the rehire strip (crew-rehire-strip.tsx:128) which uses `text-muted-fg` on `bg-muted` = 5.14:1 (passes) — the roster-board variant is the failing one. Every other badge/chip on the card (comarca 8.67:1, cert 12.37:1, present/rest-day 7.7/4.84:1) passes; only the sky chip fails.
findings.scenario: On Janson's crew (~90% Ngäbe-Buglé, many ngäbere-first speakers), most roster cards display this exact chip. A field user in bright Volcán daylight on a phone cannot reliably read the `es · ngäbere` label — degrading the single affordance built for this crew's dignity.
findings.fix: Darken the foreground to a sky-700-class token, e.g. add `--color-sky-700` ≈ #2a527d and use `text-sky-700` (≈ 6.3:1 on sky-100), mirroring the honey-700 pattern already in the palette for honey text. Or reuse the rehire-strip's `text-muted-fg` treatment for consistency. Add a contrast assertion / token usage test.

findings.title: Crew loading skeleton's summary strip is `grid-cols-3` while the real CrewSummary is `grid-cols-1 sm:grid-cols-3` — a mobile layout shift the skeleton was supposed to prevent
findings.location: loading.tsx:30 `glass-card grid grid-cols-3 ...` vs the real component crew-summary.tsx:44 `grid grid-cols-1 ... sm:grid-cols-3`
findings.problem: The route skeleton hardcodes a 3-column summary grid at ALL breakpoints (`grid-cols-3`), but the real CrewSummary stacks to a SINGLE column on mobile (`grid-cols-1 sm:grid-cols-3`). So on a phone the skeleton paints three cramped side-by-side tiles, then the data lands and reflows into one vertical stack — a visible content layout shift (CLS). loading.tsx's own doc-comment (lines 6-8) and line 41 explicitly promise 'the layout never shifts under the user', which this contradicts. (The roster-board skeleton below it is correctly matched: `grid-cols-1 sm:grid-cols-2 xl:grid-cols-3`.)
findings.scenario: Load /crew on a phone with a slow connection (offline-first farm, weak Volcán signal). The summary strip flashes as 3 squeezed columns during the skeleton, then jumps to a 1-column stack when the server component resolves — a jarring reflow on the most-used field surface.
findings.fix: Change loading.tsx:30 to `grid grid-cols-1 sm:grid-cols-3` (and match the real card's divider/`gap` treatment) so the skeleton occupies the same box as the resolved CrewSummary at every breakpoint. Extend the existing loading test to assert the responsive grid classes match the real component.

findings.title: Append-only pasada guard (supersede-only UPDATE trigger) is unpinned — removing it leaves the whole S8 db suite green
findings.location: describe 'replan_pasada is append-only/superseded' lines 253-294 (vs migration trigger pasada_schedule_supersede_only at supabase/migrations/20260622100000_harvest_planning.sql:161-162 + guard 140-158)
findings.problem: The slice's KEY append-only invariant for pasada_schedule is enforced by pasada_schedule_guard via the BEFORE UPDATE trigger 'pasada_schedule_supersede_only' — it rejects every UPDATE except the exact supersede stamp (status -> 'superseded' with same plot/pasada/date). NO test exercises a direct UPDATE rejection. The replan_pasada describe only asserts that the owner-run supersede path WORKS (2 rows exist, 1 active, new date) and that v_pasada_calendar shows only the active plan. It never asserts that an attempt to re-date or un-supersede an active plan in place is BLOCKED. The maturation_signal ledger DOES have an UPDATE-rejection test (line 99-103), so this is a glaring asymmetry: the pasada ledger's equivalent guard is untested. I mutation-tested it: deleting the entire `create trigger pasada_schedule_supersede_only ...` registration (so a caller could now silently rewrite harvest-plan history in place — the exact invariant violation the slice forbids) leaves all 15 tests PASSING. The guard could regress to a no-op and CI stays green.
findings.scenario: Mutation: comment out the `create trigger pasada_schedule_supersede_only before update on pasada_schedule ...` line in the migration. Run `npx vitest run --project db src/test/db/s_p2s8_harvest_planning.db.test.ts` -> 15 passed. The append-only/superseded chain — the slice invariant guaranteeing the morning's plan is never edited away — is provably unguarded by the suite. (Verified: with the trigger present, a direct `update pasada_schedule set predicted_ready_date='2026-05-01' where idempotency_key='k1'` correctly raises 'pasada_schedule is append-only'; with it removed, the update silently succeeds and no test notices.)
findings.fix: Add a DB test in the replan_pasada describe that, after the initial schedule_pasada, issues a raw `update pasada_schedule set predicted_ready_date = <other> where plot_id=... and pasada_number=... and status <> 'superseded'` and asserts it `rejects.toThrow(/append-only|supersede|not permitted/i)`. Also assert a raw `delete from pasada_schedule where ...` is rejected (DELETE guard, also untested). Mirror the existing maturation_signal append-only test (line 99-103). These pin both arms of pasada_schedule_guard so removing either trigger turns the suite red.

findings.title: Worker-resolution correctness (_resolve_pasada_worker tiers) is unpinned — fired task only asserted `toBeTruthy`, wrong-worker mutant survives
findings.location: line 220 `expect(task[0].worker_id).toBeTruthy()` (vs _resolve_pasada_worker at migration lines 267-287)
findings.problem: The task-firing invariant ('inserts a valid tasks row, worker_id NOT NULL honored') is supposedly the point of the schedule_pasada test, and _resolve_pasada_worker makes a SPECIFIC documented promise: plot's most-recent picker (tier 1) -> else a Supervisor (tier 2) -> else any worker (tier 3). The only assertion on the resolved worker is `toBeTruthy()`. That pins nothing about which worker — it would pass for ANY worker, the wrong picker, or a picker from a different plot. Mutation test 1: I changed tier-1 from `where h.plot_id = p_plot_id` to `where h.plot_id is not null` (resolve ANY plot's picker, ignoring the requested plot) — 15/15 tests PASS. The fixture seeds w-06 as p-cuesta-piedra's picker; the test never asserts the fired task went to w-06 specifically. Mutation test 2: removing the Supervisor + any-worker fallback arms entirely (tiers 2-3) does crash the replan_pasada beforeAll (p-bambito has a signal but no harvest, so it relies on the supervisor fallback) — but as an UNASSERTED setup side-effect, not a behavior assertion; the any-worker tier-3 (no picker AND no supervisor) is exercised by NOTHING.
findings.scenario: Run the suite against the tier-1 mutant (`where h.plot_id is not null`): all 15 green despite the assignee now being resolved from the wrong plot. A real regression where harvest passes get assigned to a picker who has never worked that plot would ship undetected.
findings.fix: Strengthen the schedule_pasada test to assert `task[0].worker_id === 'w-06'` (the seeded plot picker, tier 1). Add a positive test for tier 2: a plot with a phenology signal but no harvest fires a task assigned to the Supervisor (w-01). Add a tier-3 test: a plot+world with NO picker and NO supervisor still fires a task with a non-null worker_id (proving the NOT-NULL invariant holds at the last-resort tier). These convert the truthy check into real per-tier behavior pins.

findings.title: gdd.ts is NOT NaN-safe (slice invariant claims it is) and has ZERO NaN test coverage — predictReadyDate THROWS on NaN, readinessScore returns NaN
findings.location: whole file — no NaN case (vs gdd.ts readinessScore:113-125 clamp at 42-43, predictReadyDate:78-91 line 90 `new Date(readyMs).toISOString()`)
findings.problem: The S8 invariant explicitly requires 'GDD/altitude-stagger math correct + NaN-safe', yet gdd.test.ts contains no NaN input anywhere and the code is not NaN-safe. Verified by probe: (1) readinessScore({gddAccumulated: NaN,...}) returns NaN — `clamp` uses Math.min/Math.max, which propagate NaN, so a NaN GDD yields a NaN readiness that flows straight into readinessTone/the meter. (2) readinessScore with ndviLatest:NaN also returns NaN. (3) predictReadyDate('2026-01-01', 50, NaN, 2200) THROWS `RangeError: Invalid time value` at gdd.ts:90 (`new Date(NaN).toISOString()`), crashing instead of returning the honest `null` the function's contract promises for un-substantiable inputs. The module's own docstring sells it as 'exhaustively unit-tested so the model is exhaustively unit-testable at $0' and 'degrades cleanly' — but the degradation/garbage-input path is the one thing untested.
findings.scenario: A malformed numeric arriving from the read port (mapPlotReadiness does `Number(r.altitude_masl)` / `numOrNull(Number(r.ndvi_latest))`; a non-numeric or undefined value coerces to NaN) feeds NaN into the pure model. readinessScore returns NaN -> the readiness meter and rank break silently; or a NaN altitude into predictReadyDate throws and 500s the planner. No test would have caught the regression because the suite never feeds a NaN.
findings.fix: Add NaN-guard tests AND the guards they demand (TDD red first): assert `readinessScore` with NaN gddAccumulated/ndvi returns a finite value in [0,1] (add `Number.isFinite` guards or `Number.isNaN(x) ? 0 : x` before clamp); assert `predictReadyDate` with a NaN/non-finite altitude or gddPerDay returns `null` (guard `!Number.isFinite(altitudeMasl)` / extend the `gddPerDay <= 0` check to `!Number.isFinite(gddPerDay) || gddPerDay <= 0`, and bail before `new Date(NaN)`). This is the highest-value missing test on the pure-logic side — it pins the stated NaN-safe invariant the suite currently leaves entirely open.

findings.title: getHarvestReadiness 'most-ready-first' ordering contract is asserted-as-table-name-only — flipping to ascending (worst plots to dispatch) survives the suite
findings.location: lines 90-185 (getters describe); the `order` spy is created at line 100 but never asserted; vs planning.ts:107-114 `.order("readiness", { ascending: false })`
findings.problem: The planning.test.ts header claims it pins 'the getter fetch/ORDER contract', and the docstring on getHarvestReadiness says it returns plots 'most-ready first — the input S5 dispatch reads to draft the morning card'. The mock builder defines `order: vi.fn(() => builder)` but NO test ever asserts what `.order` was called with. Only `from` (the view name) is checked. Mutation test: changing planning.ts to `.order("readiness", { ascending: true })` (so the LEAST-ready plots sort to the top of the dispatch card) leaves all 6 planning.test.ts tests PASSING. The ranking direction — the entire point of 'most-ready-first' and the contract S5's morning dispatch consumes — is pinned by no test in the repo (the DB readiness test at line 137 supplies its own explicit `order by readiness desc` in the test SQL, so it validates the VIEW's score values, not the getter's ordering).
findings.scenario: A refactor or fat-finger flips the order direction (or drops the `.order` for a default sort); the morning dispatch card silently surfaces the greenest, weeks-early plots first instead of the ready-to-pick ones. Every test stays green; the bug ships.
findings.fix: Assert the order contract the comment claims: `expect(builder.order).toHaveBeenCalledWith('readiness', { ascending: false })` in the getHarvestReadiness test, and `expect(builder.order).toHaveBeenNthCalledWith(1, 'predicted_ready_date', { ascending: true })` (+ the altitude tiebreak) in getPasadaCalendar. The `order` spy already exists on the builder; it just needs to be checked. Without this the getter mock 'mocks away the very contract it claims to verify'.

findings.title: Migration self-idempotency (enum ADD VALUE) and re-apply safety untested — no double-replay test catches a non-idempotent migration
findings.location: whole file (freshDb() replays each migration exactly once); vs migration line 49 `alter type task_category add value if not exists 'Harvest'`
findings.problem: The slice lists 'the Harvest enum add is idempotent' as an explicit invariant. Nothing tests it. The PGlite harness (freshDb) replays each migration ONCE in filename order; no S8 test re-applies the slice migration or re-runs `alter type task_category add value ...`. The `if not exists` clause IS what makes it idempotent (verified by probe: re-running the ADD VALUE succeeds), but a future edit dropping `if not exists` (e.g. switching to a plain `add value 'Harvest'`, which errors on second apply) would pass every test, then fail only when the migration is re-applied against a DB that already has the value — exactly the prod-replay scenario the invariant exists to prevent. Similarly, the maturation_signal DELETE guard (the migration registers a no_delete trigger at lines 102-103) is never exercised — only the UPDATE arm is tested (line 99-103) and only the pasada DELETE/UPDATE arms are missing too.
findings.scenario: Drop `if not exists` from the enum line, or convert the migration to a form that errors on re-apply; the suite stays green because it only ever applies the migration once on a virgin DB. The non-idempotency surfaces only in a real re-run/replay.
findings.fix: Add a focused idempotency test: after freshDb(), explicitly `alter type task_category add value if not exists 'Harvest'` a second time and assert it does not throw (and that the enum has exactly one 'Harvest' member via pg_enum). Optionally add a `delete from maturation_signal` rejection test to complete the ledger-immutability coverage. This is the lowest-cost pin for the stated enum-idempotency invariant, which currently has none.

findings.title: The whole drying slice ships zero write UI — record-moisture, assign-station, and advance-to-mill have no reachable surface
findings.location: page.tsx:31-50 (whole route); src/lib/db/commands/recordMoisture.ts; src/lib/db/commands/assignStation.ts
findings.problem: The /drying route renders only two read-only server components (DryingBoard + StationOccupancyBoard). There is NO interactive write surface anywhere in the slice: no actions.ts (compare src/app/(app)/processing/actions.ts), no 'use client' island, no shared Dialog, no form. `grep -rn 'recordMoisture|assignStation' src/app src/components` returns nothing — the two fully-built command validators/ports (recordMoisture.ts, assignStation.ts) are dead code with no caller. So in the running app there is no way to record a moisture reading, assign a lot to a drying station, or advance a lot to mill. The page's own docstring promises 'every lot resting before the mill shows its moisture curve... the advance-to-mill button is disabled... until then' — but the moisture curve can never be fed and the advance can never fire, because the only door (the SECURITY DEFINER RPCs) is never opened from the UI. Sibling slices ship their action surfaces (processing/advance-stage-control.tsx, harvests/cherry-intake-form.tsx, costing/cost-entry-form.tsx); drying ships none.
findings.scenario: Family member opens /drying after measuring a bed with a moisture meter. They want to log 11.2% for lot JC-571 and then push it to the mill. There is no 'Record reading' button, no 'Assign station' control, and the green 'Advance to mill' button does nothing (next finding). The reposo gate is observable but the entire workflow it gates is unreachable — the feature is a read-only dashboard, not the 'first-class protected process control' the slice claims.
findings.fix: Add src/app/(app)/drying/actions.ts with recordMoistureAction / assignStationAction / advanceToMillAction server actions (mirroring processing/actions.ts: useActionState-driven, stable hidden idempotency key per open form, friendly-error passthrough). Add 'use client' islands that open the shared portal-fixed Dialog (src/components/ui/dialog.tsx) for 'Record reading' (per lot card) and 'Assign station' (per lot/station), and wire the advance button to advanceToMillAction. Ship each with a test-first action test like processing/__tests__/actions.test.ts.

findings.title: Enabled 'Advance to mill' button is a dead control — full CTA styling, no handler
findings.location: drying-board.tsx:86-103 (button); component is a server component, drying-board.tsx:1 has no 'use client'
findings.problem: When `lot.reposo.ready` is true the button renders enabled, with a prominent primary CTA look (`bg-forest text-paper hover:bg-forest-600`), label 'Advance to mill', and a title 'Advance this lot to milling'. But DryingBoard is a pure server component with no 'use client', no onClick, no formAction, and no enclosing <form> anywhere up the tree ((app)/layout.tsx has none). Pressing it with mouse OR keyboard (it is a focusable type=button) does absolutely nothing. The disabled state is honest courtesy; the ENABLED state is a lie — it looks fully actionable and is inert.
findings.scenario: A rest-stable lot shows the green gate chip and an inviting green 'Advance to mill' button. The operator clicks it (or tabs to it and hits Enter) expecting the lot to move to milling. Nothing happens — no dialog, no navigation, no feedback. They click repeatedly, assume the app is broken, and fall back to a paper log. A disabled-looking affordance that does nothing is bad; an enabled, hover-animated primary button that does nothing is worse — it actively misleads.
findings.fix: Either (a) wire the button to a real advanceToMillAction (open the shared Dialog confirm flow with a stable idempotency key, per the AdvanceStageControl pattern), or (b) until the action exists, do not render an enabled primary button — render a link to the lot's processing/advance surface or keep it visibly non-interactive. Never present an enabled primary CTA with no behavior.

findings.title: ReposoGateChip red status rides cherry tokens that fail WCAG AA contrast (4.12:1 < 4.5)
findings.location: reposo-gate-chip.tsx:50-51 ('bg-cherry-100 text-cherry'); chip text at :46 is text-xs font-semibold (12px)
findings.problem: The blocked/'Resting' chip is the primary status surface this slice introduces and it hard-codes `bg-cherry-100 text-cherry` (not routed through Badge). cherry #b5482e on cherry-100 #f6ddd4 measures 4.12:1. The chip text is text-xs (12px) semibold, which is NOT 'large text' under WCAG (large = 18px, or 14px/18.66px bold), so the 4.5:1 normal-text floor applies and this FAILS. The CLAUDE.md/global bar explicitly requires WCAG-AA on the real background. The green 'Rest-stable' variant (forest-600 on forest-100) is fine at 7.74:1; only the red/blocked state — the one shown most often during reposo — is under floor. The same sub-floor cherry pair is reused by the 'over capacity'/'Cover' red badges in station-occupancy-board.tsx.
findings.scenario: Outdoors in bright Volcán daylight on a phone, a crew member reads the most common state ('Resting · 4 days · 11.8% · blocked') in muddy-red-on-pink at 4.12:1. The lock icon is aria-hidden so the text carries the meaning, and that text is below the contrast floor — hard to read in glare, exactly the field condition this app targets.
findings.fix: Darken the red status text token used for chip labels (e.g. introduce a cherry-700 around #8f3522 reaching >=4.5:1 on cherry-100) and use it in reposo-gate-chip.tsx for the blocked state and in the cherry Badge tone. Add a contrast assertion/snapshot so the floor can't regress. (cherry-100/text-cherry is a Phase-1 token; flag for owner since fixing it touches the shared Badge.)

findings.title: Weather 'Cover' badge renders a redundant double leading marker (dot + cloud icon)
findings.location: station-occupancy-board.tsx:78-86 (risk badge uses `tone="cherry" dot` AND a CloudRain icon child); Badge renders its own dot at badge.tsx:49-51
findings.problem: The cover-risk badge is `<Badge tone="cherry" dot><CloudRain/> Cover {day}</Badge>`. Badge prints a leading status dot when `dot` is set, THEN the children — so the rendered chip is: [• dot][cloud icon][Cover Wed], two leading markers stacked. The sibling 'Clear' badge (line 82-86) deliberately omits `dot` and shows just [sun icon][Clear]. The result is visually inconsistent between the two states and the dot is meaningless noise next to the cloud glyph.
findings.scenario: On the stations board a patio with rain incoming shows '• ☁ Cover Wed' while a clear bed shows '☀ Clear' — the rain chip has an extra orphan dot the clear chip doesn't, drawing the eye to a marker that conveys nothing.
findings.fix: Drop `dot` from the cover-risk Badge (the CloudRain icon already is the status marker), matching the 'Clear' badge: `<Badge tone="cherry"><CloudRain.../> Cover {risk.day}</Badge>`.

findings.title: QC-HOLD commerce gate is BEFORE INSERT only — an UPDATE to a held lot's existing claim row bypasses the hold (diverges from the prevent_oversell INSERT-OR-UPDATE precedent it claims to extend)
findings.location: lines 217-223 (create trigger lot_reservations_prevent_held_commit / lot_shipments_prevent_held_commit — both `before insert` only); the fn _prevent_held_lot_commit at lines 199-216
findings.problem: The migration header states THE TEETH `extend the Phase-1 prevent_oversell trigger family`, but prevent_oversell fires `before insert OR update on lot_reservations/lot_shipments` (green_inventory.sql:135,139 — it even has dedicated tg_op='UPDATE' self-exclusion logic, proving UPDATE is a real guarded path), whereas _prevent_held_lot_commit registers `before insert` ONLY (qc_cupping.sql:218,222). So a held lot's ALREADY-EXISTING reservation/shipment row can be mutated (e.g. bump kg) while the QC-HOLD is open, and the hold guard never fires. The invariant `a QC-HOLD lot CANNOT be reserved or shipped` is asymmetric vs the oversell guard it parallels. Reachability today: the claim tables grant only INSERT to authenticated (green_inventory.sql:305-306) and there is NO UPDATE-driving RPC, so a normal authenticated REST caller is blocked by the grant posture (confirmed: PROBE F => `permission denied for table lot_reservations`). It IS reachable by the single-owner full-CRUD path (the app's stated live posture) — PROBE B (owner role) => `UPDATE-ALLOWED-guard-did-NOT-fire`: with an open hold on JC-9001, `update lot_reservations set kg=20` succeeded with no qc-hold rejection. So this is a defense-in-depth / consistency gap (the data-layer invariant is enforced one statement-class narrower than its sibling), not a client-reachable CRIT.
findings.scenario: Owner (or any future code path that gains UPDATE on a claim table) places a QC-HOLD on a flawed Geisha micro-lot, then edits an existing reservation row's kg or buyer while the hold is open; the held-commit guard does not run, so the held lot's commercial commitment is silently changed despite the quarantine. Refuted as CRIT because no authenticated-role UPDATE grant and no UPDATE RPC exist today.
findings.fix: Mirror the prevent_oversell registration exactly: change both triggers to `before insert or update on lot_reservations` / `... on lot_shipments`. The fn already reads `new.green_lot_code` which is valid for UPDATE too, so no body change is needed. Add a DB test that places a hold then attempts an UPDATE of an existing claim row (as the owner) and asserts it is rejected — the current s6_qc_cupping.db.test.ts only covers the INSERT path (lines 102-117).

findings.title: v_qc_status.latest_cup_score returns 0 for a lot whose newest cupping session has no scores yet — masking the real prior score and mislabeling a Best-of-Panama Geisha as 'Below Specialty'
findings.location: v_qc_status latest_cup_score subquery, lines 296-301 (`select f.final_score from v_cup_final_score f join cupping_sessions s2 on s2.id = f.session_id where f.green_lot_code = g.lot_code order by s2.occurred_at desc limit 1`)
findings.problem: v_cup_final_score is a LEFT JOIN of sessions to scores with `coalesce(sum(c.score),0)` (lines 247-264), so a session that has been OPENED but has no score rows yet appears with final_score = 0. v_qc_status.latest_cup_score picks the `order by occurred_at desc limit 1` session's final_score, so the moment a cupper opens a new session for a lot (a normal first step before entering attribute scores), the roll-up's latest score flips to 0 — even though an earlier, fully-scored session of the same lot exists. CONFIRMED with PROBE I: lot with an earlier scored session (total 17) plus a later empty session => `v_qc_status.latest_cup_score = 0`. The qc.ts mapper (mapQcStatus, line 38-40) faithfully forwards 0 (it only nulls a literal SQL NULL, never a 0), and cupQualityBand(0) (cva-scoring.ts) returns 'Below Specialty'. So the QC table on /qc and the cup-to-cause panel show the farm's flagship Geisha as a 0 / Below Specialty the instant someone starts a new cupping — directly contradicting the slice's own `never a fabricated baseline` promise (qc.ts:33, cva-scoring.ts docstring).
findings.scenario: A cupper finishes scoring lot JC-9001 (say 88, Specialty), then a week later opens a fresh session to re-cup it and steps away before entering the first attribute. The /qc dashboard and the lot's QC banner now display 0 / 'Below Specialty' for that lot, misrepresenting an export-grade lot to anyone reading the board — and feeding a wrong signal into any downstream sell/no-sell judgment.
findings.fix: Exclude empty sessions from the latest-score pick: either filter `where f.attribute_count > 0` in the latest_cup_score subquery, or change v_cup_final_score to emit NULL (not 0) for a session with no scores (e.g. `case when count(c.id)=0 then null else sum(c.score) end`) so the roll-up + the mapper's existing null-handling render 'no score yet' instead of 0. Add a DB test: a scored session then a newer empty session must leave latest_cup_score at the real scored total (or NULL), never 0.

findings.title: QC-HOLD release/place have no actor identity in the audit trail and release clears ALL open holds in one shot — placed_by/released_by capture only a device id
findings.location: place_qc_hold lines 425-442 (placed_by := p_device_id, qc_cupping.sql:436) and release_qc_hold lines 453-471 (`set released_by = p_device_id ... where green_lot_code = ... and released_at is null` — no per-hold targeting, no auth.uid())
findings.problem: The qc_holds ledger is positioned as the auditable quarantine of record (the column comment calls placed_by `device/actor that placed it`), but both RPCs only ever store the device id (p_device_id) into placed_by/released_by — never the authenticated user (auth.uid()/auth.jwt() is available inside these SECURITY DEFINER fns but unused). For a ~90% Ngabe-Bugle migrant crew sharing field devices, `placed_by='srv'`/a shared tablet id cannot attribute WHO quarantined or released a lot. Separately, release_qc_hold stamps EVERY open hold on the lot (released_at is null) in a single UPDATE with no way to release one specific hold — so if two independent holds (e.g. 'off-flavor' + 'moisture') are open, releasing 'addresses one concern' actually re-opens commerce by clearing both (PROBE E confirmed both open holds drop to 0 after one release call). Not a security hole given the authenticated-only single-owner posture this phase ships, but it weakens the compliance/audit story the slice advertises.
findings.scenario: A quality lead places a hold for off-flavor; separately the warehouse places a moisture hold on the same lot. A worker releases what they think is just the moisture hold via release_qc_hold; both holds clear and the off-flavor lot becomes sellable. The ledger records released_by as a shared device id, so no one can tell who released the off-flavor quarantine.
findings.fix: Stamp the actor: set placed_by/released_by from auth.uid() (or a passed-and-validated worker id) rather than the raw device id, keeping device_id/device_seq as the replay key. If per-hold release is desired, add a p_hold_id (or reason) selector to release_qc_hold so a single hold can be cleared without re-opening commerce while another hold is still open; otherwise document the all-holds-clear semantics explicitly. Test: two open holds, release one by id, assert the other still blocks.

findings.title: verify_chain('attendance:<id>') reads lot_event, not attendance_event — the CHAIN-VERIFIED badge is a permanent false-positive for every worker
findings.location: people.ts:379-387 (verifyAttendanceChain) calls verify_chain; verify_chain body lives at 20260621092000_event_log_units_lot_graph.sql:309-312 and selects `from lot_event e where e.stream_key = ...`. Consumed by src/app/(app)/crew/page.tsx:62 and rendered by src/components/sections/crew/worker-profile-sheet.tsx:146.
findings.problem: The S1 attendance ledger lives in its OWN table `attendance_event` (stream_key 'attendance:<id>'), but the migration never overloads `verify_chain` for it. The Phase-1 `verify_chain(text)` iterates ONLY `lot_event`, which contains ZERO rows for any 'attendance:<id>' key. The loop runs zero iterations and returns `true` unconditionally. So `verifyAttendanceChain(workerId)` ALWAYS returns true — for an empty ledger, a fully corrupted ledger, or a ledger whose stored hashes were re-forged by anyone with raw table-write access. The green CHAIN-VERIFIED badge on the worker profile sheet (the payroll/labor-law evidence-integrity signal) is a permanent false-positive that verifies nothing. The append-only invariant's user-facing proof is hollow. A pre-existing repro test (src/test/db/_s1repro.db.test.ts) already documents and LOCKS IN this behavior: it records 2 real attendance rows, asserts verify_chain returns true, asserts 0 rows were actually verified in lot_event and 2 sit unverified in attendance_event — and the test PASSES, so the broken behavior is asserted as correct.
findings.scenario: Open /crew, expand any rehire-eligible worker's profile sheet. The badge shows green CHAIN-VERIFIED. Now (as owner with table write, or via any future drift) tamper with that worker's attendance_event hashes — the badge stays green. Confirmed by running `npx vitest run src/test/db/_s1repro.db.test.ts`: verify_chain('attendance:w-06')=true while lot_event has 0 rows and attendance_event has 2 unverified rows.
findings.fix: Add an `attendance_event` (and `worker_stream_event`) variant of verify_chain in the S1 migration that recomputes the chain over the correct table using its own canonical-bytes folding (event_kind for attendance, kind for worker_stream), ordered by device_seq per stream_key; or generalize verify_chain to dispatch on the stream_key prefix. Point people.ts:verifyAttendanceChain at the attendance-aware RPC. Then FLIP the assertion in src/test/db/_s1repro.db.test.ts to expect detection of a real ledger (and false on a tampered row) instead of asserting the vacuous-true bug.

findings.title: sign_por_obra_contract supersedes ALL non-superseded contracts for worker+task, destroying historical closed-window rates the payroll resolver needs
findings.location: sign_por_obra_contract, lines 637-640: `update por_obra_contracts set superseded_by = new_id where worker_id=... and task_kind=... and id <> new_id and superseded_by is null`
findings.problem: The supersede UPDATE targets EVERY prior contract for the worker+task whose superseded_by is null — not just the one whose effective window is currently open/overlapping. Because the RPC never sets superseded_by on a freshly-inserted contract, multiple non-overlapping CLOSED historical contracts (e.g. a Jan–Mar piece rate and an Apr–Jun piece rate) both legitimately carry superseded_by IS NULL. Signing a NEW contract for that worker+task stamps superseded_by on ALL of them. v_active_por_obra (lines 472-474) filters on `superseded_by is null`, so it then resolves NOTHING for any past date the now-superseded historical contract used to cover. Back-dated payroll reruns and piece-work reconciliation silently lose the rate that was actually in force — a money-correctness defect against the 'por-obra rate immutable / forever auditable on a given day' invariant. The official p2s1_people test only covers two OPEN-ended contracts (effective_to=null) where superseding the prior one is correct, so this escapes coverage.
findings.scenario: Sign A: picking, $3.00, 2026-01-01..2026-03-31 (key A). Sign B: picking, $3.50, 2026-06-01..open (key B). After B, contract A is superseded. Now `select * from v_active_por_obra('w-06','picking','2026-02-15')` returns ZERO rows — payroll can no longer resolve the Feb piece rate. Confirmed with a PGlite repro (sign two non-overlapping closed/open contracts; resolver for a date inside the first window returns []).
findings.fix: Scope the supersede UPDATE to only the contract whose window the new one actually replaces — e.g. `... and superseded_by is null and (effective_to is null or effective_to >= <new effective_from>) and effective_from <= coalesce(<new effective_to>, 'infinity')` (only overlapping/open windows), or supersede only the single most-recent open contract (order by effective_from desc limit 1 via a subselect). Add a db test with two non-overlapping closed historical contracts asserting the older one stays resolvable for its own past dates.

findings.title: _backfill_people() aborts with crews_pkey duplicate when two distinct workers.crew strings slug to the same id — breaks the lossless backfill + the whole seed/migration txn
findings.location: _backfill_people(), lines 115-120: insert into crews (id, name) ... `on conflict (name) do nothing`. The id is a slug of the name (line 117).
findings.problem: crews has id (PK, the slug) and name (UNIQUE). The backfill inserts one crews row per DISTINCT workers.crew string with `on conflict (name) do nothing`. But two DISTINCT name strings can slug to the SAME id (e.g. 'Crew Norte', 'Crew  Norte' (double space), 'Crew-Norte' all -> 'crew-norte'; or accented vs unaccented variants the translate() only partially folds). The `on conflict (name)` clause guards only the unique NAME — it does NOT catch the PRIMARY KEY (id) collision, so the second insert raises `duplicate key value violates unique constraint "crews_pkey"`. _backfill_people is SECURITY DEFINER and is called by BOTH this migration (line 139) AND seed.sql (line 58), so the collision aborts the entire seed (or migration) transaction — no crews, no memberships, no identity rows land. Violates the 'crew backfill lossless' invariant: free-text crew entry with trivial punctuation/spacing variance is plausible on a family farm.
findings.scenario: A future or edited worker row carries crew = 'Crew-Norte' alongside existing 'Crew Norte'. `select _backfill_people();` raises crews_pkey duplicate and rolls back. Confirmed with a PGlite repro seeding three workers with 'Crew Norte' / 'Crew  Norte' / 'Crew-Norte' — _backfill_people errors `duplicate key value violates unique constraint "crews_pkey"`. (Current committed seed strings are distinct enough not to collide today, so prod seed is green — but the guard is wrong.)
findings.fix: Make the crews insert idempotent on the actual collision surface: either `on conflict (id) do nothing` (and fold same-slug names to one crew, accepting the name merge), or deduplicate the slug source first (group distinct workers.crew by slug, pick a representative name), or add a uniqueness disambiguator to the slug. Add a db test seeding two crew strings that collide on slug and asserting _backfill_people succeeds losslessly.

findings.title: enroll_crew_member appends a redundant WORKER_ENROLLED event to the immutable ledger on a same-crew no-op re-enroll
findings.location: enroll_crew_member, lines 567-589: the membership update/insert is correctly guarded (`crew_id <> p_crew_id` on close; `if not exists ... active in this crew` on open), but the worker_stream_event WORKER_ENROLLED insert at 580-586 runs UNCONDITIONALLY after that guard.
findings.problem: When a worker is already actively in the target crew and enroll_crew_member is called again with a NEW idempotency_key (a distinct deliberate user action, not a double-submit), the membership logic correctly no-ops (count stays 1), but a WORKER_ENROLLED event is STILL appended to the append-only, hash-chained worker:<id> stream. The immutable ledger now records an enrollment that never happened — a permanent false event in the worker's life-stream that downstream readers (getWorkerStream, audit, any future payroll/labor proof) treat as a real crew change. It cannot be deleted (append-only). Distinct from the idempotency_key dedupe, which only catches true replays of the SAME key.
findings.scenario: Worker w-06 is active in crew-tizingal (from backfill). Call enroll_crew_member('w-06','crew-tizingal',<now>,'server',5,'enr1') — membership count stays 1 but a WORKER_ENROLLED row is appended to worker:w-06. Confirmed with a PGlite repro: 1 membership, 1 spurious WORKER_ENROLLED event.
findings.fix: Only append the WORKER_ENROLLED event when an actual membership change occurred — guard the worker_stream_event insert behind the same `if not exists (active in this crew)` condition (or capture whether the membership insert ran and append only then). Add a db test asserting a same-crew re-enroll appends no new worker_stream_event.

findings.title: Online-only (flag-OFF) write path resets device_seq to 1 on every page load → guaranteed (device_id, device_seq) UNIQUE collisions and 23505 rejections
findings.location: runtime.ts:88-91 (store = createMemoryStore() when !enabled) + enqueueCommand.ts:52 (disabled path calls outbox.enqueue) + outbox.ts:126 (nextDeviceSeq) + device.ts:43-48
findings.problem: On the offline-DISABLED path (flag off, or no IndexedDB — private mode / locked-down WebView), getRuntime() builds the outbox over an in-memory store (runtime.ts:88-91). enqueueCommand's disabled branch still calls outbox.enqueue() (enqueueCommand.ts:52), which mints device_seq via nextDeviceSeq(store) (outbox.ts:126 → device.ts:43). Because the store is in-memory and thrown away per tab/load, the persisted counter restarts: the FIRST command of every page load gets device_seq = 1, the second = 2, etc. The Phase-1 lot_event schema enforces UNIQUE (device_id, device_seq) (migration 20260621092000:223). The Phase-1 server actions deliberately AVOID this by using a 48-bit random device_seq (harvests/actions.ts:54-59) or a Date.now()*1000 base (processing/actions.ts:54-57) precisely 'because a per-process monotonic cursor restarts at every cold start and collides' (their own HIGH-review comments). S0 re-introduces the exact bug those comments fixed, but on the CLIENT: every Volcán phone/WebView that can't persist (or has the flag off) replays device_seq 1,2,3… each reload. If those commands carry a shared device_id (which the design intends — getDeviceId is a stable per-install id, device.ts:29-36), the SECOND day's intake at seq=1 collides with the first day's seq=1 → Postgres 23505 on lot_event_device_id_device_seq_key.
