The idempotency_key early-return (migration:396-401) does NOT save it: a genuinely-new intake has a fresh idempotency_key, so it passes the dedup check and then trips the (device_id, device_seq) UNIQUE on insert → the picker's real weigh-in is rejected as a phantom 'already recorded'.
findings.scenario: Picker's phone is in private mode (no IndexedDB) so offlineCapable() is false → disabled path. Monday: 3 intakes mint device_seq 1,2,3 against device_id X, all succeed. Tuesday after a reload: first intake mints device_seq=1 again, fresh idempotency_key → passes idempotency check, hits UNIQUE(device_id=X, device_seq=1) → 23505 → friendlyRpcError maps it to 'This intake looks like it was already recorded — please refresh' (recordCherryIntake.ts:153-159). A real harvest is silently refused with a misleading 'already recorded' message. Crew payroll under-counts.
findings.fix: The disabled/online-only path must NOT use the persisted-monotonic device_seq with a stable device_id. Either (a) keep the Phase-1 random-48-bit device_seq for the disabled path (don't mint via nextDeviceSeq when offline is off), or (b) make nextDeviceSeq durable on the disabled path too (persist the counter in localStorage, not the throwaway memory store), or (c) make device_seq globally unique by deriving it from the v7 uuid's timestamp+counter rather than a restartable 1,2,3. The cleanest: on the disabled path, fall through to the existing Phase-1 server action which already mints a collision-proof seq, instead of re-enqueuing through the outbox.

findings.title: getDeviceId (the stable per-install device identity the whole module is built around) is never wired into the enqueue path — device_id is left to each caller, so the (device_id, device_seq) causal key is incoherent
findings.location: device.ts:29-36 (getDeviceId, exported, never called); runtime.ts:83-104 (getRuntime / getEnqueueCommand never resolve a device_id); outbox.ts:124-139 (enqueue takes deviceId from CommandInput, never fills it)
findings.problem: device.ts documents device_id as 'a stable per-install UUIDv7 — the SAME value every session, so a device's event stream is coherent across reloads' and pairs it with the persisted-monotonic device_seq to satisfy lot_event's UNIQUE(device_id, device_seq). But getDeviceId() is exported (index.ts:21) and NEVER called outside its own test (grep: only device.test.ts + the export). The enqueue path (outbox.ts:124) takes deviceId from the caller's CommandInput, and the runtime's getEnqueueCommand() (runtime.ts:102-104) does nothing to inject a resolved device_id. So the load-bearing invariant — a stable device_id paired with the monotonic device_seq — is only half-wired: device_seq IS minted by the outbox, but device_id is whatever string the field surface happens to pass. This means: (1) the monotonic device_seq (1,2,3…) is paired with an UNRELIABLE device_id, defeating the collision-avoidance the whole device.ts comment promises; (2) if two field surfaces pass DIFFERENT device_ids while sharing the same monotonic seq space, or the SAME device_id while having independent seq spaces, the UNIQUE key is either needlessly collision-prone or silently wrong. The module ships an unused identity primitive and a half-built contract.
findings.scenario: A future field slice (S2 weigh) calls getEnqueueCommand()({ rpc, args, occurredAt, deviceId: 'phone' }) — it must hand-roll a device_id because the runtime won't supply one. A second surface (S1 attendance) passes deviceId: 'phone' too (the obvious value). Now both share device_id='phone' AND the single outbox's monotonic seq space, which is fine — but nothing enforces it. The moment one surface forgets and passes a different/empty device_id, the seq space (1,2,3 from the shared outbox) is re-used under two device_ids — and on the disabled path resets to 1 — producing the collisions in finding #1. The contract is 'coherent per-install identity'; the code delivers 'whatever the caller types'.
findings.fix: Resolve device_id once in the runtime (await getDeviceId(store)) and have getEnqueueCommand/enqueue stamp it onto every CommandInput, exactly as it already stamps device_seq — so callers cannot supply (or omit) it. Pair the persisted device_id with the persisted device_seq as a single atomic identity. Add a test asserting two enqueues from the same runtime carry the identical device_id and strictly-increasing device_seq.

findings.title: No single-flight guard on flush()/drain() — concurrent online events double-send every queued command (exactly-once relies entirely on the server)
findings.location: sync.ts:126-160 (drain has no in-flight gate; start() awaits drain while onOnline also calls drain) + outbox.ts:141-170 (flush reads queued entries, awaits send per entry, only persists status AFTER the await resolves)
findings.problem: flush() (outbox.ts:141) snapshots all queued entries, then for each `await transport.send(attempted)` and only persists status='done' AFTER the await resolves (outbox.ts:150-153). drain() (sync.ts:126) has no re-entrancy guard, and two drain triggers overlap trivially: start() does win.addEventListener('online', onOnline) then `await drain()` (sync.ts:153,159); if an 'online' event fires during that first drain, onOnline → void drain() (sync.ts:145-147) runs a SECOND flush concurrently. Both flushes read the same entry while its status is still 'queued' (the first send's await hasn't resolved/persisted), so BOTH call transport.send() for the SAME command — a double replay. The local outbox provides NO exactly-once guarantee; it leans 100% on the server's idempotency_key dedup. For registered RPCs that's mostly safe (migration:396-401 early-returns), but: (a) it doubles every network round-trip on a flaky reconnect (costly on a 1,700-masl cellular link); (b) any command whose Server Action is NOT idempotent at the transport layer (e.g. a non-event side effect, or the S0 router dead-lettering an unregistered rpc TWICE) is replayed twice; (c) it contradicts the slice's own header claim of 'exactly-once replay on idempotency_key' — the CLIENT does not enforce exactly-once, only the server does, and only for rpcs that happen to dedup.
findings.scenario: Picker reconnects at the mill gate; the radio flaps. SyncStatusIsland mounts → engine.start() → drain()→flush() begins sending entry A (await in flight). The flapping fires an 'online' event → onOnline → drain()→flush() reads A (still 'queued') → sends A again. Two identical POSTs for the same weigh-in race to the server. DB dedups on idempotency_key so no double row — but the family burned 2x bandwidth and, if A's rpc isn't registered yet (S0), it's dead-lettered, then the second send finds it already 'dead' and is skipped... only because timing was lucky.
findings.fix: Add a single-flight gate: a module-level `let draining = false` in createSyncEngine (or an isFlushing flag in the outbox) so a second drain/flush while one is in progress is coalesced/awaited rather than run concurrently. Add a test that fires two overlapping drains and asserts transport.send is called exactly once per entry.

findings.title: enqueueCommand performs ZERO argument validation — negative kg, bad enums, missing FKs are queued durably offline and only rejected hours later as opaque dead-letters
findings.location: enqueueCommand.ts:39-47 (offline branch enqueues cmd.args verbatim, no validation) + outbox.ts:124-139 (enqueue stores args as-is)
findings.problem: enqueueCommand is documented as 'the ONE call every later field-capture slice makes instead of the raw Server Action' (enqueueCommand.ts:5-6). The Phase-1 commands each ship a pure validator that mirrors the SQL CHECKs to 'surface friendly errors before the round-trip' (recordCherryIntake.ts:63-114 rejects cherriesKg<=0, unknown variety, missing plot/worker, bad occurredAt). enqueueCommand has NO equivalent: on the offline branch it enqueues cmd.args verbatim and returns 'queued' immediately (enqueueCommand.ts:42-47). So an offline intake of -5 kg, or variety='Bourbon' (not in the enum), or a missing worker_id, is accepted as 'queued ✓', the picker walks away believing it's recorded, and the bad command sits in the outbox until reconnect — where the SQL CHECK fires, the transport returns 'rejected', and it becomes a dead-letter with a raw-ish server message. The whole point of the friendly-error seam (validate before the round-trip) is bypassed for every offline write, which is the PRIMARY path of this slice. Worse, a -kg value passed the client and is now durable; nothing catches it at capture time.
findings.scenario: Picker weighs cherries offline; a fat-fingered '-12.4' (or the form sends kg as a string the SQL coerces oddly). enqueueCommand returns 'queued', pill shows 'offline · safe'. Three hours later at reconnect, the command dead-letters. The picker is gone; the operator sees a cryptic dead-letter for a weigh-in that the picker swears they recorded. The crew member may be under-paid for that bag. On the online (disabled) path it's only marginally better — it surfaces synchronously but still as a server 'rejected', not the friendly per-field validator.
findings.fix: enqueueCommand should run the command's pure validator (the same ValidationResult seam the Phase-1 commands export) BEFORE enqueuing, returning outcome:'rejected' with per-field errors at capture time — never durably queue input that the client already knows is invalid. Register a validator per rpc alongside the Server Action in the runtime registry, and gate enqueue on it. Add tests for negative kg / bad enum rejected at enqueue.

findings.title: A throwing 4xx (non-idempotent permanent failure) is misclassified as a retryable network-error and requeued forever — no attempt cap, so it never dead-letters
findings.location: transport.ts:57-62 (catch → network-error for ANY throw) + runtime.ts:70-75 (router: catch → network-error) + outbox.ts:155-159 (network-error stays queued, attempts++ uncapped)
findings.problem: The classification contract is: action resolves {ok:false} = business rejection (dead-letter); action THROWS = transport/network failure (requeue forever). transport.ts:57-62 and the router (runtime.ts:70-75) map EVERY thrown error to network-error → the outbox keeps it 'queued' and retries on every drain with no attempts cap (outbox.ts:155-159; attempts is incremented but never consulted — its own comment says 'for backoff/telemetry, not behaviour', outbox.ts:53). This is correct ONLY if field Server Actions are perfectly disciplined to return {ok:false} for ALL deterministic refusals and throw ONLY on transient failures. In practice a Server Action that hits a permanent 4xx, a serialization error, a thrown PostgREST error, or any uncaught exception (e.g. a bad arg that makes the rpc throw before the CHECK returns a clean false) will THROW — and be misclassified as retryable, looping forever as a silent 'queued' that never reaches the dead-letter drawer the operator watches. The slice invariant 'a business rejection → visible dead, NEVER silently lost or confused with a retryable failure' is violated for any rejection that surfaces as a throw rather than {ok:false}.
findings.scenario: S2's weigh Server Action forwards to the rpc; the rpc raises (e.g. unknown lot, FK violation, or an unmapped exception) instead of returning {ok:false}. The transport catches the throw → network-error → the command stays 'queued' forever, retried every reconnect, never dead-lettered, never surfaced as 'failed'. The pill shows a perpetual 'pending', the operator never sees it needs attention, and the write is effectively lost-but-invisible — the exact confusion the invariant forbids.
findings.fix: Distinguish transient from permanent in the catch: inspect the error (HTTP status / PG errcode) — 5xx/network/timeouts → network-error (requeue); 4xx/constraint/permission → rejected (dead-letter). At minimum add an attempts cap so an entry that has failed N times moves to dead-letter with a clear message rather than requeuing forever, so a misclassified permanent failure can't hide as 'pending' indefinitely. Add a test that a throwing-permanent action eventually dead-letters.

findings.title: schedule_pasada / replan_pasada fire a non-idempotent tasks INSERT before their dedup row, so a concurrent S0 outbox replay leaks orphan Harvest tasks and the second call mis-classifies 23505 as retryable → infinite requeue
findings.location: schedule_pasada lines 363-402 (tasks insert 381-392, pasada_schedule insert 394-400); replan_pasada lines 435-484 (tasks insert 455-466, pasada_schedule insert 468-474). Schema: pasada_schedule.idempotency_key UNIQUE (line 129) + UNIQUE(device_id,device_seq) (130). Compare record_maturation_signal which DOES use `on conflict (idempotency_key) do nothing` (line 320).
findings.problem: Both pasada RPCs implement exactly-once with a check-then-act: `select id from pasada_schedule where idempotency_key=...; if found return` (364/436), then they `insert into tasks (id=gen_random_uuid(), category='Harvest', ...)` with NO idempotency guard and NO ON CONFLICT (382/456), and only THEN insert pasada_schedule WITHOUT `on conflict do nothing` (394/468). This is not concurrency-safe and it orders the irreversible side-effect (a real /tasks board row) before the dedup row commits. The S0 outbox can replay the SAME entry concurrently: createSyncEngine.start() registers the `online` listener (sync.ts:153) and then `await drain()` (159); outbox.flush() (outbox.ts:141-170) has NO in-flight/reentrancy guard and marks an entry `done` only AFTER `await transport.send` resolves. So if an `online` event fires while the initial drain's flush is awaiting the network (the literal 1,700 masl intermittent-signal scenario), onOnline → drain → a SECOND flush observes the same entry still `queued` and sends it again. Two concurrent schedule_pasada calls with the same idempotency_key both pass the line-364 existence check (neither sees the other's uncommitted row), both insert a tasks row with a DIFFERENT gen_random_uuid → one orphan Harvest task is committed by the loser even after its pasada_schedule insert hits the idempotency_key/`(device_id,device_seq)` UNIQUE and throws 23505. The throw propagates to the Server Action which rethrows; transport.ts:57-62 maps any throw to `{kind:'network-error'}`, so outbox.flush() keeps the entry `queued` (outbox.ts:155-159) and retries it forever — a deterministic 23505 is being treated as a transient retryable failure, violating the S0 invariant that a business/deterministic rejection becomes a visible dead-letter, never a silent infinite retry. Net: duplicate phantom 'Pasada N — pick <plot>' tasks land on the picker's board, and the queue never drains.
findings.scenario: A supervisor schedules a pasada offline at a plot. The entry queues. Back in patchy signal the radio flaps: the SyncStatus island mounts (start() begins the initial drain, flush() in-flight awaiting the POST), and an `online` event fires → onOnline → drain → second flush picks up the same still-queued entry and POSTs schedule_pasada again. Server txn A commits the plan + task A; txn B (same idempotency_key) passes the existence check, inserts task B (new uuid), then hits 23505 on pasada_schedule. The picker now sees TWO identical Harvest tasks; the outbox shows the entry as 'Waiting to sync' and retries it on every reconnect with no progress.
findings.fix: Make the side-effect idempotent and put the dedup row FIRST. Option A (cleanest): give tasks a deterministic, idempotency-derived id and `on conflict (id) do nothing` — e.g. `task_id := md5('pasada:'||p_idempotency_key)::uuid::text` (or gen_random_uuid only AFTER a successful guarded pasada_schedule insert), insert pasada_schedule with `on conflict (idempotency_key) do nothing` and short-circuit-return if the insert hit a conflict, and only fire the task on the genuinely-new path. Option B: add a unique advisory lock per idempotency_key (`perform pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext(p_idempotency_key))`) at the top so concurrent same-key calls serialize. Separately, harden the transport so a deterministic constraint error (23505/23503/23514/raise with a 4xx-style class) is classified `rejected` (dead-letter), not `network-error`, so a poison replay surfaces instead of looping. Add a concurrent-flush test (two overlapping outbox.flush() promises send a queued entry exactly once) — the current outbox.test.ts:97-105 only covers SEQUENTIAL flushes.

findings.title: outbox.flush() has no reentrancy guard, so overlapping drain() calls re-send the same queued entry — exactly-once holds only for sequential flushes, and the suite never tests the concurrent case
findings.location: flush() lines 141-170 (no in-flight lock; entry marked `done` only at 150-153 after `await transport.send` at 148); driven by sync.ts createSyncEngine.start() 152-160 + onOnline 145-147 (both call drain → flush) and drain() 126-143 (sets `syncing` but does NOT prevent a second drain entering). Test gap: outbox.test.ts:97-105 only flushes sequentially.
findings.problem: flush() iterates `queued` entries and awaits transport.send for each before writing `done`. There is no `if (draining) return` / in-flight promise guard, and drain() in the sync engine likewise has none — it sets state.syncing but a second drain() can enter while the first's `await outbox.flush()` is pending (start() registers the online listener at sync.ts:153 BEFORE awaiting drain at 159). So two overlapping flush()es each observe the same not-yet-`done` entry as `queued` and both call transport.send. For idempotent RPCs (record_moisture_reading, record_ferment_reading, etc.) the server dedups on idempotency_key so the duplicate is harmless — but it is wasted work, and for the non-idempotent-side-effect RPCs (schedule_pasada/replan_pasada, see the related HIGH) it produces real corruption. The slice's central claimed invariant is 'exactly-once replay on idempotency_key'; the client half only guarantees that under strictly sequential flushes, and nothing in S0 enforces sequentiality.
findings.scenario: Device has queued entries and connectivity is flapping (online event during an in-flight flush), or the user taps a future 'Sync now' / the engine re-starts on a route change while a drain is in-flight. Two flush() runs overlap and double-send every still-in-flight entry to the server in the same window.
findings.fix: Serialize flush(): keep a module-level `let inflight: Promise<FlushSummary> | null`; `if (inflight) return inflight;` at the top, assign the running promise, clear it in a finally. Mirror the guard in createSyncEngine.drain() (skip/queue a re-drain if one is running rather than starting a second). Add a regression test that starts two flush() promises against a transport whose send() does not resolve until both have begun, and assert send was called exactly once per entry.

findings.title: Service-Worker cache-bust invariant is dead: CACHE_VERSION is a hard-coded 'janson-v1' literal with no build-time injection, so activate() purges nothing on deploy and returning field users get the stale shell / 404'd Next chunks the comment claims to defeat
findings.location: const CACHE_VERSION = "janson-v1" (line 16); activate purge filters on `!k.startsWith(CACHE_VERSION)` (line 65); precache shell addAll(['/', ...]) install-time only (lines 23, 48-54); cacheFirst for /_next/static/ (123-124). package.json build is plain `next build` (no SW version/codegen step); files in public/ are served verbatim.
findings.problem: The S0 invariant states the SW 'must cache-bust on deploy', and the file's own header (lines 11-13) claims 'The cache version is bumped per release so a new deploy purges the old shell on activate — the cache-bust that defeats the stale-chunk white-screen footgun.' But CACHE_VERSION is a static string literal that nothing ever bumps: the build is plain `next build`, public/sw.js ships byte-for-byte, and there is no env/hash/codegen injection anywhere (grep confirms the only writers of CACHE_VERSION are the three literal lines in sw.js). Consequently, on EVERY future deploy the activate handler's filter `!k.startsWith('janson-v1')` matches no caches and deletes nothing; the precache `/` (navigation HTML) captured on first install is never refreshed; and cacheFirst keeps serving old content-hashed /_next/static chunks. After a deploy, Next has purged the old chunk filenames, the cached HTML references them, and a returning installed user gets a stale shell pointing at 404'd chunks — the exact white-screen footgun the comment says it prevents. This breaks every returning field user after a deploy, defeating the whole offline-trust premise (90% Ngäbe-Buglé crew on installed PWAs at altitude).
findings.scenario: v1 deploys; a picker installs the PWA, SW caches shell+chunks under janson-v1. A new build ships (new chunk hashes). The picker reopens the installed app offline-or-online: activate purges nothing (version unchanged), the cached navigation HTML loads, its <script> tags request old /_next/static/*.js which 404 (online) or miss cache (offline) → blank/broken app until a manual cache wipe.
findings.fix: Stamp CACHE_VERSION at build time so it changes per deploy: e.g. a tiny prebuild script that rewrites public/sw.js (or generates it) with the git SHA / Date.now() / package version, or move the SW to an app route that interpolates `process.env.VERCEL_GIT_COMMIT_SHA`. With a real per-build version, activate's existing purge logic works and the precached '/' is replaced. Add a test/guard asserting CACHE_VERSION is not a constant literal across two builds (or that the build script produces a differing version).

findings.title: "One active pasada plan per (plot, pasada)" invariant is bypassable — no advisory lock, no unique constraint on the active set (sibling slice uses pg_advisory_xact_lock for this exact race)
findings.dimension: DATA-LAYER INVARIANT BYPASS (advisory-lock race / missing unique guard)
findings.location: replan_pasada lines 411-484 (read-modify-write at 447-481); schedule_pasada 340-403; partial index line 133 (pasada_schedule_active_idx is NON-unique)
findings.problem: The append-only/supersede design promises exactly one ACTIVE (non-superseded) pasada per (plot, pasada): replan_pasada does a read-modify-write — SELECT the current active plan (line 447-451), INSERT a new 'planned' row (468-474), then UPDATE the old one to 'superseded' (477-481). There is NO `pg_advisory_xact_lock` and NO unique index enforcing one active row per (plot, pasada_number). The active partial index at line 133 is explicitly non-unique. Under the default READ COMMITTED isolation, two concurrent replan_pasada txns for the same (plot, pasada) — two browser tabs, a double-click, or an S0 outbox replaying two distinct envelopes — both SELECT the same `current_id`, both INSERT a new active 'planned' row, and both UPDATE the SAME old row to superseded. Result: TWO active plans and TWO fired tasks on the /tasks board for one harvest pass. This is the IDENTICAL race the sibling green_inventory slice defends with `pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext('green_lot:'||code))` taken BEFORE the committed-total read (20260621093500_green_inventory.sql:95-110); S8 simply omits that defense. The invariant the whole append-only/superseded chain exists to protect (a single canonical current plan, one task per pass) is silently broken, and v_pasada_calendar (line 259, status<>'superseded') then surfaces both — double-dispatching the Ngäbe-Buglé crew to the same plot on the same morning.
findings.scenario: Owner opens /plan in two tabs (or the field tablet retries on flaky Volcán connectivity). Both tabs fire replanPasada for plot Geisha-Alto, pasada 2, around the same rain front. Tab A txn: SELECT current active -> row #10; INSERT row #11 (active); UPDATE #10->superseded. Tab B txn (interleaved, started before A committed): SELECT current active -> still sees #10 (A not committed); INSERT row #12 (active); UPDATE #10->superseded (no-op/redundant). Commit both. pasada_schedule now has #11 AND #12 both status='planned' for (Geisha-Alto, 2); the board has two 'Pasada 2 — pick Geisha-Alto' tasks. No further replan ever heals it: the next replan_pasada SELECT ... LIMIT 1 supersedes only ONE of {#11,#12}, leaving the other active forever.
findings.fix: Mirror the green_inventory defense exactly. (1) At the top of BOTH schedule_pasada and replan_pasada (after the idempotency early-return), take `perform pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext('pasada:'||p_plot_id||':'||p_pasada_number));` so concurrent (re)plans for the same pass serialize and each sees the prior's committed state. (2) Add a hard backstop the lock can't be forgotten past: `create unique index pasada_schedule_one_active_idx on pasada_schedule (plot_id, pasada_number) where status <> 'superseded';` — this makes a second active row fail-closed at the DB even if a future caller bypasses the function. (Adding the unique index alone also surfaces the schedule_pasada duplicate in the next finding.)

findings.title: schedule_pasada has ZERO duplicate-active guard — single-threaded re-call creates a second active plan + second task, and replan's LIMIT-1 supersede can never heal it
findings.dimension: DATA-LAYER INVARIANT BYPASS (missing WHERE / missing existence check)
findings.location: schedule_pasada lines 340-403 (only existence check is on idempotency_key, line 364); replan_pasada supersede SELECT line 447-451 (order by recorded_at desc LIMIT 1)
findings.problem: schedule_pasada's ONLY guard against creating a duplicate is the idempotency_key replay check (line 364-367). It never checks whether an ACTIVE plan already exists for (plot_id, pasada_number). So a perfectly sequential second call with a fresh idempotency_key — which is exactly what the Server Action mints every invocation (`p_idempotency_key: crypto.randomUUID()` in actions.ts:43) — appends a SECOND active 'planned' row and fires a SECOND task. There is no concurrency required: this is reachable today with one user clicking 'Schedule pasada 2' twice, or scheduling pasada 2 once and then replanning won't supersede the FIRST schedule if a second schedule already created an extra active row. Worse, once two active rows exist, replan_pasada's heal path SELECTs the active plan with `order by recorded_at desc LIMIT 1` (line 447-451) and supersedes only ONE — and since both duplicates default `recorded_at` to now() and can share a timestamp, WHICH one gets superseded is non-deterministic. The append-only-with-single-active invariant is bypassable through the front door with no special timing.
findings.scenario: Owner schedules pasada 2 for plot Geisha-Bajo (row #20, task T1). The calendar doesn't make the existing plan obvious, so a week later they click 'Schedule pasada 2' again (fresh idempotency_key) -> row #21 active, task T2. Both show on v_pasada_calendar; the crew sees two identical harvest tasks. Owner notices and replans pasada 2 to dodge rain: replan supersedes only #21 (LIMIT 1), appends #22, leaves #20 active alongside #22 — and its stale task T1 still sits on the board with the old date.
findings.fix: In schedule_pasada, after the plot-exists check, reject when an active plan already exists: `if exists (select 1 from pasada_schedule where plot_id = p_plot_id and pasada_number = p_pasada_number and status <> 'superseded') then raise exception 'pasada % for plot % is already scheduled — replan it instead', p_pasada_number, p_plot_id using errcode = 'unique_violation'; end if;` (under the advisory lock from the prior finding). Make the friendlyError mapper in actions.ts surface a 'that pass is already scheduled — re-plan it' message. The `where status <> 'superseded'` partial-unique index proposed above is the data-layer backstop that makes this fail-closed regardless of the function.

findings.title: pasada_schedule supersede-stamp guard only pins 3 of ~12 columns — the supersede UPDATE can silently rewrite ripeness band, season, reason, fired_task_id, and the device/idempotency envelope
findings.dimension: DATA-LAYER INVARIANT BYPASS (append-only trigger that under-checks the permitted op)
findings.location: pasada_schedule_guard lines 140-158, specifically the permitted-UPDATE predicate at 151-153
findings.problem: The guard's stated contract (comment lines 135-139, 149-150) is: the ONLY legal UPDATE is 'a planned row becoming superseded' and 'everything else is rejected... history is never rewritten.' But the predicate at 151-153 only asserts `status` flips to superseded and that `predicted_ready_date`, `plot_id`, `pasada_number` are unchanged. It does NOT pin `predicted_ripe_pct`, `season`, `reason`, `fired_task_id`, `occurred_at`, `recorded_at`, `device_id`, `device_seq`, or `idempotency_key`. So an UPDATE that flips status->superseded is free to ALSO rewrite any of those audit fields, and the guard waves it through — directly contradicting 'history is never rewritten.' Today the only superseder is replan_pasada (owner) which changes only status+superseded_by, so it is not actively exploited; but the guard is the codified invariant, not the current caller. A future writer (another slice, a maintenance script, a manual owner fix) that issues an UPDATE touching status plus, say, fired_task_id or idempotency_key would pass the guard and corrupt the ledger's audit fields, including the idempotency_key that the exactly-once contract depends on. A defense-in-depth append-only trigger that leaves 9 columns rewritable on its one permitted path is a weak guard.
findings.scenario: A future cleanup writer runs `update pasada_schedule set status='superseded', fired_task_id=null, idempotency_key='manual-fix' where id=#30` to 'tidy' a superseded row. The guard checks status flipped, ready_date/plot/pasada unchanged -> passes. The row's idempotency_key is now mutated, so the original schedule_pasada envelope can replay and re-fire; the fired_task linkage is lost. History was rewritten, exactly what the guard claims to prevent.
findings.fix: Tighten the permitted-UPDATE predicate so the supersede stamp may change ONLY status and superseded_by — pin everything else: add to the AND-chain `and new.predicted_ripe_pct is not distinct from old.predicted_ripe_pct and new.season = old.season and new.reason is not distinct from old.reason and new.fired_task_id is not distinct from old.fired_task_id and new.occurred_at = old.occurred_at and new.recorded_at = old.recorded_at and new.device_id = old.device_id and new.device_seq = old.device_seq and new.idempotency_key is not distinct from old.idempotency_key and new.pasada_number = old.pasada_number`. (The cleanest form: compare the whole OLD and NEW rows with only status/superseded_by allowed to differ.)

findings.title: Fired harvest-pass task is assigned to an absent / departed / wrong-role worker — _resolve_pasada_worker ranks the most-recent picker #1 with no attendance or active-membership filter
findings.dimension: DATA-LAYER INVARIANT BYPASS (task-firing 'valid tasks row' invariant — worker_id satisfies NOT NULL but is semantically invalid)
findings.location: _resolve_pasada_worker lines 267-287 (rank-1 picker at 275-277 has no filter; rank-3 fallback 282-283 picks ANY worker)
findings.problem: The slice's stated invariant is 'task-firing inserts a VALID tasks row (worker_id NOT NULL honored).' The resolver guarantees NOT-NULL but not validity: rank 1 is the plot's most-recent picker from harvests (`order by rank, ord desc` on h.date), with NO filter on workers.attendance ('present'|'absent'|'rest-day') and NO filter on the people-system active crew membership. The most-recent picker on a plot is very often a seasonal Ngäbe-Buglé migrant who has since left the farm (workers row persists for harvest-history FK integrity) or is marked 'absent'/'rest-day' today. The rank-3 last-resort (`select w.id from workers w`, line 283) likewise picks ANY worker — could be a Driver or Mill Operator, never a picker. So schedule_pasada/replan_pasada fire a real /tasks-board harvest task onto someone who is gone, off, or unqualified. The NOT-NULL constraint is satisfied, but the 'valid tasks row' invariant — the actual point of the resolver — is violated, and the harvest pass silently lands on a no-show assignee.
findings.scenario: Plot Geisha-Alto's most-recent picker is worker W-Picker-Seasonal, who finished last season's harvest and left (attendance='absent', crew membership closed). Owner schedules pasada 1. _resolve_pasada_worker picks W-Picker-Seasonal (rank 1, most recent h.date) — no attendance/active check. The task fires onto the board assigned to a worker who isn't on the farm. The pass is 'assigned' but nobody is actually dispatched; the morning card shows a phantom assignee.
findings.fix: Filter rank 1 to currently-active, present pickers: join workers and require `w.attendance <> 'absent'` (and, given the people_system, an open crew_memberships row / not departed); prefer rank-1 candidates whose worker is active. For rank 3, prefer `where role = 'Picker'` before falling back to any worker. Keep the 'any worker' arm only as the true last resort so NOT NULL still always holds, but exhaust active-present-picker -> active Supervisor -> active worker first. Add a unit/SQL test asserting an absent or departed most-recent picker is NOT chosen when an active picker exists.

findings.title: The qc_holds UPDATE-guard's branches (re-open blocked, re-key blocked) have ZERO test coverage — only DELETE is pinned
findings.location: lines 175-179 (the only qc_holds mutation test) vs migration _qc_holds_guard_mutation lines 159-186
findings.problem: `_qc_holds_guard_mutation` is the most intricate guard in the slice: it must (a) block DELETE, (b) block re-opening an already-released hold, (c) block re-keying any column other than the release stamp, and (d) reject an UPDATE that does not actually stamp released_at. The DB test exercises ONLY branch (a) — `delete from qc_holds … rejects(/append-only/)`. Branches (b), (c), (d) — the entire nuanced one-way-release contract the migration's 28-line comment block calls out as 'fragile' and load-bearing — are completely untested. I verified by live probe that these branches currently DO fire ('qc_holds is append-only; only a release stamp may be applied' for a re-key; 'qc_holds N is already released; re-opening is not allowed' for a re-open), but nothing pins them: a future refactor that drops the column-equality comparison or the `old.released_at is not null` check would let history be rewritten or a release reverted, and the suite would stay green.
findings.scenario: Delete the `if old.released_at is not null …` block (or the column-distinct comparison) from `_qc_holds_guard_mutation` and re-run `npm run test` — all 39 S6 tests still pass, because nothing asserts re-open or re-key is blocked. The append-only-in-spirit guarantee for the quarantine ledger (a hold is placed once, released once, never rewritten) silently degrades.
findings.fix: Add to the 'qc_holds is an append-only ledger' describe block: (1) place a hold, legally release it, then `update qc_holds set released_at=null, released_by=null …` → expect rejects(/already released|re-open/i); (2) place a hold, then `update qc_holds set released_at=now(), released_by='x', reason='rewritten' …` → expect rejects(/only a release stamp|append-only/i); (3) `update qc_holds set released_by='x' …` (no released_at) → expect rejects(/must stamp a release/i). These run as the table owner in PGlite, which is exactly the path the DEFINER release RPC takes, so they pin the guard the RPC depends on.

findings.title: No test for placing a QC-hold on a non-existent green lot — an explicitly-listed invariant scenario with a live, uncovered raise branch
findings.location: place_qc_hold migration lines 432-434 (raise 'unknown green lot'); no covering test in the file
findings.problem: The slice INVARIANTS explicitly say to try 'a hold on a non-existent lot', and `place_qc_hold` has a dedicated guard for it (`if not exists (select 1 from green_lots …) then raise exception 'unknown green lot %' using errcode='foreign_key_violation'`). No test ever calls `place_qc_hold` with an unknown lot code. I confirmed via probe the branch fires ('unknown green lot JC-PHANTOM'), but it is dead-to-the-suite: if someone removed that pre-check (relying on the table FK), the error code/message contract would change from a clean foreign_key_violation to a raw FK error, and idempotency-key ordering (the check sits AFTER the idempotency lookup) would be untested. This is the cheapest highest-signal missing test the invariant list directly asks for.
findings.scenario: Comment out lines 432-434 of the migration and re-run — the suite stays green, even though place_qc_hold's behavior on a phantom lot (and the position of the existence check relative to the idempotency-key short-circuit) is now unverified.
findings.fix: Add `it('rejects a hold on a non-existent green lot', async () => expect(h.query("select place_qc_hold('JC-NOPE','r',now(),'srv',999,'k-phantom')")).rejects.toThrow(/unknown green lot|foreign_key/i))` to the teeth or hold-ledger describe block.

findings.title: The held-lot commerce gate is tested only on INSERT; the UPDATE path (which prevent_oversell DOES guard) is unpinned, leaving a guard-asymmetry invisible to tests
findings.location: teeth tests lines 102-137 (INSERT only); migration trigger lines 217-223 (BEFORE INSERT only)
findings.problem: The Phase-1 prevent_oversell family this slice claims to 'extend' fires on BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE on both claim tables (green_inventory.sql lines 134-140) — the design deliberately treats an UPDATE to a reservation/shipment as a re-validation point (it even excludes the self-row). But S6's `_prevent_held_lot_commit` fires on BEFORE INSERT ONLY (lines 218, 222). The teeth tests only ever INSERT against a held lot, so this asymmetry is invisible to the suite: an `update lot_reservations set green_lot_code='<held-lot>' …` or a kg bump on an existing reservation against a now-held lot is NOT blocked by the QC gate. Today no UPDATE table-grant exists for `authenticated` (only INSERT), so it is not reachable via PostgREST — hence MED not CRIT — but the test gap means the moment any future slice adds a reservation-amend RPC/grant, the QC quarantine is silently porous and nothing in S6 will go red. A test that asserts the gate also covers UPDATE would have forced the trigger to match prevent_oversell's INSERT-OR-UPDATE shape.
findings.scenario: Add a reservation-edit path in a later slice (or run as owner): reserve 10kg on a clear lot, place a QC-hold, then `update lot_reservations set kg=20 where … ` — prevent_oversell re-checks but the QC gate does not fire, so a held lot's reservation is silently mutable. No S6 test catches it.
findings.fix: Either (preferred) extend the trigger to `before insert or update on lot_reservations/lot_shipments` to match the prevent_oversell family, AND add a teeth test that UPDATEs an existing reservation on a held lot and expects a qc-hold rejection; or, if INSERT-only is intentional, add an explicit test documenting that UPDATE is NOT gated and a comment justifying it against the prevent_oversell precedent.

findings.title: No test pins the 'final score is a VIEW, not written to green_lots' invariant — recording cup scores could silently mutate green_lots and stay green
findings.location: v_cup_final_score describe block lines 243-302 (asserts the view total only)
findings.problem: A named slice invariant is 'the final score is a VIEW (v_cup_final_score), not written to green_lots.' green_lots carries a separate NOT-NULL `cupping_score` column (set at materialize time, default 84.5 in the fixtures) whose generated `sca_grade` the family reads. The S6 tests verify v_cup_final_score sums correctly, but NOTHING asserts that recording a cupping session/scores leaves green_lots.cupping_score untouched. So the VIEW-not-column separation — the whole point of the invariant — is unpinned: a future change that 'helpfully' writes the cup total back into green_lots.cupping_score (causing sca_grade to flip) would pass the entire suite. The conflation risk is real because v_qc_status.latest_cup_score (from the view) and green_lots.sca_grade (from the column) are two different numbers for the same lot.
findings.scenario: Snapshot green_lots.cupping_score, run a full cupping session + scores summing to e.g. 62, snapshot again — they are equal today, but no test demands it. Add a write-back and the suite stays green while sca_grade silently changes.
findings.fix: Add `it('a cupping session does not mutate green_lots.cupping_score (final is a VIEW, not a column)')`: read green_lots.cupping_score for JC-9001, record a session + several scores, re-read, expect unchanged. This pins the invariant the slice explicitly claims.

findings.title: cupQualityBand is tested only with bare literals (90/85/80) that a real SCA-CVA total can never reach — the band/protocol composition is asserted against impossible inputs
findings.location: lines 92-110 (cupQualityBand tests) vs cva-scoring.ts lines 57-74
findings.problem: cupFinalScore for 'sca-cva' sums 8 attributes each capped 0-10 → max 80 (the file's own comment: 'each 0-10 → max 80'). cupQualityBand maps ≥90→Presidential, ≥85→Specialty, ≥80→Premium. The test asserts cupQualityBand(91)→Presidential and cupQualityBand(85)→Specialty with hand-fed literals, but NEVER composes the two functions for the CVA protocol — so it 'verifies' bands (Presidential/Specialty) that a real CVA cup score can mathematically never produce (a perfect CVA card totals exactly 80 = the very bottom of Premium). The test passes while the band-vs-protocol integration is semantically incoherent: every SCA-CVA lot lands 'Premium' or 'Below Specialty' regardless of cup quality, and no test surfaces that the SCA-CVA scale is being read with the legacy-100 thresholds. This is a test that is green precisely because it never exercises the realistic path.
findings.scenario: Score a flawless CVA card (all 10s) → cupFinalScore('sca-cva', …)=80 → cupQualityBand(80)='Premium'. A world-class Geisha that should band as Presidential/Specialty reads 'Premium'. The unit tests for both functions pass individually; nothing ties them together for the CVA protocol, so the mismatch ships.
findings.fix: Add a composed, protocol-aware test: `expect(cupQualityBand(cupFinalScore('sca-cva', perfectCvaCard))).toBe(<expected>)` and decide the contract — either cupQualityBand must take the protocol (and scale CVA's 80-max appropriately) or the bands must be protocol-specific. At minimum, a test asserting the realistic CVA ceiling (80) and what band it yields would have exposed the incoherence the current literal-only tests hide.

findings.title: Outbox drawer rolls its own modal instead of the shared portal-fixed Dialog — claims aria-modal but has no focus trap, focus restore, or scroll lock
findings.location: OutboxDrawer, lines 62-215 (createPortal at 81; role="dialog" aria-modal="true" at 84-85)
findings.problem: The drawer hand-rolls createPortal + role="dialog" aria-modal="true" (its own docstring promises 'a focus-trapped, Escape-closable dialog portalled to <body>'), but delivers none of the modal contract the shared src/components/ui/dialog.tsx already implements: (1) focus is NEVER moved into the drawer on open — there is no panelRef and no .focus() call, so after click-opening, focus stays on the pill button which is OUTSIDE the portal; (2) no focus restore on close — closing orphans keyboard focus to <body>; (3) no body scroll-lock (the shared Dialog sets document.body.style.overflow='hidden'; this does not), so the cream page scrolls behind the open drawer; (4) no Tab/Shift+Tab focus trap, so Tab walks out of the 'modal' into the page behind it while aria-modal="true" lies to AT that it is contained. This is exactly the 'roll your own modal' pattern the project contract (extend the shared shell primitives, don't fork them) and the phase-1 portal-fix were meant to retire.
findings.scenario: An owner on a phone in a Volcán dead zone taps the 'Needs attention · N' pill to open the outbox. Focus is on the pill (in the topbar, not the portal). They press Tab to reach the Retry button — focus instead walks into the page behind the translucent drawer (Notifications bell, sidebar links), which a screen-reader/keyboard user cannot see is still 'open'. The background also scrolls under their thumb. aria-modal="true" tells VoiceOver the rest of the page is inert, but it is fully reachable — a WCAG 2.1 (1.3.1 / 2.4.3 / 4.1.2) failure.
findings.fix: Render the outbox panel through the shared Dialog (src/components/ui/dialog.tsx), or lift its focus-management into the drawer: on open capture document.activeElement, move focus to a panel ref, trap Tab/Shift+Tab inside the aside, set body overflow hidden, and restore focus on close. Reuse the primitive rather than duplicating a half-modal.

findings.title: Escape never closes the outbox drawer — keydown handler sits on a non-focusable root that never receives focus
findings.location: OutboxDrawer root div onKeyDown, lines 82-90
findings.problem: The Escape handler is a React onKeyDown on the outermost portal <div role="dialog"> which has no tabIndex (so it cannot hold focus) and into which focus is never programmatically moved (see prior finding). React synthetic keydown only fires when the event originates from a node inside this subtree. After the drawer opens via pill click, focus remains on the pill button — a DOM child of the topbar, NOT of the portal — so the Escape keypress dispatches on the pill and never reaches the drawer's handler. Result: Escape is dead until the user has manually clicked a button inside the drawer. The docstring explicitly advertises 'Escape-closable dialog'. The shared Dialog avoids this by attaching a document-level keydown listener instead of an in-subtree React handler.
findings.scenario: Owner opens the sync drawer, decides not to act, presses Escape to dismiss — nothing happens. They must locate and click the small X or the backdrop. On a touch device that is mildly annoying; for a keyboard-only user it is a trap-feeling dead end that contradicts the stated contract.
findings.fix: Attach the Escape listener at the document level inside a useEffect (mirror Dialog: document.addEventListener('keydown', ...)), or — better — route the panel through the shared Dialog which already owns Escape-to-close correctly.

findings.title: Count-badge text fails WCAG AA contrast in the syncing state (text-sky on solid sky-100)
findings.location: VISUALS.syncing badge 'bg-sky-100 text-sky' (lines 62-64) rendered via COUNT_BADGE (line 24, 11px font-semibold) at line 113
findings.problem: The syncing-state count badge renders text-sky (#3b6ea5) on solid bg-sky-100 (#d8e4f0) at text-[11px] font-semibold tabular-nums. Measured contrast is 4.11:1, below the 4.5:1 WCAG-AA threshold for normal-size text (11px is well under the 18.66px/14px-bold large-text exemption). The pill body in syncing/failed states is similarly marginal (text-sky on sky-100/60 ≈ 4.45:1; text-cherry on cherry-100/70 ≈ 4.38:1), all just under 4.5. The number is the load-bearing piece of information ('how many of my weigh-ins are still in flight') so it must clear AA, and the cream/glass background only lowers effective contrast further.
findings.scenario: A picker glances at the topbar mid-sync in bright Volcán daylight on a low-end phone; the '3' on the pale blue badge is the count they care about and it sits below the AA legibility floor — exactly the 'near-invisible series on the cream background' failure this dimension flags.
findings.fix: Darken the badge/pill text for the syncing and failed states to AA-clearing tokens (e.g. a sky-700 around #2a527d and keep cherry text at full #b5482e on a lighter cherry-50 fill, or bump font weight/size). Mirror the deliberate honey-700 'darker — for honey TEXT on light bg (WCAG AA)' token already defined in globals.css and add a sky-700 equivalent.

findings.title: Dead-letter error text (server-supplied lastError) renders unwrapped — long messages overflow / break the narrow drawer card
findings.location:
dead-letter item,

{e.lastError}

, line 149 (inside the max-w-sm aside, line 98)
findings.problem: lastError is set from the transport's rejection message (outbox.ts line 163: result.message ?? 'rejected by the server'), i.e. an arbitrary server string. It is rendered with no break-words / overflow-wrap and no max-width clamp inside a max-w-sm (~24rem) drawer. A realistic Postgres/RPC rejection — e.g. an oversell guard or reposo-gate raise that includes a long lot code, a UUID idempotency_key, or a search_path-style detail with no spaces — will not wrap and will either force horizontal overflow inside the card or be clipped, on the exact surface whose whole purpose (per the docstring) is to show 'exactly why it failed and what to do'.
findings.scenario: A queued record_weigh_in is rejected because the lot is on QC-hold; the server returns 'rejected: lot 2026-GEISHA-LOTE-A7F3B2C1-... is on reposo hold until ...'. In the dead-letter card the un-wrapped string overflows the 24rem panel, so the operator on a phone can't read the actionable reason — defeating the dead-letter UX.
findings.fix:
Add break-words (overflow-wrap: anywhere) to the error

, and consider line-clamp-3 with a title attribute carrying the full text. Same hardening applies to the e.rpc label if RPC names can be long.

findings.title: Cupping scoresheet has NO save path — the core S6 capability is unreachable through the UI
findings.location: whole component, esp. lines 44-182 (no <form>/submit), 53-54 (cupperId/isCalibration state), 33-34 (doc comment)
findings.problem: The scoresheet is the make-quality capstone's primary interactive surface, but it renders no Save/Record/Submit affordance and imports neither recordCuppingSessionAction nor recordCupScoreAction. The cupper picks who cupped (cupperId state, line 53), ticks 'Calibration sample' (isCalibration state, line 54), drags all 8/10 sliders, and watches the live total — then there is literally no way to persist any of it. cupperId and isCalibration are bound to inputs but never read by any write path (grep confirms they are only ever assigned/displayed). The component's own doc comment (lines 33-34) claims 'Persisting a session/score routes through the recordCuppingSession + recordCupScore Server Actions; this island is the capture surface' — but it does not. The scoresheet test (cupping-scoresheet.test.tsx lines 6-10) even mocks those two actions, implying a save was intended, yet none is wired. Result: cupping — the feature that defines the cup for a Best-of-Panama Volcán Geisha farm — cannot be recorded from the app at all. v_cup_final_score, v_qc_status 'latest cup', and v_cupper_drift will stay empty forever because no session/score is ever inserted via the UI.
findings.scenario: Open /qc/cup/JC-9001. Select cupper 'Diego', tick 'Calibration sample', score all 8 CVA attributes, see live total 78. There is no button to save. Reload — the cup is gone; /qc 'Latest cup' stays '—' and the cupper-drift card stays in its empty state permanently.
findings.fix: Add a submit flow to CuppingScoresheet: a primary 'Record cup' button inside a <form action={...}> driven by useActionState over recordCuppingSessionAction, then append each attribute score via recordCupScoreAction (or a single batched action), passing cupperId, protocol, isCalibration, and the scores map. Surface success/error state (role=status/alert) and reset on success. Until then, the slice's headline capability is non-functional.

findings.title: Live quality-band badge is wrong on the DEFAULT SCA CVA protocol — even a perfect cup never reaches Specialty/Presidential
findings.location: line 66 (band = cupQualityBand(total)) + lines 175-177 (Badge); thresholds in src/lib/ui/cva-scoring.ts:69-74
findings.problem: The scoresheet defaults to protocol 'sca-cva' (line 51). The SCA CVA affective scale here is 8 attributes x 0-10 = max 80 (CVA_ATTRIBUTES, cva-scoring.ts:21-30; cupFinalScore is a raw sum). But cupQualityBand uses the legacy 100-pt thresholds: Presidential >=90, Specialty >=85, Premium >=80 (cva-scoring.ts:69-73). So on the default CVA protocol the live Badge can NEVER show 'Specialty' or 'Presidential' — a flawless CVA cup (all 10s = 80, proven by cva-scoring.test.ts:51-54) shows 'Premium', and every realistic Volcán Geisha CVA cup (~60-79) shows 'Below Specialty'. The badge — the 'where the cup lands' signal the doc promises (lines 27-28) — is misleading on the protocol the form opens in. The real SCA CVA 2023 affective scoresheet maps to a 58-100 cupping score via a formula, not a raw 0-80 sum, so the band thresholds and the CVA total are on incompatible scales.
findings.scenario: Cup a top Geisha on the default SCA CVA sheet: fragrance 9, flavor 9, aftertaste 9, acidity 9, sweetness 9, mouthfeel 9, overall 9, uniformity 9 -> live total 72 -> Badge reads 'Below Specialty' in a near-invisible neutral tone. The family's flagship lot is labelled sub-specialty on screen.
findings.fix: Either (a) band the CVA total against a CVA-appropriate scale (or convert the raw sum to the 58-100 SCA CVA score before banding), or (b) make cupQualityBand protocol-aware so CVA and legacy each band on their own range. At minimum, do not show legacy 80/85/90 thresholds against an 80-max CVA total.

findings.title: Cupper-drift card shows raw worker IDs (e.g. 'w-03'), not cupper names
findings.location: line 69 ({d.cupperId}); data source src/app/(app)/qc/page.tsx:22-23, 34
findings.problem: CupperDriftCard renders d.cupperId verbatim as the human-facing identity (line 69, font-mono). cupper_id is a worker code like 'w-03' / 'w-cup-2' (workers PK, migration line 44 'cupper_id text references workers(id)'; workers.ts ids are 'w-01'..). The /qc page (page.tsx:22-23) fetches only getCupperDrift() and never joins worker names, so the calibration-bias evidence — the thing used to weigh a scarce-lot decision — labels each cupper as an opaque code. The cup page DOES pass real names to the scoresheet (cup/[lot]/page.tsx:42), proving names are available; the drift card just doesn't get them. For a ~90% Ngäbe-Buglé crew this is exactly the surface where a human-readable name matters.
findings.scenario: Open /qc with two calibration cuppers. The drift card lists 'w-03  +2 on acidity' and 'w-05  −1 on acidity'. The owner cannot tell which person over-scores acidity without cross-referencing the worker roster by ID.
findings.fix: Join worker names into the drift data: either extend v_cupper_drift to expose the cupper name, or in QcPage map cupperId -> name via getWorkers() (already used elsewhere) and pass a display name into CupperDriftCard. Render the name with the ID as secondary.

findings.title: Release-hold button has no error surface — a failed release silently does nothing
findings.location: ReleaseButton, lines 56-76
findings.problem: ReleaseButton reads state from useActionState but only ever renders pending/success label changes (line 72). It never renders state.message or state.errors on failure. releaseQcHoldAction CAN return {status:'error', message} (actions.ts:69-78, holdToState -> error with message). If a release fails (lot not found, no open hold, RPC/permission error), the button flips from 'Releasing…' back to 'Release' with zero feedback — the user thinks they clicked wrong, retries, and the lot stays held with no explanation. The place-hold drawer, by contrast, renders an error region (lines 197-205); the release path has no equivalent.
findings.scenario: Click 'Release' on a held lot whose release RPC errors (e.g. transient failure). Button shows 'Releasing…' then 'Release' again; no toast, no message. The lot remains un-sellable and the operator has no idea why.
findings.fix: In ReleaseButton, render state.message when state.status==='error' (a role=alert inline near the button or a small toast), mirroring the drawer's error region. Reset to allow retry.

findings.title: QC-hold drawer claims aria-modal but has no focus management (no initial focus, no Tab trap, no focus restore)
findings.location: HoldPanel, lines 115-210 (role=dialog aria-modal=true) vs shared Dialog src/components/ui/dialog.tsx:54-102
findings.problem: HoldPanel declares role='dialog' aria-modal='true' (lines 116-121) but rolls its own portal and implements none of the modal a11y the shared Dialog provides: no initial focus into the drawer, no Tab/Shift+Tab focus trap, no focus restore on close. The shared Dialog (dialog.tsx:54-102) does all three. With aria-modal='true', a screen-reader/keyboard user is promised the background is inert, but Tab walks straight out of the drawer into the page behind the blurred backdrop, and on close focus is lost (not returned to the 'Hold' trigger). The drawer's own doc comment (lines 25-27) cites 'the Phase-1 drawer fix' for stacking context but reuses none of Dialog's focus logic. (Note: the sibling reservation-drawer and audit-drawer share this omission, so it is an app-wide side-drawer gap, not S6-only — but S6 introduces a NEW aria-modal drawer that inherits it.)
findings.scenario: Keyboard user opens the Hold drawer on /qc, presses Tab past the reason field and the buttons — focus leaves the modal and lands on the page's Cup links behind the backdrop; pressing Enter activates page content the modal was supposed to block. On Escape, focus is dropped rather than returned to the Hold button.
findings.fix: Use the shared Dialog (dialog.tsx) as the drawer container, or factor its focus-trap/initial-focus/restore logic into a shared hook and apply it here. At minimum: focus the reason input on mount, trap Tab within the panel, and restore focus to the trigger on close — otherwise drop aria-modal='true'.

findings.title: Live total updates silently for screen-reader users; protocol toggle is mislabeled 'View mode'
findings.location: live total lines 162-178 (no aria-live); Segmented group label src/components/ui/segmented.tsx:21
findings.problem: Two a11y polish gaps on the scoresheet. (1) The 'Live total' value (lines 168-173) is the central running feedback as sliders move, but it carries no aria-live/role=status, so a screen-reader user dragging a slider gets no announcement of the new total or band — the headline affordance is silent for non-sighted users. (2) The protocol toggle reuses <Segmented>, whose hardcoded group aria-label is 'View mode' (segmented.tsx:21). Here it switches the cupping protocol (SCA CVA vs legacy 100-pt), so a screen reader announces the scoring-protocol control as a generic 'View mode' group — a confusing, wrong label for the one control that changes which attributes get scored.
findings.scenario: A low-vision cupper using VoiceOver focuses the protocol toggle and hears 'View mode, group' instead of 'Cupping protocol'. They then move a slider and hear no total/band update — they cannot tell where the cup landed.
findings.fix: (1) Wrap the live total in an aria-live='polite' region (or add role='status') so total+band changes announce. (2) Add an aria-label prop to Segmented (default 'View mode') and pass aria-label='Cupping protocol' from the scoresheet.

findings.title: The cupping scoresheet captures NOTHING — no submit, no Server Action call; the dogfood score evaporates and the append-only cup ledger has no UI write path
findings.location: whole component (lines 44-182); imports at lines 3-20; comment at lines 33-34
findings.problem: CuppingScoresheet is the slice's headline capture surface, but it is a read-only preview. It imports only useState/useMemo, the pure cva-scoring math, and UI primitives (lines 3-20) — it imports NO Server Action, has NO <form>, NO submit button, and NO call to recordCuppingSession/recordCupScore. Scores live only in local React state (line 52) and are discarded on navigation. The fully-built Server Actions recordCuppingSessionAction and recordCupScoreAction (qc/actions.ts lines 80-116) and their command ports (recordCuppingSession.ts, recordCupScore.ts) are ORPHANED — grep shows their only callers are qc/actions.ts itself and two __tests__ files; no production component invokes them. The scoresheet's own test (cupping-scoresheet.test.tsx lines 6-10) mocks the actions but never asserts a save, because there is no save. Consequently cupping_sessions and cupping_scores can never receive a row through the app, v_cup_final_score / v_qc_status.latest_cup_score / v_cupper_drift are permanently empty, and the route is /qc/cup/[lot] (a stateless lot-keyed sheet) instead of the spec's /qc/cup/[session] — no session is ever opened. The whole append-only cup-score ledger, the cupper-drift evidence layer, and the score side of the cup-to-cause loop are unreachable from the UI.
findings.scenario: The family opens /qc, clicks 'Cup' on a finished Geisha micro-lot, scores all eight attributes, watches the live total land at the band shown — then navigates away (or the next person opens the lot). The score is gone: nothing was written to cupping_sessions/cupping_scores. The QC table's 'latest score' column stays blank forever, cupper-drift never accumulates a single sample, and the dogfood 'sees an 89.5' is a transient client number that no one else, and no report, will ever see.
findings.fix: Wire the scoresheet to persist: add a 'Save session' submit that calls recordCuppingSessionAction (with lotCode, cupperId, protocol, isCalibration) to open a session, then fan the per-attribute scores through recordCupScoreAction (one append per attribute) against the returned sessionId; use useActionState like qc-hold-control.tsx does. Rename the route to /qc/cup/[session] (or open the session on entry and pass its id) so an actual session row backs the sheet. Add a test that asserts the action is invoked with the scored attributes (red first). Until persisted, the slice's central capability — cup scores as evidence bound to a lot forever — does not exist in the running app.

findings.title: SCA CVA 2023 final-score math is wrong: it is a plain additive sum, not the affective transform — a perfect CVA cup maxes at 80, so 'Specialty'/'Presidential' are unreachable and the 89.5 dogfood number is impossible on the CVA protocol
findings.location: cupFinalScore lines 57-62; CVA_ATTRIBUTES lines 21-30; cupQualityBand lines 69-74; enshrined by test cva-scoring.test.ts lines 51-54
findings.problem: The spec names the SCA CVA scoring math as the slice's HIGHEST RISK and mandates it be 'protocol-correct' and 'exhaustively-tested against published CVA worked examples' before any UI. The real SCA CVA 2023 cup-form final score is an affine transform: Score = 0.65625 x Sum(8 section scores, each 1-9) + 52.75 (minus 2/non-uniform and 4/defective cup), giving a 0-100 scale where a flawless cup = 100. The build's cupFinalScore just returns Sum(scores) with each attribute 0-10 (lines 57-62), so the maximum possible CVA total is 80 (test line 51-54 even asserts 'a perfect CVA card ... totals 80'). cupQualityBand (lines 69-74) bands Presidential >=90 and Specialty >=85 — both arithmetically UNREACHABLE on the CVA protocol (max 80 always bands 'Premium'). The DB view v_cup_final_score is also a raw additive sum (migration lines 234-245), so server and client agree on the wrong number. The slice's own dogfood claim ('sees an 89.5' on the SCA CVA form) is impossible: 89.5 cannot be produced by summing 8 attributes each capped at 10.
findings.scenario: A cupper scores a Best-of-Panama Geisha on the SCA CVA 2023 sheet, all attributes 9-10. The live total reads ~78-80 and the band badge says 'Premium' — under-grading a world-class lot by ~20 points and mislabeling it below specialty, the exact subtle error the spec warned about. Any external buyer or competition entry that expects a real CVA score gets a number ~20 points low.
findings.fix: Implement the real CVA 2023 affective formula in cupFinalScore for protocol 'sca-cva' (0.65625 * sum + 52.75, with the per-cup non-uniform/defective deductions exposed as inputs), keep legacy-100 as its own transform (legacy SCA is base 36 + sum of 10 attrs, also not a naive sum), and update v_cup_final_score to compute the same transform so server/client parity holds on the CORRECT number. Re-derive cupQualityBand against the true 0-100 scale. Add tests against published CVA worked examples (the spec's explicit de-risk), replacing the 'perfect = 80' assertion. This is the 'durable asset must be provably correct' requirement, currently unmet.

findings.title: Cup-to-cause panel shows none of the ferment curve, drying curve, or plot/elevation the spec's dogfood moment requires — its 'sibling slices haven't shipped' excuse is false now that S3 and S4 are on main
findings.location: panel body lines 52-151; data fetch in qc/cup/[lot]/page.tsx lines 35-39 (only getLotGenealogy); component comment lines 22-26
findings.problem: The spec's dogfood moment is explicit: beside the score the cupper 'sees the exact ferment curve and drying curve and the 1,650 masl plot that produced it — the cup-to-cause loop closed.' The built panel shows only: variety, a stage chain (lot codes + kg), and primary/secondary defect tallies. It surfaces NO ferment data, NO drying data, and NO plot/elevation. The component justifies this (lines 24-26) by claiming 'Ferment curves and drying curves ship in sibling slices (S3/S4)' — but S3 (20260622092000_fermentation.sql, which ships v_ferment_curve / v_ferment_cutpoint / v_water_per_kg) and S4 (20260622094000_drying_reposo.sql, which ships moisture_readings + v_reposo_status) are BOTH integrated on main, and Phase-1 already exposes plots.altitude_masl reachable via harvests->plot. The page (page.tsx lines 35-39) only awaits getLotGenealogy and getQcStatus — it never queries the ferment curve, the drying/moisture curve, or the plot elevation, none of which the LotNode type carries (types.ts lines 181-189 have no plot/elevation field). The available cause data exists and is left entirely on the table.
findings.scenario: The family cups a finished Geisha and looks to the right to see WHY it tastes how it does. They get a variety chip, a list of lot codes with weights, and defect counts — but no ferment pH/temp curve, no drying/moisture curve, and no '1,650 masl' plot. The single most-quoted dogfood sentence of the slice ('right beside the score sees the exact ferment curve and drying curve and the 1,650 masl plot') is not achievable, despite all the source data being present on main.
findings.fix: In qc/cup/[lot]/page.tsx, also fetch the lot's ferment curve (v_ferment_curve), drying/moisture curve (moisture_readings / v_reposo_status), and the originating plot's altitude_masl (via the harvest->plot link), each with .catch graceful-degrade. Render them in CupToCausePanel: a small ferment-pH sparkline, a moisture-decline sparkline, and a 'Plot Las Lagunas - 1,650 masl' line. Keep the empty-state per-section so a genuinely-absent stage still degrades honestly. Add a render test asserting the elevation and curve sections appear when data is present. Only then is the cup-to-cause loop the spec describes actually closed.

findings.title: Defect grading has no command port and no UI — record_defect RPC and green_defects ledger are write-unreachable, so the QC 'real defect input' that feeds sca_grade can never be entered
findings.location: missing recordDefect.ts (only recordCuppingSession.ts, recordCupScore.ts, placeQcHold.ts exist); migration record_defect RPC lines 378-407; spec lists record_defects as a Command RPC
findings.problem: The spec promises defect grading as a first-class capability: a defect_assessments/green_defects ledger that 'feeds the Phase-1 generated green_lots.sca_grade ... QC now supplies the real defect input', with record_defects(lot, ...) listed among the Command RPCs. The migration delivers the table green_defects (lines 82-95) and the RPC record_defect (lines 378-407), and the read port getGreenDefects exists (qc.ts lines 164-172). But there is NO recordDefect.ts command port (ls of src/lib/db/commands shows only recordCuppingSession, recordCupScore, placeQcHold), NO Server Action for defects (qc/actions.ts has place/release/session/score only), and NO UI to enter a defect — grep for record_defect/recordDefect/green_defects writes in src/ outside tests/types returns nothing. The cup-to-cause panel and v_qc_status read defect tallies that can only ever be 0/0 because no app path can append a green_defects row.
findings.scenario: A grader finds quakers and full-black defects in a green lot. There is nowhere in the app to record them — no defect form, no action, no command. green_defects stays empty, v_qc_status shows 0 primary / 0 secondary on every lot, and the 'green-grading defects' box in the cup-to-cause panel is permanently zero. The defect-driven half of the make-quality story (and the input the family is told supplements sca_grade) does not exist in the running product.
findings.fix: Add src/lib/db/commands/recordDefect.ts (mirroring recordCupScore.ts: validate, build the RPC envelope, call record_defect), a recordDefectAction in qc/actions.ts, and a defect-entry UI (a small append-only grid with kind/count/category on the cupping page or the QC table row). Write the failing command test first. Then green_defects is reachable and the defect tallies the panel/v_qc_status already read become real.

findings.title: Defense-in-depth is a lie the tests can't detect: deleting the in-RPC reposo precondition (layer 1) leaves ALL three RPC-level gate tests green
findings.location: lines 69-90 (RPC RAISES too-wet), 92-107 (RPC RAISES too-fresh), 109-126 (RPC SUCCEEDS); vs migration 20260622094000_drying_reposo.sql:439-491 (layer 1 in-RPC precondition + layer 2 trigger)
findings.problem: The migration's load-bearing claim (header lines 6-13) is that the gate is enforced in TWO independent layers 'so it can't be bypassed': (1) a precondition INSIDE advance_processing_stage and (2) a BEFORE-UPDATE trigger backstop on lots. But the RPC's own mutation is `update lots set stage='milled'` (sql:451-454), which itself fires the layer-2 trigger. So every RPC-level reposo test asserts only `rejects.toThrow(/reposo gate/i)` — a message BOTH layers emit verbatim (sql:445 and sql:480). I proved with a throwaway PGlite repro (now deleted): redefining advance_processing_stage with the layer-1 reposo block REMOVED but the trigger KEPT, the line-69 scenario still throws /reposo gate/, still writes zero lot_event (ev_count=0), and still leaves stage='drying' — i.e. all three tests stay GREEN. Layer 1 is NOT independently pinned by any test. A future refactor that drops the in-RPC precondition (the documented 'ONLY addition', easy to lose in a create-or-replace) would silently reduce the system to a single layer with a fully green suite, defeating the entire 'two layers' design intent and removing the in-transaction early-exit before the mass/event work.
findings.scenario: Someone refactors advance_processing_stage in a later slice (e.g. S6 QC adds a new precondition) and accidentally drops the reposo block from the RPC body. Suite is green. The trigger still catches direct bypasses, but the RPC no longer fails fast and the claimed redundancy is gone — and nobody knows because no test isolates the two layers.
findings.fix: Add a test that drops/disables the lots_enforce_reposo_gate trigger (e.g. `alter table lots disable trigger lots_enforce_reposo_gate`) and then asserts the RPC STILL blocks a wet drying->milled advance — pinning layer 1 alone. Symmetrically, the existing direct-UPDATE test (line 141) already pins layer 2 in isolation (it bypasses the RPC). With both isolation tests present, removing either layer turns the suite red.

findings.title: Out-of-order / backdated wet reading silently bypasses the reposo gate — ready=true with a 13% reading on record (zero coverage)
findings.location: reposo_status sql:217-224 (the `order by mr.occurred_at desc, mr.id desc limit window` recent-window) vs test file: NO test inserts readings out of occurred_at order
findings.problem: moisture_stable is computed over the most-recent `reposo_stable_window` readings strictly by `occurred_at desc, id desc`. A reading whose occurred_at is stamped in the PAST (a backdated correction, a hygrometer with a wrong clock, a re-wet reading taken after rain but mis-entered, or the offline Lamport-replay arriving with an old field timestamp) is dropped from the window even though it is the physically newest evidence. I proved with a deleted PGlite repro: a lot with two in-band readings (11.0, 11.0) plus a 13.0% reading whose occurred_at was 10 days in the past yields `ready=true, latest_moisture=11` — the panic re-wet reading is invisible to the gate, and the lot is cleared to mill at 13% moisture (cup-ruining for a Best-of-Panama Geisha, and a real money/quality invariant). No test exercises any out-of-order or backdated reading; every test inserts readings in ascending occurred_at order, so the ordering logic is never stressed and this reachable bypass has no regression net.
findings.scenario: Drying yard, rain hits; tech records a 13% re-wet reading but the tablet's offline queue replays it with the field timestamp from the morning batch (occurred_at earlier than the two stable afternoon readings). v_reposo_status shows ready=true, the UI mill button enables, the lot is milled wet.
findings.fix: Add a DB test: seed two in-band readings, then record a high (e.g. 13.8%) reading with an occurred_at EARLIER than them, and assert the lot is STILL ready (documenting the by-occurred_at semantics) — OR, better, assert it is NOT ready and change reposo_status to also consider the highest moisture in the last N physical readings (order by recorded_at, or guard `latest > max_band` regardless of window). Either way the behavior must be pinned by a test; today it is undefined-by-omission.

findings.title: The exact moisture-band boundaries (10.5% / 11.5%) are never tested — the inclusive `between` is unpinned
findings.location: tests use 13.8 (line 73), 11.0-11.3 (lines 112-114), 14.0 (line 143) — never 10.5 or 11.5; migration sql:224 `between cfg.reposo_moisture_min_pct and cfg.reposo_moisture_max_pct`
findings.problem: The prompt explicitly calls out 'a reading right at the boundary' as a CRIT-focus probe, and the band is the gate's whole definition. The stability check uses SQL `between` (inclusive on both ends). No test asserts what happens AT 10.5 or 11.5 (the most off-by-one-prone values). I confirmed via a deleted repro that 11.5 and 10.5 both yield ready=true (inclusive) — but that contract is enforced only by an un-asserted `between`. If someone 'tightens' the gate to `> min and < max` (a plausible, well-intentioned edit to exclude the exact ceiling), a lot reading exactly 11.5% would flip from millable to blocked (or vice-versa) and the entire suite stays green.
findings.scenario: A future tweak changes `between` to exclusive bounds, or shifts to `<=`/`<`. Lots sitting exactly on a band edge silently change gate behavior; no test catches the regression.
findings.fix: Add two boundary tests: (a) all window readings exactly at 11.5 (upper edge) -> moisture_stable true / ready true given rest met; (b) a reading at 11.51 (just over) -> moisture_stable false / blocked. Symmetrically pin 10.5 and 10.49. This converts the inclusive-band contract from accidental to asserted.

findings.title: The `reposo_stable_window` requirement (need >= N in-band recent readings) is never tested — a single in-band reading's fail-closed behavior is unpinned
findings.location: migration sql:226 `m_stable := (cnt >= cfg.reposo_stable_window and in_band_cnt >= cfg.reposo_stable_window)` (default window=2, sql:38); every passing test records 3 readings, every blocking test relies on out-of-band/too-fresh, never on too-FEW readings
findings.problem: moisture_stable requires at least `reposo_stable_window` (default 2) total readings AND that many recent in-band readings. The 'one perfect reading is not enough rest evidence' rule is a core part of the stability contract, but no test records exactly ONE in-band reading and asserts the lot is NOT yet ready. I confirmed via deleted repro: 1 reading at 11.0 with window=2 yields ready=false, moisture_stable=false (correct fail-closed) — but nothing pins it. If the guard degraded to `cnt >= 1` or the second `in_band_cnt` clause were dropped, a lot could be cleared to mill on a single lucky in-band reading with zero test failure.
findings.scenario: A refactor simplifies the stability predicate to 'latest reading in band'. A lot with one in-band reading and no trend now passes the gate; suite stays green; the reposo discipline that defines the cup is gone.
findings.fix: Add a test: seed a fully-rested lot (rest_met true) with exactly ONE in-band reading and assert advance drying->milled RAISES /reposo gate/ and v_reposo_status.ready=false; then add the second in-band reading and assert it succeeds. This pins the window>=N contract.

findings.title: prevent_overcapacity's UPDATE branch (`tg_op = 'UPDATE'` / re-opening a released assignment) is entirely uncovered
findings.location: overcapacity test only covers INSERT (lines 269-280, via assign_drying_station); migration sql:118-120 fires the trigger `before insert OR UPDATE`, and sql:108 has a UPDATE-specific self-exclusion `not (tg_op = 'UPDATE' and id = new.id)`
findings.problem: The capacity trigger fires on UPDATE too, and contains UPDATE-specific logic (excluding the row's own prior committed_kg when recomputing the sum so a no-op/partial update doesn't double-count). The only capacity test commits a too-big lot via INSERT. The UPDATE path — reactivating a released assignment (`released_at = null`) or mutating committed_kg on an open row beyond capacity — is never exercised, so the `tg_op='UPDATE'` self-exclusion clause (a classic off-by-self bug surface) has no test. I proved via deleted repro that re-opening a released 70kg row into an already-70kg-committed st-small (cap 80) correctly raises — but if that self-exclusion were wrong (e.g. counted the row twice, or excluded it on INSERT), a legitimate move could be falsely blocked or an over-commit allowed, with a green suite.
findings.scenario: Reposo workflow moves a lot off a station then back (release then re-open via UPDATE), or corrects committed_kg. The UPDATE-path capacity math decides whether the station over-commits. No test guards it.
findings.fix: Add a test that UPDATEs an existing open assignment's committed_kg to exceed capacity (expect raise) AND one that UPDATEs it to a still-fitting value (expect success, proving the self-exclusion doesn't false-positive), plus a release-then-reopen-beyond-cap case.

findings.title: record_moisture_reading's side effect (the processing_batches.moisture_pct mirror UPDATE) has no test
findings.location: migration sql:334-336 `update processing_batches set moisture_pct = p_moisture_pct where lot_code = p_lot_code;` — no test in the suite reads processing_batches.moisture_pct after a reading
findings.problem: record_moisture_reading writes a second, non-append-only side effect: it mutates processing_batches.moisture_pct (a flat Phase-1 column) so old reads see the latest value. This UPDATE is unconditional and un-idempotent-aware (it runs even on the on-conflict-do-nothing replay path? — actually it runs after, so a replay re-UPDATEs the flat column to the SAME row but a backdated/out-of-order replay would clobber the flat column with an OLDER value). No test asserts the mirror happens, asserts it targets the right batch, or asserts a replay/out-of-order reading doesn't regress the flat column to a stale value.
