Because the reposo gate reads moisture_readings (not this column) the corruption is silent in the gate but the Phase-1 UI surfaces the flat column. Untested write path that crosses into a Phase-1 table is exactly the kind of regression the slice's tests should fence.
findings.scenario: Two readings arrive out of order (offline replay): the older one's RPC call runs last and stamps processing_batches.moisture_pct back to the older value; Phase-1 screens show stale moisture. No test catches it.
findings.fix: Add a test asserting after record_moisture_reading the matching processing_batches row's moisture_pct equals the reading, AND a test that a later-recorded older-occurred_at reading does not regress the flat mirror (or assert/define the chosen semantics). At minimum pin that the mirror fires for the correct lot.

findings.title: Missing farm_season_config row makes reposo_status emit a misleading 'awaiting moisture readings' reason while fail-closing — undocumented, untested edge
findings.location: reposo_status sql:204-226 reads cfg from farm_season_config where id=1; if cfg is NULL the predicates evaluate to NULL -> ready NULL; gate uses coalesce(st.ready,false) at sql:444 and sql:479
findings.problem: If the singleton config row (id=1) is ever absent, reposo_status returns ready=NULL and reason='awaiting moisture readings' even for a lot WITH in-band readings (proven via deleted repro: 2 in-band readings + deleted config -> ready=null, reason='awaiting moisture readings'). The gate fails CLOSED (correct, via coalesce), but the reason text is actively misleading (it claims no readings when there are) and no test asserts the fail-closed behavior under missing config. The whole gate's thresholds hinge on this one row existing; that dependency is unguarded by any test.
findings.scenario: A future migration or reseed transiently drops/replaces the config singleton; reposo_status silently reports a wrong reason and the gate's fail-closed-ness is unverified.
findings.fix: Add a test that deletes farm_season_config id=1 and asserts (a) the drying->milled advance still RAISES (fail-closed) and (b) ready is false/null — pinning that the gate never opens without config. Optionally fix the reason branch to detect null cfg and say 'reposo config missing'.

findings.title: Out-of-order / backdated wet reading silently bypasses the reposo gate — ready=true with a 13% reading on record (zero coverage)
findings.location: reposo_status sql:217-224 (the `order by mr.occurred_at desc, mr.id desc limit window` recent-window) vs test file p2s4_drying_reposo.db.test.ts: NO test inserts readings out of occurred_at order
findings.problem: moisture_stable is computed over the most-recent `reposo_stable_window` readings strictly by `occurred_at desc, id desc`. A reading whose occurred_at is stamped in the PAST (a backdated correction, a hygrometer with a wrong clock, a re-wet reading taken after rain but mis-entered, or the offline Lamport-replay arriving with an old field timestamp) is dropped from the window even though it is the physically newest evidence. I proved with a deleted PGlite repro: a lot with two in-band readings (11.0, 11.0) plus a 13.0% reading whose occurred_at was 10 days in the past yields `ready=true, latest_moisture=11` — the panic re-wet reading is invisible to the gate, and the lot is cleared to mill at 13% moisture (cup-ruining for a Best-of-Panama Geisha, a real money/quality invariant). No test exercises any out-of-order or backdated reading; every test inserts readings in ascending occurred_at order, so the ordering logic is never stressed and this reachable bypass has no regression net.
findings.scenario: Drying yard, rain hits; tech records a 13% re-wet reading but the tablet's offline queue replays it with the field timestamp from the morning batch (occurred_at earlier than the two stable afternoon readings). v_reposo_status shows ready=true, the UI mill button enables, the lot is milled wet.
findings.fix: Add a DB test: seed two in-band readings, then record a high (e.g. 13.8%) reading with an occurred_at EARLIER than them, and assert the lot is STILL ready (documenting the by-occurred_at semantics) — OR, better, assert it is NOT ready and change reposo_status to also consider the max moisture in the last N physical readings (order by recorded_at, or guard `latest > max_band` regardless of window). Either way the behavior must be pinned by a test; today it is undefined-by-omission.

findings.location: migration sql:226 `m_stable := (cnt >= cfg.reposo_stable_window and in_band_cnt >= cfg.reposo_stable_window)` (default window=2, sql:38); every passing test records 3 readings, every blocking test relies on out-of-band/too-fresh, never on too-FEW readings

findings.title: record_moisture_reading's processing_batches.moisture_pct mirror side effect has no test
findings.location: migration sql:334-336 `update processing_batches set moisture_pct = p_moisture_pct where lot_code = p_lot_code;` — no test in the suite reads processing_batches.moisture_pct after a reading

This UPDATE is unconditional and runs even after the idempotent on-conflict path, and it ignores ordering — a backdated/out-of-order reading clobbers the flat column with an OLDER value. No test asserts the mirror happens, asserts it targets the right batch, or asserts a replay/out-of-order reading doesn't regress the flat column to a stale value. At minimum pin that the mirror fires for the correct lot.

findings.title: Service Worker caches authenticated Supabase REST data into persistent, shared Cache Storage that survives sign-out — cross-user data leak on shared field devices
findings.location: public/sw.js:96-109 (networkFirst), :41-46 (chooseStrategy/isApiOrAuth); src/components/auth/sign-out-button.tsx:13-18
findings.problem: `chooseStrategy` routes every GET to `https://<ref>.supabase.co/rest/v1/...` to `network-first`, and `networkFirst()` does `cache.put(request, response.clone())` for any `response.ok && method==='GET'` (sw.js:99-101). The cache key is the *request URL only* — the per-user bearer token rides in the `Authorization` header, not the URL — so the cached JSON body of one user's authenticated farm-data reads lands in the persistent `janson-v1-runtime` Cache Storage bucket. `signOut()` (sign-out-button.tsx) clears the auth cookie but never purges Cache Storage, and `activate` only deletes caches that don't match the *current* version, so the runtime cache is preserved across sign-out. Two concrete reachable leaks on this farm's shared tablets/phones (the ~90% Ngäbe-Buglé migrant crew share devices): (1) User B signs in on a device User A used; while offline, any GET that `fetch` rejects falls to `networkFirst`'s catch branch and serves User A's cached `/rest/v1/` body to User B (cache key matches because URLs are identical). (2) Any script on origin — or a logged-out person — can read the cached bodies directly via `caches.match()` / `caches.open('janson-v1-runtime')` with no auth check at all. This is reachable today: the SW is registered from the root layout (layout.tsx:50) and caching fires on the first authenticated page that reads Supabase.
findings.scenario: Field supervisor signs in on the shared harvest tablet, opens the lots/harvest dashboard (REST GETs cached). Signs out, hands the tablet to a picker. Picker (or a curious bystander) opens DevTools→Application→Cache Storage, or simply loads the app offline — User A's lot/cost/EUDR data is served from cache despite no valid session.
findings.fix: Do NOT write Supabase REST responses to a shared persistent cache. Either (a) drop the `cache.put` in `networkFirst` for `.supabase.co`/`/rest/v1/` and `/auth/` requests entirely (live data should be network-only-with-no-fallback, or fall back to an explicitly per-session-scoped cache), or (b) on sign-out, call a SW message that runs `caches.delete('janson-v1-runtime')` (add a `CLEAR_DATA_CACHE` handler alongside `SKIP_WAITING` and post it from `SignOutButton.signOut()` before `auth.signOut()`). Add a test asserting an authenticated `/rest/v1/` GET is not persisted to a cross-session cache.

findings.title: Deploy cache-bust invariant depends on a hardcoded `CACHE_VERSION` literal that nothing bumps — the stale-shell purge silently fails when a human forgets
findings.location: public/sw.js:16-18, :57-71 (activate purge); contradicts the file header at :12-13 and sw-strategy.ts:10-14
findings.problem: The SW header comment claims "The cache version is bumped per release so a new deploy purges the old shell on activate" (sw.js:12-13), and the whole deploy cache-bust invariant (the slice's stated SW requirement: "must cache-bust on deploy") hangs on `activate` deleting every cache whose key doesn't start with `CACHE_VERSION` (sw.js:65). But `CACHE_VERSION = "janson-v1"` is a frozen string literal (sw.js:16) — there is no build-time injection: `grep` of next.config.mjs/scripts finds zero references and no `generateBuildId`/`__BUILD_ID__` substitution into `public/sw.js`. So unless a human manually edits `janson-v1` → `janson-v2` on every release, the byte-identical SW won't even re-activate (no update event), the precache (`/`, `/manifest.webmanifest`) stays frozen, and the purge filter (`!k.startsWith('janson-v1')`) keeps the same-named caches forever. This is exactly the "stale chunk → white screen" footgun the slice says it defends against — left to manual discipline with no guard.
findings.scenario: A new build ships (new `_next/static` hashes, updated shell). Nobody edits the version string. Browsers keep the old `/sw.js` (HTTP-cached up to 24h) and the precached `/` shell; `activate` never re-runs against a new version so nothing is purged. Field users on the old shell see stale navigations / a white screen until the 24h SW byte-check eventually re-fetches — defeating the invariant.
findings.fix: Tie `CACHE_VERSION` to the build: inject Next's `generateBuildId` (or a content hash / commit SHA) into `public/sw.js` at build time (a tiny prebuild script or serve `sw.js` from a route handler that templates the id), so each deploy produces a new version string automatically. Add a build-step/test guard that fails if the SW version equals the previous release's. At minimum, document the manual bump as a release checklist item and add a test that the version constant is read from an env/build value, not a literal.

findings.title: Dead-letter "Retry" (and the manual sync action) revives an entry to queued but never re-sends it when already online — recovery is a silent no-op
findings.location: src/lib/offline/useSyncStatus.ts:58-60; src/lib/offline/sync.ts:110-124 (refresh) vs :126-143 (drain), :145-160 (drain only fired by online event/start); src/components/layout/sync-status-island.tsx:157-160 (Retry button)
findings.problem: The outbox drawer's "Retry" button (sync-status-island.tsx:157) calls `useSyncStatus.retry` = `outbox.retry(uuid).then(() => engine.refresh())` (useSyncStatus.ts:58). `outbox.retry` correctly moves the dead-letter back to `queued`, but the follow-up `engine.refresh()` only recomputes counts (sync.ts:110-124) — it never calls `outbox.flush()` / `drain()`. `drain()` (the only path that actually re-sends) is invoked solely by the `online` window event and by `start()` (sync.ts:145-160). So when the operator is already online (the normal case — they fixed the cause of a QC-hold/reposo/oversell rejection and tap Retry), the entry just flips from "failed" to "pending" and sits there indefinitely; no send happens until connectivity actually toggles offline→online. The `flush` exposed on the API (useSyncStatus.ts:60) is likewise wired to `engine.refresh()`, not a real flush — so there is no working manual "sync now". This breaks the slice's dead-letter recovery story (a business rejection is supposed to be fixable and re-driven), and it does so invisibly: the count badge says "pending", implying progress that never comes.
findings.scenario: A weigh-in dead-letters on a reposo-gate rejection. The operator corrects the underlying record, opens the drawer (online), taps Retry. The entry moves to the "Waiting to sync" list and stays there forever; nothing is sent. The picker believes the capture will sync, but it only would if the device later loses and regains signal.
findings.fix: After `outbox.retry(uuid)`, trigger an actual drain: add a `drain()`/`flush()` method to the SyncEngine surface and call it from `useSyncStatus.retry` (and wire the `flush` API to it too), guarded by `isOnline()`. Add a test: enqueue → transport rejects (dead) → retry while online → assert the transport is invoked again and the entry reaches `done`/`queued`-then-sent, not left pending.

findings.title: Reposo gate is fully bypassable via the `parchment` stage — the slice's load-bearing 'impossible to skip' invariant is defeated by the normal forward path
findings.location: lines 442 (RPC gate) and 477 (trigger backstop); batch_stage enum at 20260620120000_init.sql:17
findings.problem: The gate fires ONLY on the exact transition cur_stage='drying' AND p_to_stage='milled' (RPC line 442; trigger line 477). But the batch_stage enum is ('cherry','fermentation','drying','parchment','milled','green') — there is a `parchment` stage BETWEEN drying and milled, and parchment IS the reposo state (coffee rests as pergamino/parchment after drying, before milling — the exact rest this gate is built to protect). The forward-only guard (line 429) only requires p_to_stage >= cur_stage with no adjacency requirement, so `drying -> parchment` is a legal advance and `parchment -> milled` is a legal advance. Neither matches the `drying -> milled` predicate, so on BOTH layers the gate never evaluates reposo_status. A lot reaches 'milled' completely un-rested by taking the two-step path the system itself defines as valid. The word 'parchment' appears nowhere in the gate logic (grep confirms). The design's whole promise — line 185 'it is *impossible* to skip', line 169 'a lot physically cannot advance drying->milled until moisture-stable AND min-rest-days met' — is false: skipping is the DEFAULT path, and the only path that is gated (the unusual single-step drying->milled jump) is the one a careful user is least likely to take.
findings.scenario: Family seeds lot JC-571 at stage 'drying' with no moisture readings and 0 rest days. advance_processing_stage(JC-571,'parchment',...) succeeds (forward-only OK, gate predicate false). Then advance_processing_stage(JC-571,'milled',...) succeeds (cur_stage is now 'parchment' not 'drying', gate predicate false). JC-571 is milled with zero reposo, zero moisture evidence — the cup-defining rest skipped, exactly what the slice exists to prevent. The DB test never exercises this path (grep: no 'parchment' in p2s4_drying_reposo.db.test.ts), so it passes green while the invariant is open.
findings.fix: Gate the milling boundary on the FROM-set {drying, parchment} OR (preferably) gate any transition whose target is at/after 'milled' when the lot has not yet cleared reposo, by comparing against a persisted reposo-cleared marker. Minimal correct form: change both predicates to `coalesce(nullif(cur_stage,''),'cherry') in ('drying','parchment') and p_to_stage::batch_stage >= 'milled'::batch_stage` (RPC) and the analogous `old.stage in ('drying','parchment') and new.stage::batch_stage >= 'milled'::batch_stage` (trigger). Add a DB test for the drying->parchment->milled bypass that FAILS on the current code and passes on the fix.

findings.title: Rest clock counts from drying START, not drying COMPLETE — contradicts the spec and clears the gate before any real reposo has happened
findings.location: lines 228-244 (reposo_status rest-clock); spec line 168, dogfood line 185
findings.problem: Spec line 168 defines `rest_days_elapsed` as 'since drying COMPLETE', and the dogfood (line 185) frames it as 'a lot FINISHES drying ... resting 6/10 days'. The build measures rest from `min(drying_assignments.assigned_at)` ('when drying physically BEGAN', per the code's own comment line 228) or, failing that, the first 'drying' stage_advance event (line 233-237) — i.e. from drying START. Reposo is the post-drying rest that defines this farm's cup; conflating it with drying duration means the clock runs during the entire drying period. A Geisha can spend 15-25 days on a raised bed/parabolic dryer; with min_reposo_days defaulting to 5, the gate's rest_met goes true long before the lot has actually rested as parchment — and could be true the instant drying ends with zero true reposo. The 'rest that protects the cup' is not being measured at all.
findings.scenario: Lot assigned to a parabolic tunnel on day 0 (assigned_at = day 0), dries slowly to 11% over 18 days. On day 18 the family wants to mill. rest_days_elapsed = 18 >= 5 = rest_met true, moisture in-band -> gate green and 'clears'. But the lot has had ZERO days of post-drying reposo — the exact rest the slice promises to protect. The chip even says 'rest-stable — clear to mill' (line 256) while no reposo has occurred.
findings.fix: Start the reposo clock at the drying->parchment ('drying complete') transition, not the drying-start. Use `min(e.occurred_at) where kind='stage_advance' and payload->>'to_stage' = 'parchment'` as the rest anchor (and require that event to exist before rest_met can be true). Update the test to assert a lot that just finished drying has rest_days_elapsed ~0.

findings.title: record_moisture / assign_drying_station have NO UI and are not wired through the S0 offline seam — the capture half of the slice and its dogfood loop are unreachable
findings.location: recordMoisture.ts:138 + assignStation.ts:89 (no caller); src/app/(app)/drying/page.tsx (read-only); src/lib/offline/enqueueCommand.ts (never called from UI)
findings.problem: The spec lists `record_moisture(...)` and `assign_drying_station(...)` as the slice's command RPCs, names the offline port `src/lib/db/commands/recordMoisture.ts` 'offline via S0' (Ports line), lists 'S0 (offline moisture capture)' as a hard dependency (line 183), and the dogfood requires moisture readings to accumulate so a lot goes red->green. But: (1) grep shows NO component/route calls recordMoisture or assignStation — the only references are the DB test and a comment in drying.ts; (2) there is no moisture-capture form and no station-assign form anywhere in src/components/sections/drying or the /drying route (the page is a read-only server component, the only UI files are reposo-gate-chip and moisture-curve); (3) `enqueueCommand`/`makeEnqueueCommand` (the S0 offline seam) is called from NO UI anywhere in the app — so even the moisture command, which the spec singles out as 'offline via S0', is not actually wired to the outbox. The drying curve can only ever be populated by direct SQL/seed, not by the family in the field. The dogfood ('record readings, watch it go green, advance') is not achievable through the app.
findings.scenario: Family on the patio with a moisture meter opens /drying to log today's 11.4% reading on JC-571. There is no input — the page only displays curves built from pre-seeded rows. They cannot record a reading, cannot assign a lot to a bed, and nothing queues offline. The capture trunk the whole slice is built on is absent from the product surface.
findings.fix: Add the moisture-reading capture form and the station-assign control on the per-lot drying surface, wired through enqueueCommand (S0) so they queue offline and replay exactly-once on reconnect, mirroring how the sibling field-capture slices were supposed to call the seam. At minimum a Server Action calling recordMoisture/assignStation, plus the offline-enqueue path the spec names as a hard dependency.

findings.title: Promised `/process/[lot]/drying` per-lot surface does not exist — only the `/drying` board was built
findings.location: spec line 176 ('UI: /process/[lot]/drying + a /drying stations board'); referenced only in drying.ts:171 and types.ts:421 comments
findings.problem: The S4 UI spec (line 176) promises TWO surfaces: '/process/[lot]/drying' (the per-lot detail with the moisture curve and the advance-to-mill button) AND a '/drying' stations board. Only /drying exists (find on src/app shows no [lot]/drying route; the only process dir is /processing with a flat page.tsx). The route path '/process/[lot]/drying' is referenced solely in code comments (drying.ts:171, types.ts:421) — a getDryingLots() doc says it composes 'the view the /process/[lot]/drying surface renders', but that surface was never created. The per-lot drying detail (the deep-link target a manager would open for one lot from /processing) is missing; the content was folded into the /drying board's grid instead.
findings.scenario: Manager viewing /processing wants to drill into JC-571's drying detail (full-size moisture curve, station history, the gate reason, the advance action for that one lot). There is no /process/JC-571/drying route — clicking through is impossible; they can only see the lot as one of many cards on the shared /drying board.
findings.fix: Either build the `/process/[lot]/drying` (or the repo's `/processing/[lot]/drying`) per-lot route the spec and the getDryingLots() doc-comment promise, or, if the consolidation into /drying is the intended design change, update the spec/comments and ensure a per-lot deep-link still exists. As-is the comments reference a route that 404s.

findings.title: Weather-cover alert joins the static 7-day mock narrative, not the promised per-plot Open-Meteo feed, and fires no task onto the board (not closed-loop)
findings.location: lines 284-295 (v_drying_weather_risk); weather table at 20260620120000_init.sql:118
findings.problem: Spec line 277-279 and 183: 'v_drying_weather_risk(station) joins the Phase-1 per-plot Open-Meteo forecast feed to fire a cover/move-the-beds TASK ONTO THE BOARD when rain is incoming — closed-loop'. The build joins the Phase-1 `weather` table (init.sql:118), which is a static, hand-authored 7-day narrative figure set (the migration comment itself admits 'NOT a per-plot Open-Meteo table, which does not exist') with fixed rows — not a live, per-plot, or even refreshed forecast. And the 'closed loop' (firing a task onto the board) is not implemented: the view exposes a `cover_risk` boolean (line 292) but nothing creates a task; the migration comment defers it ('a board task is the UI's job') and no UI consumes getDryingWeatherRisk to create a task either. The promised closed-loop weather coupling degrades to a static badge derived from mock data.
findings.scenario: Rain is genuinely forecast for tomorrow. The weather table is a fixed demo narrative, so cover_risk reflects the canned figures, not reality; and even when cover_risk is true, no 'cover/move the beds' task appears on any board — the family gets no actionable alert. The closed loop the spec sells is open.
findings.fix: Wire v_drying_weather_risk to the real per-plot Open-Meteo forecast feed the spec references (or flag explicitly that the $0 build degrades to the static feed), and implement the closed-loop by surfacing a cover-the-beds task on the board (or task table) when cover_risk is true for an open-air station with an open assignment.

findings.title: Moisture curve hardcodes the 10.5–11.5 band instead of reading farm_season_config — the 'one owned place' tunable band promise is broken at the UI
findings.location: moisture-curve.tsx:8-11 (bandMin=10.5/bandMax=11.5 defaults); drying-board.tsx:80 (renders <MoistureCurve curve=... /> with no band props); drying.ts / page.tsx never read reposo_moisture_min/max
findings.problem: The migration deliberately puts the band in ONE owned, tunable place — farm_season_config.reposo_moisture_min_pct/max_pct (lines 33-38), 'never hardcoded across five SQL bodies ... the family tunes them later'. But the UI never reads that config: MoistureCurve defaults bandMin/bandMax to literal 10.5/11.5 (lines 8-11), the DryingBoard renders it with no band props (drying-board.tsx:80), and neither drying.ts nor the page fetches the config. The gate (DB) uses the tuned band; the curve (UI) draws the untuned literal. So if the family tunes the band, the gate logic and the visual target band silently disagree — the family sees the curve settle into the old window while the gate is judging a different one. The page header copy (page.tsx:15) and the curve also state '10.5-11.5%' as fixed text. The tunable-band SSOT is honored in SQL and broken in the UI.
findings.scenario: Family raises reposo_moisture_max_pct to 12.0 for a natural process lot. The DB gate now passes at 11.8%. But the moisture-curve target band still draws 10.5-11.5 and the endpoint marker shows red (out of the OLD band) at 11.8% — the UI says 'not in band' while the gate says 'clear'. The chip and the curve contradict the actual rule.
findings.fix: Fetch reposo_moisture_min_pct/max_pct from farm_season_config in the read port, pass them into MoistureCurve (and the chip/band label) so the visual band tracks the same SSOT the gate uses; drop the hardcoded literals and the fixed '10.5-11.5%' header copy.

findings.title: 'Trending flat' stability requirement reduced to a plain all-in-band check — oscillating readings pass as stable
findings.location: lines 215-226 (reposo_status moisture-stable computation)
findings.problem: Spec line 168 defines moisture-stable as 'last N readings within 10.5-11.5% AND trending flat'. The build (lines 217-226) only checks that the most-recent `reposo_stable_window` readings are ALL inside [min,max] (in_band_cnt == window). There is no slope/delta/variance check for 'trending flat'. With the default window of 2, two in-band readings of 10.6% then 11.4% (a 0.8-point swing, clearly not flat) count as stable. The 'trending flat' half of the spec definition is unimplemented; the gate accepts in-band oscillation as settled.
findings.scenario: Window=2. Lot reads 11.4% then 10.6% on consecutive days — bouncing, not settled, but both in-band. moisture_stable becomes true and (with rest met) the gate clears, even though the curve is visibly oscillating rather than flat — the opposite of the 'settled into the window' state the spec requires.
findings.fix: Add a flatness check to reposo_status: require the spread (max-min) of the recent window to be within a small tolerance (e.g. <= 0.5 pct), or the consecutive delta to be small, in addition to the all-in-band test — or update the spec to drop 'trending flat' if all-in-band is intentionally the accepted proxy.

findings.title: QC-hold commerce gate has no per-lot lock — a hold-vs-reserve TOCTOU race lets a reservation/shipment land on a lot that is being held (the exact race the oversell gate it claims to 'extend' already fixed)
findings.location: _prevent_held_lot_commit() body lines 198-216 (the `select 1 from qc_holds ... released_at is null` check); triggers lines 217-223
findings.problem: The slice's headline invariant — 'a QC-HOLD lot CANNOT be reserved or shipped' — is enforced by `_prevent_held_lot_commit`, a BEFORE INSERT trigger that does a bare `select 1 from qc_holds where green_lot_code=new.green_lot_code and released_at is null` with NO lock. The Phase-1 `prevent_oversell` trigger on the SAME two tables explicitly documents and fixes this very class of check-then-insert race by taking `pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext('green_lot:'||code))` BEFORE its read (green_inventory.sql 'finding #1'). The held-commit gate fires FIRST (trigger name 'lot_reservations_prevent_held_commit' sorts before '..._prevent_oversell'), reads qc_holds, and returns — all before the oversell trigger acquires the per-lot lock. So the hold check runs entirely OUTSIDE any serialization. Confirmed on disk: `pg_get_functiondef('_prevent_held_lot_commit')` contains no pg_advisory/FOR UPDATE/LOCK; `prevent_oversell` does. Under PostgREST's READ COMMITTED, two concurrent transactions — T1 inserting a reservation, T2 calling place_qc_hold — interleave: T1's held-commit trigger reads qc_holds and sees no open hold; T2 commits the hold; T1 proceeds to oversell (passes), commits. Net: a reservation/shipment is committed against a lot that is now under an open QC-HOLD — exactly the state the invariant says is impossible. For a Volcán Geisha Best-of-Panama micro-lot pulled for an off-flavor re-cup, this is a held lot getting sold out from under the QC team in the race window.
findings.scenario: Seed JC-9001 (100kg green). Cupper hits 'place hold' (off-flavor) at the same instant sales hits 'reserve 100kg to Acme'. T1(reserve) trigger reads qc_holds → empty → passes held-gate, then takes oversell lock, passes (100<=100), still mid-txn. T2(place_qc_hold) commits the open hold. T1 commits the reservation. Now `v_qc_status.held=true` AND a 100kg reservation exists on the held lot — the gate was bypassed. (Single-connection PGlite can't interleave, so this is not caught by s6_qc_cupping.db.test.ts; the structural absence of the lock — verified via pg_get_functiondef — is the proof.)
findings.fix: Acquire the same per-lot advisory lock the oversell gate uses, BEFORE reading qc_holds, inside `_prevent_held_lot_commit`: add `perform pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext('green_lot:' || new.green_lot_code));` as the first statement. Because prevent_oversell already takes the identical lock key on the same INSERT, both gates then serialize on the same per-lot mutex and a concurrent place_qc_hold (which should ALSO take that lock — see fix below) cannot interleave. Better: also have place_qc_hold take `pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext('green_lot:'||p_green_lot_code))` before inserting the hold, so hold-placement and claim-insertion fully serialize per lot. Add a regression test that holds a lot and asserts no reservation can coexist (and, where the harness supports two connections, an interleaved probe).

findings.title: QC-hold gate is BEFORE INSERT only while the oversell family it 'extends' is BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE — an UPDATE to a reservation/shipment row skips the hold check entirely
findings.location: triggers `lot_reservations_prevent_held_commit` / `lot_shipments_prevent_held_commit`, lines 217-223 (`before insert on ...`)
findings.problem: `_prevent_held_lot_commit` is wired `BEFORE INSERT` only, but the Phase-1 `prevent_oversell` trigger on the very same tables is `BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE` (green_inventory.sql) and its body even special-cases tg_op='UPDATE'. The slice header claims the hold gate 'EXTENDS the Phase-1 prevent_oversell trigger family' — but it does NOT cover UPDATE. Verified on disk: `pg_get_triggerdef` returns 'BEFORE INSERT ON public.lot_reservations' for the held trigger vs 'BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE' for oversell. Confirmed by repro: with JC-9001 under an open hold, `update lot_reservations set kg=50 where green_lot_code='JC-9001'` did NOT throw the qc-hold error (the held gate never fired), whereas oversell's UPDATE branch would have re-validated. This means any future or owner-level UPDATE path that mutates a reservation/shipment (raise the kg, repoint green_lot_code, 'edit' a claim) bypasses the hold quarantine. Today the client has no UPDATE grant and no RPC updates these tables, so it is not anon/authenticated-reachable RIGHT NOW — hence MED not CRIT — but it is a latent hole in a load-bearing invariant and a direct inconsistency with the family it claims to mirror: the moment a sibling slice adds a reservation-edit RPC (running SECURITY DEFINER as owner, RLS-bypassing), held lots become editable.
findings.scenario: Owner/definer path (or a future edit-reservation RPC): JC-9001 held; `update lot_reservations set kg=50` succeeds with no qc-hold rejection (confirmed in throwaway repro — UPDATE did not throw). The same UPDATE is correctly re-checked by prevent_oversell, proving the asymmetry is specific to the hold gate.
findings.fix: Change both triggers to `before insert or update on lot_reservations` / `... lot_shipments` so the hold gate has the identical surface as prevent_oversell. The trigger body already keys only on `new.green_lot_code`, so it works unchanged for UPDATE. Add a regression test: place a hold, attempt an owner UPDATE of a reservation's kg/green_lot_code, assert it is rejected with the qc-hold error.

findings.title: place_qc_hold writes p_device_id into BOTH placed_by and device_id — the 'who placed this quarantine' actor is permanently unrecoverable, and there is no separate actor parameter
findings.location: place_qc_hold INSERT, line 435-436: `insert into qc_holds (..., placed_by, device_id, ...) values (..., p_device_id, p_device_id, ...)`
findings.problem: qc_holds.placed_by is declared `not null` and commented 'device/actor that placed it' (line 107) — it is meant to record WHO quarantined the lot, the audit anchor for a quality decision on a Best-of-Panama lot. But place_qc_hold has no actor/cupper parameter at all; its INSERT passes `p_device_id` for BOTH placed_by and device_id (line 436). So placed_by is always literally the device id (e.g. 'srv' from the server action, since placeQcHold.ts defaults deviceId to 'server'/'srv'), never the human cupper. The release path is the same: release_qc_hold stamps released_by = p_device_id (line ~478). Result: the quarantine ledger can answer 'which tablet' but never 'which cupper held/released this lot' — and because the ledger is append-only with no correction path, the actor is unrecoverable forever. This is a compliance/traceability defect for the cup-quality audit trail the slice exists to protect (cup-to-cause is the slice's stated goal). The append-only-ness that is supposed to make the hold trustworthy instead locks in a useless actor value.
findings.scenario: Marisol (cupper w-cup-1) places a hold via the form. The row lands with placed_by='server' (the device default), not 'w-cup-1'. Later a Best-of-Panama dispute asks 'who quarantined lot JC-9001 and who cleared it?' — the ledger only shows the server/device, and there is no UPDATE path to ever fill in the real cupper.
findings.fix: Add a `p_placed_by` (actor / cupper_id) parameter to place_qc_hold and a `p_released_by` to release_qc_hold; insert/stamp the real actor into placed_by/released_by, keeping device_id separate. Thread cupperId/actorId from the validated command (placeQcHold.ts already has a cupper concept in the session flow) and the server action. Update the function-signature grants/revokes for the new arity. Add a test asserting placed_by reflects the supplied actor, distinct from device_id.

findings.title: release_qc_hold clears EVERY open hold on a lot in one call and never dedups on its idempotency_key — a second cupper's independent hold is silently released by an unrelated release
findings.location: release_qc_hold body lines ~470-484: `update qc_holds set released_at=p_occurred_at, released_by=p_device_id where green_lot_code=p_green_lot_code and released_at is null`
findings.problem: place_qc_hold has no uniqueness on green_lot_code, so a lot can carry MULTIPLE concurrent open holds (e.g. an off-flavor hold AND a separate green-defect hold placed by two different cuppers with different idempotency keys — confirmed reachable: two distinct keys insert two open rows). release_qc_hold then stamps ALL of them in one UPDATE (no hold-id targeting; the WHERE only matches green_lot_code + released_at is null). So a cupper releasing THEIR off-flavor hold silently also releases the OTHER cupper's still-valid defect hold, re-opening commerce on a lot that a second QC reason wanted quarantined. Additionally the function accepts p_idempotency_key purely as 'envelope' and never dedups on it (the comment admits this) — a replayed release with the same key is a no-op only because all holds are already closed, not because of idempotency; and a same-key release on a freshly-held lot does NOT collide (confirmed: second release with reused key 'rel-SAME' did not throw, because release never inserts a key row). The net invariant gap: 'release re-opens commerce' is coarser than 'release the hold I closed' — one release can blanket-clear independent quarantines.
findings.scenario: Marisol places hold A (off-flavor, key h-A) on JC-9001; Diego places hold B (green defects, key h-B) on the same lot. Sales asks Marisol to clear the off-flavor concern; she calls release_qc_hold(JC-9001). BOTH A and B get released_at stamped, v_qc_status flips held=false, and the lot is sellable despite Diego's defect hold never being addressed (repro showed open-hold count 2 -> 0 on one release).
findings.fix: Either (a) enforce one-open-hold-per-lot with a partial unique index `create unique index on qc_holds(green_lot_code) where released_at is null` and have place_qc_hold treat a re-hold as idempotent/append a reason, or (b) make release target a specific hold id (`p_hold_id`) so each release closes exactly the hold it names, and have v_qc_status/the gate consider a lot held while ANY open hold remains. Add a test: two independent holds on one lot, release one, assert the other stays open and the lot stays held.

findings.title: device_seq minted from now.getTime() reintroduces the exact serverless-collision HIGH bug the sibling slices already fixed
findings.location: writeEnvelope(), line 42: `p_device_seq: now.getTime()` (comment line 32 calls it "monotonic per call via the clock")
findings.problem: Every planner write (schedule_pasada / replan_pasada / record_maturation_signal) shares one device_id ('planner-console', line 33) and draws device_seq from `now.getTime()` (epoch-ms). pasada_schedule and maturation_signal both carry `unique (device_id, device_seq)` (migration lines 86, 130). Two writes landing in the same millisecond — a double-click submit, two plots scheduled back-to-back, or two concurrent serverless instances both seeded from the clock — mint the SAME (device_id, device_seq) and the second write hard-fails on the unique key. This is verbatim the bug the harvests slice documents and fixed: "each instance seeds from epoch-ms, so two instances can mint the SAME small sequence and collide on lot_event's (device_id, device_seq) UNIQUE key (HIGH review finding)" (src/app/(app)/harvests/actions.ts lines 43-49, which switched to crypto.getRandomValues 48-bit randomDeviceSeq). The crew slice solved the same problem with the DB-backed next_server_seq() (people_system.sql lines 163-184; crew/actions.ts lines 66-84). S8 landed AFTER both fixes and regressed to the known-bad clock approach.
findings.scenario: Owner schedules Pasada 1 for plot A, then immediately Pasada 1 for plot B (or React double-invokes the action). Both writeEnvelope() calls return the same now.getTime() ms. Repro confirmed in PGlite: two schedule_pasada calls with identical (device_id='planner-console', device_seq=1700000000000) but different idempotency_keys → `duplicate key value violates unique constraint "pasada_schedule_device_id_device_seq_key"`. The second pasada silently fails; friendlyError maps the raw 'duplicate key' string (matches none of its branches) to the generic 'We couldn't save that plan' even though the user did nothing wrong.
findings.fix: Mirror the sibling fix: replace `p_device_seq: now.getTime()` with either a draw from the DB sequence (`next_server_seq()` already exposed and granted) or the harvests slice's `randomDeviceSeq()` (48-bit crypto.getRandomValues). Either makes ('planner-console', seq) unique across concurrent calls and cold starts; idempotency_key stays the exactly-once anchor.

findings.title: No partial-unique index on (plot, season, pasada): scheduling the same pasada twice creates two ACTIVE plans and fires two duplicate tasks
findings.location: schedule_pasada lines 340-403; pasada_schedule table lines 112-133 (only unique keys are idempotency_key and (device_id,device_seq)); v_pasada_calendar lines 243-259 + replan_pasada's `order by recorded_at desc limit 1` line 450
findings.problem: schedule_pasada appends a plan and fires a tasks row, but nothing enforces ONE active (non-superseded) plan per (plot_id, season, pasada_number). idempotency_key only dedupes a REPLAY of the same logical write; two DISTINCT schedule calls for the same pasada (different keys) both succeed. The whole slice is built on the single-active-plan invariant: v_pasada_calendar is documented as "the ACTIVE plan per (plot, pasada)" and replan_pasada does `order by recorded_at desc limit 1` to find "the" current active plan to supersede — which silently assumes singularity. With two active rows, a later replan supersedes only ONE, orphaning the other active plan + its task forever.
findings.scenario: Repro confirmed in PGlite: two schedule_pasada('p-a','2026',1,...) with different device_seq/idempotency_keys → DUP_ACTIVE_PLANS=2, DUP_TASKS=2 (two pickers both told to pick the same plot on the same pass). A subsequent replan_pasada('p-a','2026',1,...) supersedes one and leaves the other active (REPLAN_AFTER_DUP stayed at 2). Reachable from a double-submit past the 1ms window or an owner deliberately re-scheduling.
findings.fix: Add a partial unique index `create unique index pasada_active_uq on pasada_schedule (plot_id, season, pasada_number) where status <> 'superseded';` so a second active plan for the same pass is rejected at the data layer (then map the violation to a friendly 'That pasada is already scheduled — re-plan it instead'). Alternatively, make schedule_pasada supersede-or-noop when an active plan for the tuple already exists.

findings.title: record_maturation_signal has no occurred_at ordering guard: a late/out-of-order offline replay overwrites newer phenology with stale GDD/NDVI, corrupting derived readiness
findings.location: record_maturation_signal upsert lines 325-331 (`on conflict (plot_id) do update set gdd_accumulated = coalesce(p_gdd_accumulated, ...), updated_at = excluded.updated_at`)
findings.problem: plot_phenology is upserted last-writer-wins by EXECUTION order, with no comparison of the incoming occurred_at against the stored row. The function takes p_occurred_at and device_seq precisely so writes are offline-replayable in causal order (comment lines 19-20, 292-293), but the upsert ignores occurred_at entirely — it just sets the columns and stamps updated_at = the incoming occurred_at. An older signal applied after a newer one clobbers the newer derived inputs. Since readiness is DERIVED from plot_phenology.gdd_accumulated/ndvi, this silently corrupts the readiness rank and the predicted_ready_date the picker's morning runs on.
findings.scenario: Repro confirmed in PGlite: record a newer signal (occurred_at 2026-06-20, gdd=2000), then a STALE one (occurred_at 2026-06-01, gdd=500) arrives late via the S0 outbox → plot_phenology.gdd_accumulated becomes 500 and updated_at goes BACKWARD to 2026-05-31. The plot drops from 'ready' to 'not ready' on stale data. With the offline outbox (the slice's stated goal), out-of-order delivery is normal, not exotic.
findings.fix: Guard the upsert on occurred_at: only advance a column when the incoming signal is newer than the row's last-applied occurred_at — e.g. store last_signal_at and `do update set gdd_accumulated = case when excluded.last_signal_at >= plot_phenology.last_signal_at then coalesce(p_gdd_accumulated, plot_phenology.gdd_accumulated) else plot_phenology.gdd_accumulated end, ...`. The append-only maturation_signal ledger already preserves the true history; the derived row must reflect the latest-by-occurred_at, not latest-by-execution.

findings.title: NULL idempotency_key silently disables exactly-once: the existence guard becomes NULL=NULL and every replay re-applies
findings.location: record_maturation_signal lines 312-314 (`if exists (select 1 ... where idempotency_key = p_idempotency_key) then return`); also schedule_pasada line 364 and replan_pasada line 436; column `idempotency_key text unique` lines 85/129 (NULLs are distinct in a Postgres unique index)
findings.problem: The exactly-once gate is `where idempotency_key = p_idempotency_key`. When p_idempotency_key IS NULL, `NULL = NULL` is NULL (never true), so the guard never fires AND the unique index permits unlimited NULL rows. The RPC will happily append a second ledger row / second plan / re-apply phenology on a replay. The TS actions always mint crypto.randomUUID() so the app path is safe today, but the RPC is the granted authenticated command door — any direct caller (and the future S0 outbox replaying a row whose key was lost) passing NULL gets no replay protection.
findings.scenario: Repro confirmed in PGlite: two record_maturation_signal calls with p_idempotency_key = NULL (distinct device_seqs) → NULL_KEY_LEDGER_ROWS=2, both inserts succeed, phenology applied twice. The function advertises 'exactly-once: a replay with the same key is a no-op' (lines 310-311) but provides zero protection for a NULL key.
findings.fix: Reject a NULL/blank key at the top of each command: `if p_idempotency_key is null or btrim(p_idempotency_key) = '' then raise exception 'idempotency_key is required' using errcode = 'invalid_parameter_value'; end if;` (or `not null` on the column). The exactly-once contract must not depend on the caller never sending NULL.

findings.title: Command args reach the RPC completely unvalidated; raw CHECK-constraint rejections are mapped to a useless generic message
findings.location: schedulePasada lines 76-90, replanPasada 105-119, recordMaturationSignal 133-145 (args passed straight to sb.rpc, no validation); friendlyError lines 47-61
findings.problem: Unlike the sibling command port advanceProcessingStage (which validates currentKg > 0, integer device_seq >= 0, required fields, valid stage before any write — src/lib/db/commands/advanceProcessingStage.ts lines 77-106), the plan actions do NO input validation: negative gddAccumulated, NDVI outside [0,1], an empty season, a non-enum ripenessTarget, or a malformed date all pass straight through to the RPC and rely on DB CHECK constraints. friendlyError only recognizes 'unknown plot'/'foreign_key'/'permission'/'append-only'; every CHECK violation (negative gdd, bad ripe_pct, ndvi range) falls through to the generic 'We couldn't save that plan. Please try again.' — the family gets no actionable feedback and a perfectly retryable-looking error that will never succeed.
findings.scenario: Confirmed: record_maturation_signal('p-a', null, -500, ...) → `violates check constraint maturation_signal_gdd_accumulated_check`; schedule_pasada(... 'URGENT' ...) → `violates check constraint pasada_schedule_predicted_ripe_pct_check`. Both reach friendlyError and surface as the catch-all 'try again' with no hint that the value is the problem. A future planner form wired to these actions hands the user an unrecoverable, unexplained failure.
findings.fix: Validate in the action (or a command port mirroring advanceProcessingStage) before the RPC: gddAccumulated null-or->=0, ndviLatest null-or-in-[0,1], season non-blank, ripenessTarget in {low,medium,high}, predictedReadyDate a real ISO date, pasadaNumber >= 1 — returning field-keyed friendly errors. Keep the DB CHECKs as the backstop.

findings.title: schedule_pasada/replan_pasada fire a task with null worker_id and leak a raw NOT NULL violation when no workers exist; the resolver's 'never null' guarantee is overstated
findings.location: _resolve_pasada_worker lines 267-287 (returns null when zero workers); used at schedule_pasada line 374 / insert line 382-392, replan_pasada line 454 / insert line 456-466; comment claims 'NEVER returns null when any worker exists' / 'the NOT NULL constraint always holds' (lines 263-264, 282-283)
findings.problem: _resolve_pasada_worker's last-resort branch is 'any worker at all', so it returns NULL only when the workers table is empty. The header comment promises the resolver guarantees the NOT NULL holds, but it does not in that case — the tasks.worker_id NOT NULL constraint then raises a raw Postgres error from inside the SECURITY DEFINER RPC. The whole txn (plan + task) rolls back, and friendlyError maps the raw 'null value in column "worker_id"' string to the generic fallthrough.
findings.scenario: Repro confirmed in PGlite: with zero workers, schedule_pasada(...) → `null value in column "worker_id" of relation "tasks" violates not-null constraint`. Edge today (the farm always has workers) but reachable on a fresh/seed-less environment, and the comment's hard guarantee is false.
findings.fix: When _resolve_pasada_worker returns null, raise a friendly domain error before the task insert (e.g. `if worker is null then raise exception 'no worker available to assign the harvest pass' using errcode = 'no_data_found'; end if;`) and add a friendlyError branch, so the failure is explained rather than a raw constraint leak — or document the no-workers precondition honestly instead of claiming the NOT NULL always holds.

findings.title: Reposo gate is bypassed by the normal pipeline: drying→parchment→milled (and drying→green) skip it entirely
findings.location: L442 (RPC precondition) and L477 (trigger backstop) — both gate ONLY `cur_stage='drying' AND to_stage='milled'`
findings.problem: The load-bearing Phase-2 invariant — a lot CANNOT advance to milling until moisture-stable (10.5–11.5%) AND min rest-days — is enforced ONLY on the literal `drying → milled` transition, in BOTH layers (RPC L442: `if coalesce(nullif(cur_stage,''),'cherry')='drying' and p_to_stage='milled'`; trigger L477: `if ...old.stage...='drying' and new.stage='milled'`). But the canonical, designed pipeline is `cherry → fermentation → drying → parchment → milled → green` (documented at src/lib/data/processing.ts:10 and encoded in the yield-curve edges at 20260621092000_event_log_units_lot_graph.sql:195-196: `drying→parchment 0.90`, `parchment→milled 0.80`). The only ordering guard in advance_processing_stage is forward-only (`p_to_stage::batch_stage < cur_stage` raises). The batch_stage enum order is cherry<fermentation<drying<parchment<milled<green, so `drying→parchment`, `parchment→milled`, and `drying→green` are ALL valid forward moves and NONE of them match the gate's literal `drying→milled` predicate. Routing a lot drying→parchment (ungated) then parchment→milled (cur_stage is now 'parchment', gate never fires) reaches 'milled' with a soaking-wet, never-rested lot. The trigger backstop fails for the identical reason: on the parchment→milled UPDATE, old.stage='parchment'. This nullifies the entire feature the ROADMAP calls the operational defense of Janson's premium (docs/ROADMAP.md:332: 'a lot physically cannot advance to milling until both moisture stability... AND minimum rest days are met'), and the UI comment at src/components/sections/drying/drying-board.tsx:19 ('a milled advance is physically impossible until the lot is rest-stable') is false. The 16 passing S4 db tests never exercise the parchment route — they only test the direct drying→milled jump, so the gap is invisible to the suite.
findings.scenario: PROVEN with a throwaway PGlite migration-replay test (now deleted): mint a cherry lot → advance to 'drying' → record a single 20.0% moisture reading, zero rest. (1) Direct `advance_processing_stage(lot,'milled',...)` correctly RAISES `reposo gate`. (2) `advance_processing_stage(lot,'parchment',...)` SUCCEEDS (ungated edge). (3) `advance_processing_stage(lot,'milled',...)` now SUCCEEDS — lots.stage becomes 'milled' while v_reposo_status.ready is still false. The wet, unrested lot is milled. The direct `update lots set stage='milled'` trigger test passes only because that single hop is the one path both layers cover.
findings.fix: Gate every forward EXIT from drying, not the single drying→milled hop. In BOTH layers, change the predicate to fire whenever a lot leaves 'drying' for any stage at/after milling — i.e. `cur_stage='drying' AND p_to_stage::batch_stage >= 'milled'::batch_stage` (covers milled and green), AND additionally block `drying→parchment` unless rest-stable if parchment is meant to be the rested state — OR, cleaner: make the gate fire on `cur_stage='drying'` for ANY advance and require reposo_status.ready before the lot may leave drying at all. Equally important, forbid skipping the intermediate parchment stage by adding an adjacent-stage (or explicit allowed-transition table) constraint so a lot cannot jump drying→milled/green directly, closing the multi-hop laundering. Add db tests for: drying→parchment→milled on a wet/unrested lot (must RAISE), drying→green direct (must RAISE), and the legitimate rested drying→parchment→milled (must SUCCEED). Update the false UI claims in drying-board.tsx:19 and drying/page.tsx:19 to match the real enforcement.

findings.title: Reposo gate skipped by advancing drying→green directly (milled never visited)
findings.location: L442 / L477 — gate predicate hard-codes `p_to_stage='milled'` / `new.stage='milled'`
findings.problem: Because 'green' sorts after 'milled' in the batch_stage enum and the only ordering guard is forward-only, `advance_processing_stage(lot,'green',...)` from a 'drying' lot is a valid forward move that the gate never inspects (the predicate matches the string 'milled' exactly). A lot can go straight from drying to green — past the milling boundary the reposo gate exists to protect — without ever satisfying moisture-stability or rest-days. This is the same root cause as the parchment bypass but is a distinct reachable path (single hop) and equally defeats the invariant; green is the final inventory/sellable stage (materialize_green_lot territory), so an out-of-spec lot can reach sellable green with no rest at all.
findings.scenario: Mint→drying, record a 20% reading, call `advance_processing_stage(lot,'green',...)`. cur_stage='drying', p_to_stage='green' — the gate's `p_to_stage='milled'` predicate is false, so neither the RPC precondition nor the trigger (new.stage='green') fires; the UPDATE to stage='green' succeeds on a wet, unrested lot. Same throwaway-test substrate that proved the parchment route confirms this hop is ungated.
findings.fix: Fold into the same fix as the parchment bypass: gate ANY transition that leaves 'drying' toward milling-or-beyond (`p_to_stage::batch_stage >= 'milled'::batch_stage`) in both the RPC and the trigger, and add an adjacent/allowed-transition constraint so stages cannot be skipped. Add a regression test for the direct drying→green hop (must RAISE).

findings.title: Moisture stability + rest clock trust client-supplied occurred_at — future-dated in-band readings forge 'rest-stable'
findings.location: reposo_status L209-224 (latest + in-band window ordered by `occurred_at desc, id desc`) and record_moisture_reading L304-338 (no occurred_at sanity bound)
findings.problem: The stability window selects the most-recent `reposo_stable_window` readings by client-supplied `occurred_at` (record_moisture_reading takes p_occurred_at verbatim; the only CHECK is 0≤pct≤100). moisture_stable is true iff those latest-by-occurred_at readings sit in [10.5,11.5]. Because occurred_at is operator/device wall-clock with no upper bound, two in-band readings stamped with a FUTURE occurred_at outrank a later-recorded, truly-current 19% reading — so a physically wet lot reports moisture_stable=true and ready=true. The rest-clock (rest_days from min(assigned_at) or first drying event, L230-244) is similarly only as honest as the supplied timestamps. Append-only immutability (the block trigger) does NOT protect against this: forged evidence is appended, not mutated. For a Best-of-Panama Geisha estate where the reposo defines the cup, a gate that can be satisfied by mis-dated readings is an honor system, not a data-layer guarantee.
findings.scenario: PROVEN with a throwaway PGlite test (now deleted): drying lot, assigned 12 days ago. Record 11.0% at `now()+2 days` and 11.1% at `now()+1 day`, then the true 19.0% at `now()`. v_reposo_status returns moisture_stable=true, ready=true — the wet lot passes the gate (and, combined with finding #1, mills). No mutation was needed; the readings were merely future-dated on insert.
findings.fix: (a) Reject future-dated readings in record_moisture_reading: `if p_occurred_at > now() + interval '1 hour' then raise` (small skew tolerance), mirroring how event ledgers bound field clock vs server accept clock. (b) Consider basing 'latest' on recorded_at (server accept clock, already defaulted to now()) rather than client occurred_at for the stability decision, or require the in-band window to also span a minimum real elapsed wall-time. (c) Add a db test asserting a future-dated reading is rejected and that a wet real reading cannot be masked by future-dated in-band ones.

findings.title: QC actions never draw next_server_seq() — the 2nd online QC-hold/session collides on unique(device_id,device_seq), so a bad lot stays sellable
findings.location: placeQcHoldAction (L60-73), releaseQcHoldAction (L75-83), recordCuppingSessionAction (L85-103); commands placeQcHold.ts L37/validatePlaceQcHold + recordCuppingSession.ts L48-49; SQL qc_holds/cupping_sessions `unique (device_id, device_seq)` (migration L62, L86)
findings.problem: Every QC table carries `unique (device_id, device_seq)`. The established Phase-2 C1 fix (crew/actions.ts `nextServerSeq()` drawing the SECURITY DEFINER `next_server_seq()`) exists precisely because the single synthetic online device is `device_id='server'`, so a CONSTANT seq collides on the SECOND write system-wide. The QC actions DO NOT call next_server_seq(): they pass raw form data straight to the commands, and the QC forms supply no deviceId/deviceSeq. So validatePlaceQcHold/validateCuppingSession default to `deviceId='server'` and `deviceSeq=0` (toNumber(undefined)=null -> coerced to 0). The RPC's `on conflict (idempotency_key) do nothing` does NOT cover the `(device_id, device_seq)` unique key, which fires first and is unhandled. Result: the FIRST QC-hold or cupping session placed online succeeds; the SECOND (any other lot, any other key) throws and is rejected. Because the hold never lands, `_prevent_held_lot_commit` finds no open hold and the lot remains reservable/shippable — the load-bearing QC-HOLD invariant is defeated WITHOUT bypassing the trigger at all.
findings.scenario: Place a hold on JC-9001 (succeeds, seq=0). Then try to hold JC-9002 (off-flavor, must be quarantined): place_qc_hold('JC-9002',...,'server',0,...) throws `duplicate key value violates unique constraint "qc_holds_device_id_device_seq_key"`. The hold never records; JC-9002 has zero open holds; reserve/ship JC-9002 succeeds and the defective Geisha ships. Reproduced in pglite: two place_qc_hold on ('server',0) -> 2nd throws; JC-9002 open holds = 0. Same for two record_cupping_session calls -> `cupping_sessions_device_id_device_seq_key`.
findings.fix: Make the QC actions mint server identity exactly like crew/actions.ts: `deviceId='server'`, `deviceSeq: await nextServerSeq(sb)`, `idempotencyKey: idempotencyKeyOrNew(raw)`, spread over the raw form data, in placeQcHoldAction/releaseQcHoldAction/recordCuppingSessionAction/recordCupScoreAction. (Extract the shared nextServerSeq/idempotencyKeyOrNew helpers from crew/actions.ts.) Add a QC actions test mirroring the C1 regression test that asserts two online holds on different lots both land.

findings.title: Cupping has NO save path: the scoresheet is a pure calculator — recordCuppingSession/recordCupScore actions are never invoked, so no cup score can ever be persisted
findings.location: Entire component (no <form>, no formAction, no onSubmit, no submit button — grep count of submit-ish tokens = 0); doc comment L33-34 falsely claims 'Persisting a session/score routes through the recordCuppingSession + recordCupScore Server Actions'
findings.problem: CuppingScoresheet renders sliders + a live total but has zero wiring to persist anything — no form action, no submit handler, no call to recordCuppingSessionAction/recordCupScoreAction. A repo-wide grep shows those two actions are referenced ONLY by vi.fn() mocks in tests; no production component calls them. The cup-to-cause loop, v_cup_final_score, v_cupper_drift, and v_qc_status.latest_cup_score therefore stay permanently empty for the whole farm. The slice's headline feature (record an SCA-CVA / legacy cup score for a Best-of-Panama Geisha lot, the reposo that defines the cup) cannot be performed at all through the UI.
findings.scenario: Open /qc/cup/JC-9001, set every attribute, see the live total and band. There is no Save button. Navigate away — nothing was written. getCupFinalScores()/getCupperDrift() return []; the QC table's 'latest score' column is forever blank.
findings.fix: Wire CuppingScoresheet to the actions: on submit, call recordCuppingSessionAction to open the session, then recordCupScoreAction per attribute (or one batched RPC), via a Server Action form. Inject server identity (deviceId='server', deviceSeq via next_server_seq, a minted idempotencyKey) in the actions (see finding 1). Add an outside-in test that a submitted scoresheet calls the session+score actions and that the parity between cupFinalScore() and v_cup_final_score holds.

findings.title: Raw Postgres exceptions leak verbatim to the family in the hold drawer (duplicate-key / FK strings shown as the error toast)
findings.location: Error alert renders {state.message} (L138+); message is built in commands/placeQcHold.ts L78 `place_qc_hold: ${error.message}` and actions.ts holdToState (L52-55) passes result.message through unmapped
findings.problem: When any RPC error fires (the seq collision in finding 1, an unknown-lot foreign_key_violation from place_qc_hold's existence check, or a `restrict_violation` from the append-only guard), the command wraps the raw `error.message` and the action surfaces it directly as state.message, which qc-hold-control renders verbatim in the role=alert toast. There is no error mapping. A non-technical, ~90% Ngäbe-Buglé migrant crew / family audience is shown strings like `place_qc_hold: duplicate key value violates unique constraint "qc_holds_device_id_device_seq_key"`.
findings.scenario: Trigger the 2nd-hold collision from finding 1 in the UI: the drawer shows the alert `place_qc_hold: duplicate key value violates unique constraint "qc_holds_device_id_device_seq_key"`. Confirmed the exact Postgres string via pglite repro; confirmed it is rendered raw at qc-hold-control L138/L200.
findings.fix: Map known Postgres errcodes/constraint names to friendly copy in the action (e.g. 23505 -> 'That action was already recorded — refresh.'; foreign_key_violation -> 'That green lot does not exist.'; restrict_violation -> 'This QC record is locked and cannot be changed.'). Never pass a raw error.message to the UI; log the raw text server-side and surface a sanitized message. Add a test asserting a duplicate-key RPC error maps to friendly copy.

findings.title: record_defect RPC has no TS command, no Server Action, and no defect-entry UI — the green-grading defect ledger can never be populated
findings.location: record_defect() defined L376-401 and granted to authenticated L514/520; no corresponding src/lib/db/commands/recordDefect.ts; no action in src/app/(app)/qc/actions.ts; no defect input in any qc section component (only read-side getGreenDefects in qc.ts and mapGreenDefect)
findings.problem: The migration ships record_defect() (the sole write door for green_defects) and the read port maps green_defects, but there is no command wrapper, no Server Action, and no UI to enter a defect. The scoresheet has no defect grid. So v_qc_status.primary_defects / secondary_defects are structurally always 0 and the SCA green-grade defect input the slice comment advertises ('QC supplies the real defect input the family reads alongside it') is never captured.
findings.scenario: There is no path in the app to record a full-black or broken-bean count for a lot; every lot reads primary_defects=0, secondary_defects=0 in the QC table regardless of true cup quality.
findings.fix: Add recordDefect.ts (validator + thin command over the one .rpc), a recordDefectAction injecting server identity (finding 1), and a defect-entry control on the cupping/QC surface. Cover with a command test and an action test.

findings.title: release_qc_hold accepts p_idempotency_key but never uses it (dedup relies only on the open-hold predicate), and recordCupScore's validator demands a key the action never injects — inconsistent idempotency envelope
findings.location: release_qc_hold() L406-432 — p_idempotency_key declared in the signature but referenced nowhere in the body; commands/placeQcHold.ts releaseQcHold L? mints an idempotencyKey only to pass it to an RPC that ignores it. Compare recordCupScore.ts validateCupScore L? which REQUIRES idempotencyKey/deviceId/deviceSeq, yet recordCupScoreAction passes raw form data containing none.
findings.problem: Two RPC-envelope inconsistencies: (a) release_qc_hold takes p_idempotency_key for 'a uniform RPC envelope' but never reads it; its only dedup is `where released_at is null`. That is correct for a single open hold, but if a future outbox replays release after a NEW unrelated hold was placed on the same lot, the replay would wrongly clear the new hold (the key that should have made it a true no-op is discarded). (b) recordCupScore.validateCupScore hard-requires deviceId/deviceSeq/idempotencyKey (errors if absent), but recordCupScoreAction never injects them — so even if the scoresheet were wired (finding 2), every score submit would fail validation. The two ends of the same command disagree on whose job it is to supply the envelope.
findings.scenario: (a) Outbox replays release_qc_hold('JC-9001') after a fresh legitimate hold was placed -> the new open hold is silently released though the key proves it is a stale replay. (b) Wire the scoresheet naively -> recordCupScoreAction passes form data with no deviceSeq -> validateCupScore returns errors.deviceSeq and the score never saves.
findings.fix: Either honor p_idempotency_key in release_qc_hold (record/lookup the release event by key, no-op on a seen key) or drop the param and document the open-hold predicate as the contract. Standardize that the ACTION layer always injects deviceId/deviceSeq/idempotencyKey (as crew/actions.ts does) and the validators stop requiring them from raw form input. Add a replay test for release.

findings.title: place_qc_hold writes placed_by = p_device_id ('server'), discarding the real actor identity in the audit ledger
findings.location: place_qc_hold INSERT L? — `values (p_green_lot_code, p_reason, p_occurred_at, p_device_id, p_device_id, p_device_seq, p_idempotency_key)` maps p_device_id into BOTH placed_by and device_id; no p_placed_by / actor arg in the signature
findings.problem: qc_holds.placed_by is documented as 'device/actor that placed it' and is meant to record who quarantined a lot, but the RPC stamps it with p_device_id, which the action hardcodes to the literal string 'server'. Every hold's placed_by is 'server' and released_by is 'server', so the append-only quarantine ledger has no real actor attribution — a regression for a compliance-adjacent QC-HOLD record on a Best-of-Panama lot.
findings.scenario: select placed_by from qc_holds -> always 'server' (confirmed via repro: placed_by='server', device_id='server'). Cannot answer 'who held this lot and why' beyond the free-text reason.
findings.fix: Add an actor/placed_by param sourced from the authenticated user (auth.uid()/role) and stamp placed_by/released_by with it, keeping device_id separate for the offline-sync key. Cover with a test asserting placed_by reflects the caller, not the device.

findings.title: apply_ferment_recipe collides on lot_event(device_id,device_seq) — two recipe binds in the same wall-clock second throw 23505 and the second silently fails
findings.location: apply_ferment_recipe, line 216 (record_lot_event call: device_id 'server-ferment', device_seq = extract(epoch from clock_timestamp())::bigint)
findings.problem: apply_ferment_recipe appends its lot_event with a HARD-CODED server device_id 'server-ferment' and a device_seq of `extract(epoch from clock_timestamp())::bigint` — truncated to WHOLE SECONDS. The lot_event table carries a GLOBAL (cross-stream) `unique (device_id, device_seq)` (20260621092000_event_log_units_lot_graph.sql:223). So any two apply_ferment_recipe calls that land in the same one-second window — on ANY two batches/lots, by any two users — produce the identical pair ('server-ferment', <same epoch second>) and the second INSERT into lot_event raises `duplicate key value violates unique constraint "lot_event_device_id_device_seq_key"` (errcode 23505). Because the whole RPC is one txn, the second recipe binding is rolled back entirely: the ferment_batches.recipe_id update is undone and NO ferment_recipe_applied event lands. The binding silently fails. This breaks the exactly-once/append invariant and is squarely reachable during peak harvest at the wet mill, where multiple batches are bound to recipes back-to-back. It also contradicts the migration's own header (line 181) that 'every RPC accepts the client-minted device_id/device_seq/idempotency_key so it is offline-replayable' — apply_ferment_recipe accepts NONE of them (signature is only p_batch_id, p_recipe_id; lines 187-190), so it is neither offline-replayable nor idempotent at the event layer.
findings.scenario: REPRODUCED against the real migrations via PGlite: start two batches on JC-900/JC-901, then within the same second `select apply_ferment_recipe(bA,'rec-z'); select apply_ferment_recipe(bB,'rec-z2');`. Captured ground truth: APPLY-COLLISION-ERROR: duplicate key value violates unique constraint "lot_event_device_id_device_seq_key"; recipe_applied events landed: 1 (the second apply was rolled back). The official S3 db test (src/test/db/s_p2s3_fermentation.db.test.ts) never exercises apply_ferment_recipe beyond checking it is granted (line 455), so this is untested-and-broken.
findings.fix: Give apply_ferment_recipe the same client-minted envelope as the other three RPCs: add p_device_id/p_device_seq/p_idempotency_key params, plumb them through record_lot_event, and namespace the event idempotency key (e.g. 'apply-recipe:'||p_idempotency_key). The server action (src/app/(app)/ferment/actions.ts) already mints a 48-bit randomDeviceSeq + UUID idempotencyKey envelope — wire apply through withEnvelope exactly like start/reading/water. At minimum, replace the epoch-seconds device_seq with the same crypto-random 48-bit value used everywhere else so two same-second applies cannot collide. Add a concurrency test (two distinct applies in one second must both succeed).

findings.title: apply_ferment_recipe is not offline-replayable / not event-layer idempotent — no client envelope, contradicting the migration's stated RPC contract
findings.location: apply_ferment_recipe signature lines 187-190 and event append lines 213-218
findings.problem: The migration header (lines 17-20, 178-181) and the AD-8/ADR-002 contract promise that EVERY command RPC takes the client-minted device_id/device_seq/idempotency_key so writes are exactly-once and offline-replayable from the P2-S0 outbox. apply_ferment_recipe breaks that contract: it takes no envelope, uses now() for occurred_at and a server epoch for device_seq, and has NO idempotency_key replay short-circuit on the event append — its only dedupe is the in-row 'already bound to this exact recipe' no-op check (line 207). Consequences: (1) an offline rebind cannot be replayed deterministically (the event's occurred_at/device_seq are minted server-side at replay time, not at capture time, so the hash chain records the wrong field clock and a different seq on every replay); (2) a rebind A->B->A writes a fresh ferment_recipe_applied event each non-no-op step with a brand-new epoch seq, so a retried/duplicated rebind that is NOT a no-op (A then B) appends two events even for the same logical intent. The other three S3 RPCs (start/reading/water) honor the contract; apply is the lone violator.
findings.scenario: From the offline outbox, the same 'bind recipe rec-B' command is delivered twice (network retry). First delivery flips recipe_id to rec-B and writes an event. The second delivery is a no-op only because recipe_id already equals rec-B — but if a 'bind rec-C' slipped between them, the replay of 'bind rec-B' is treated as a fresh apply and appends a second rec-B event with a new server epoch seq and now() occurred_at, corrupting the deterministic replay the rest of the system relies on.
findings.fix: Adopt the standard envelope (p_device_id, p_device_seq, p_idempotency_key, and ideally p_occurred_at) and short-circuit on the event's idempotency_key exactly as start_ferment_batch does (select existing event by idempotency_key + kind='ferment_recipe_applied' and return early). This single change also fixes the same-second collision finding above.

findings.title: record_ferment_reading / log_mill_water: a (device_id,device_seq) clash makes the WHOLE RPC fail with a raw 23505 instead of being a clean no-op — the idempotency_key dedupe doesn't cover the device_seq key
findings.location: ferment_readings/mill_water_log `unique (device_id, device_seq)` (lines 127, 160) vs the RPCs' on-conflict only on idempotency_key (lines 305, 354)
findings.problem: Both append RPCs dedupe with `on conflict (idempotency_key) do nothing`, but the ledgers ALSO carry `unique (device_id, device_seq)`. If two DISTINCT writes (different idempotency keys) arrive with the SAME (device_id, device_seq) — which the server action permits, since randomDeviceSeq() is an independent 48-bit draw per call with no monotonic guarantee, and the form may also pass an explicit deviceSeq — the insert misses the idempotency_key conflict target and instead violates the device_seq unique, raising a raw 23505 that aborts the txn. The reading row is rolled back AND no lot_event lands. So the (device_id,device_seq) key is fail-closed (no corruption, good) but the failure is an ugly unique_violation surfaced as the generic 'That reading was already recorded' (recordFermentReading.ts friendlyRpcError maps any 23505 to the duplicate message) — misleading, since it is a seq clash, not a true replay. Confirmed via PGlite: forcing two readings with device_seq=55555 and different idempotency keys -> READING-SAME-SEQ-ERROR: duplicate key value violates unique constraint "ferment_readings_device_id_device_seq_key"; readings landed: 1.
findings.scenario: A 48-bit random collision is astronomically rare in practice, but the form path explicitly allows a client-supplied deviceSeq (actions.ts withEnvelope honors raw.deviceSeq), and the BLE-probe path the schema is built for will mint many readings/second on one device_id — a per-second monotonic counter that wraps or repeats, or a buggy probe firmware, makes a same-seq collision plausible, at which point the reading is silently dropped with a wrong error message.
findings.fix: Either (a) drop `unique (device_id, device_seq)` on the readings/water ledgers and rely solely on idempotency_key for exactly-once (the (device_id,device_seq) key is a phase-1 lot_event replay-safety device that doesn't map cleanly to these high-frequency series), or (b) catch unique_violation on the device_seq key inside the RPC and retry/return the existing row, and have friendlyRpcError distinguish a true idempotency replay from a device_seq clash.

findings.title: Stray throwaway DB repro test left in the suite (multi-agent drift in the shared worktree)
findings.location: src/test/db/ — also saw _s1repro.db.test.ts, and _probe_s3 / _zz_repro_devseq appearing then vanishing mid-review
findings.problem: The db test directory contains throwaway repro scratch files (zzz_s3_repro.db.test.ts created 10:26 during this review; _s1repro.db.test.ts) that assert `expect(true).toBe(true)` and only console.log — they are not real regression tests and pollute the local quality gate (npm run test). During this review the files _probe_s3.db.test.ts and _zz_repro_devseq.db.test.ts appeared and were deleted by a parallel session writing into the SAME worktree, which is exactly the no-shared-worktree drift the project rules warn against. FLAGGING per scope discipline — not fixing, since these files are outside this slice's authored scope and another agent is actively editing them.
findings.scenario: Run `npm run test`: these scratch specs run as part of the gate, add noise, and (because a parallel agent is creating/deleting them live) can flip the suite between green and a file-not-found/transform error race.
findings.fix: Delete the zzz_*/_*-prefixed throwaway repro specs once their findings are captured (the device_seq collision they demonstrate is now documented above with its own permanent regression test owed), and ensure only one agent writes into this worktree at a time.

findings.title: Reposo gate is fully bypassable via the `parchment` intermediate stage — a wet, never-rested lot reaches `milled`
findings.location: L442 (RPC gate `cur_stage='drying' and p_to_stage='milled'`) and L477 (trigger `old.stage='drying' and new.stage='milled'`)
findings.problem: The load-bearing reposo invariant fires ONLY on the exact `drying`→`milled` transition, in BOTH enforcement layers. But the real pipeline enum is `('cherry','fermentation','drying','parchment','milled','green')` (20260620120000_init.sql:17) — and `parchment` is literally labelled 'Resting in pergamino' in stage-pipeline.tsx:42, i.e. parchment IS the reposo rest. The canonical path out of drying is `drying → parchment → milled`, never the direct `drying → milled` jump the gate guards. So a lot advances `drying → parchment` (gate not fired: to_stage≠milled), then `parchment → milled` (gate not fired: cur_stage='parchment'≠'drying'), and the soaking-wet, zero-rest lot is milled with `v_reposo_status.ready=false`. The forward-only/no-mass-gain guards permit both hops. The DATA-LAYER invariant the entire slice exists to enforce is bypassed through the normal happy-path UI sequence, not even a hostile direct UPDATE.
findings.scenario: Confirmed with a PGlite repro on the real migrations: mint lot → advance to 'drying' → record_moisture_reading(20.0%) (soaking wet) → direct `advance_processing_stage(...,'milled')` correctly RAISES 'reposo gate'. Then `advance_processing_stage(...,'parchment')` succeeds, and `advance_processing_stage(...,'milled')` succeeds — final stage='milled', v_reposo_status.ready=false. Identical result via two direct `update lots set stage=...` statements (trigger backstop also passes because old.stage='parchment'). The existing p2s4 test suite only ever tests the direct drying→milled edge, so it is green while the gate is wide open.
findings.fix: Gate the boundary the rest actually crosses, not a single enum-adjacent pair. Enforce reposo readiness on ANY transition INTO 'milled' (and 'green') from any pre-mill stage, e.g. `if new.stage in ('milled','green') and coalesce(nullif(old.stage,''),'cherry') < 'milled'::batch_stage then <check reposo_status(new.code).ready>`. Mirror the same condition inside advance_processing_stage (gate when `p_to_stage::batch_stage >= 'milled'` and the lot has not yet passed the gate). Equivalently, stamp a `reposo_cleared_at` on the lot when the gate first passes and require it set before `milled`/`green`. Add a regression test that drives drying→parchment→milled on an unrested lot and asserts it RAISES.

findings.title: `record_moisture_reading` writes NO `lot_event` — the gate's own evidence lives off the hash-chained ledger, breaking the slice's write-door contract
findings.location: L304-338 (record_moisture_reading body — no `perform record_lot_event(...)`)
