findings.problem: The directly-analogous sibling RPC `record_ferment_reading` (20260622092000_fermentation.sql:314) appends a `'ferment_reading'` lot_event in the same txn — the slice's stated contract is 'every write mutates the domain row AND appends a lot_event' (fermentation.sql:15-18). `record_moisture_reading` inserts only into `moisture_readings` and never calls `record_lot_event`. The migration header even claims it 'Reuses the lot_event substrate semantics' and 'The drying curve is EVIDENCE' (L139-141), but the curve is NOT on the hash chain. moisture_readings has its own block trigger + `unique(device_id,device_seq)`, but those device_seq values are in a SEPARATE namespace from lot_event, so the readings carry no chained prev_hash/hash and no causal position relative to the stage_advance that gates on them. The single most compliance-critical evidence for the cup (the reposo moisture curve, Best-of-Panama) is the one reading-type left out of the tamper-evident ledger.
findings.scenario: Replayed migrations + recorded a moisture reading via the RPC, then `select count(*) from lot_event where kind like '%moisture%'` → 0. By contrast a ferment reading yields a `ferment_reading` lot_event. There is no verify_chain coverage of moisture readings and no append on the lot stream when a reading lands.
findings.fix: Append a `'moisture_reading'` lot_event in record_moisture_reading exactly as record_ferment_reading does: `perform record_lot_event(p_lot_code,'moisture_reading',jsonb_build_object('moisture_pct',p_moisture_pct), p_occurred_at,p_device_id,p_device_seq,'moisture:'||p_idempotency_key)`. Namespace the event idempotency key (as ferment does with 'ferment-reading:'||key) so the moisture device_seq and the lot_event device_seq don't have to be the same counter.

findings.title: A `(device_id, device_seq)` collision is mis-mapped to a benign 'already recorded' message — a silently LOST moisture reading
findings.location: L120-124 (friendlyRpcError: 23505 / 'duplicate key' / 'unique constraint' → 'That reading was already recorded — refresh to see it.')
findings.problem: `moisture_readings` has BOTH `unique(idempotency_key)` and `unique(device_id, device_seq)`. The RPC's exactly-once short-circuit keys ONLY on idempotency_key. If two genuinely-different readings (different lot, different value, different idempotency_key) arrive with the same `(device_id, device_seq)` — e.g. two offline tablets sharing a device_id, or a per-process counter that reset/forked — the second INSERT throws `duplicate key ... moisture_readings_device_id_device_seq_key` (raw 23505), NOT a replay. recordMoisture.ts then matches code 23505 and tells the family 'That reading was already recorded — refresh to see it.' The reading was NOT recorded; it was rejected and lost, and the message tells the user everything is fine. Because moisture is the gate's evidence, a dropped-but-reported-OK reading can leave a lot looking unstable (or, worse, the wrong value standing as 'latest').
findings.scenario: PGlite repro: record_moisture_reading(lotA,11.0,'tablet-1',500,'key-a') succeeds; record_moisture_reading(lotB,12.0,'tablet-1',500,'key-b') RAISES 'duplicate key value violates unique constraint "moisture_readings_device_id_device_seq_key"'. The TS friendly mapper classifies this 23505 as the replay case → success-shaped 'already recorded' message, no error surfaced, lotB's 12.0 reading silently dropped.
findings.fix: Distinguish the two unique violations by constraint name, not by SQLSTATE. In friendlyRpcError, only treat `idempotency_key`/`record_moisture_reading_idempotency...` violations as 'already recorded'; treat a `device_id_device_seq` violation as a real, surfaced error ('This reading clashed with another from the same device — its sequence number was reused; re-sync the tablet.'). Better: have the RPC itself catch unique_violation on the device-seq constraint and re-raise a distinct, mappable errcode/message so a device-counter collision can never masquerade as a replay.

findings.title: NULL `idempotency_key` defeats exactly-once in `record_moisture_reading` — duplicate readings insert as separate rows
findings.location: L320-333 (`select id ... where idempotency_key = p_idempotency_key` + `unique(idempotency_key)` at L151)
findings.problem: The replay short-circuit is `where idempotency_key = p_idempotency_key`; when p_idempotency_key IS NULL this predicate is NULL (never true), so the short-circuit never fires. The `unique(idempotency_key)` constraint permits multiple NULLs in Postgres, and `on conflict (idempotency_key) do nothing` does not fire for NULLs either. So two calls with a NULL key insert TWO rows. The TS validator rejects empty keys, but the SECURITY DEFINER RPC is granted to `authenticated` and callable directly via PostgREST `.rpc()` (and is invoked server-side), and neither the parameter nor the column is NOT NULL. An offline outbox that forgets to mint a key, or a direct caller, silently double-records — skewing the 'latest reading' and the in-band-window count the gate reads.
findings.scenario: PGlite repro: two record_moisture_reading calls with p_idempotency_key=NULL and different device_seq → `select count(*) from moisture_readings where idempotency_key is null` = 2 (no error). Compare to two calls with the same non-null key → 1 row, same id.
findings.fix: Make idempotency_key NOT NULL on the column (and/or the parameter), or guard at the top of the RPC: `if p_idempotency_key is null then raise exception 'idempotency_key required' using errcode='not_null_violation'`. The hash-chained lot_event append (finding above) should carry the same requirement so NULL keys can never reach either ledger.

findings.title: `assign_drying_station` reports an unknown lot as 'no declared mass' — wrong error mapping, no existence check
findings.location: L365-369 (`select coalesce(current_kg, origin_kg) into lot_kg from lots where code=p_lot_code; if lot_kg is null then raise 'no declared mass'`)
findings.problem: assign_drying_station never checks the lot EXISTS before the mass lookup. For an unknown lot code, `select ... from lots where code=<nope>` returns no row, lot_kg is NULL, and the function raises `cannot assign station: lot <code> has no declared mass` with errcode check_violation. The TS friendlyRpcError (assignStation.ts:78-80) matches /no declared mass/ and returns 'This lot has no recorded weight yet — record its drying weight before assigning a bed.' For a typo'd or nonexistent lot the family is told to go record a weight on a lot that does not exist. The FK-violation branch (assignStation.ts:75) that would say 'that lot doesn't exist' is never reached because the missing-row case is intercepted by the mass check first.
findings.scenario: PGlite repro: assign_drying_station('JC-NOPE','st-bed-1',now()) → 'cannot assign station: lot JC-NOPE has no declared mass'. recordMoisture's sibling guards on lot existence with an explicit foreign_key_violation (record_moisture_reading L324-326); assign_drying_station does not.
findings.fix: Add an explicit existence check before the mass lookup, mirroring record_moisture_reading: `if not exists (select 1 from lots where code=p_lot_code) then raise exception 'unknown lot %', p_lot_code using errcode='foreign_key_violation'; end if;` Then the 23503/unknown path in friendlyRpcError fires and the family gets 'pick one from the list.'

findings.title: Moisture mirror `update processing_batches` is unscoped and re-stamps on the lost-insert race
findings.location: L334-336 (`update processing_batches set moisture_pct = p_moisture_pct where lot_code = p_lot_code`)
findings.problem: `processing_batches.lot_code` is only indexed, not unique (init.sql:92), so a lot can have multiple batch rows; this statement stamps the new reading onto EVERY batch row for the lot, not the active drying batch. It also runs unconditionally AFTER the on-conflict insert, including the branch where the INSERT lost the idempotency race and new_id was resolved from the pre-existing row (L331-333) — re-applying a possibly-stale/duplicate p_moisture_pct to the flat column even though no new reading was actually appended. The flat column is the Phase-1 read; this can leave it reflecting a value that was never freshly recorded, and can clobber a more-recent reading if calls arrive out of occurred_at order (the update uses the call's value, not the latest by occurred_at).
findings.scenario: Two batch rows for one lot both get moisture_pct overwritten by any reading. And a duplicate/raced submit (same idempotency_key) still executes the mirror update on its way to returning the existing id, re-stamping the flat column.
findings.fix: Scope the mirror to the active drying batch (e.g. the latest/open batch for the lot) and skip it on the replay/race branch (return the existing id before the update). Better: derive the flat moisture_pct from a 'latest reading by occurred_at' view rather than imperatively stamping it, so out-of-order or duplicate submits can't regress it.

findings.title: The deploy cache-bust invariant (CACHE_VERSION bump → stale-shell purge) has ZERO test — the slice's own highest-risk footgun is unpinned
findings.location: public/sw.js:16 (CACHE_VERSION) + 57-71 (activate purge); sw-strategy.test.ts (no coverage)
findings.problem: The spec names "the SW must cache-bust on deploy" as an invariant, and sw.js implements it two ways: (1) CACHE_VERSION="janson-v1" must be bumped every release, and (2) the `activate` handler deletes every cache key not starting with CACHE_VERSION. NEITHER is tested. No test reads public/sw.js at all (confirmed: grep for sw.js in *.test.* finds only the strategy unit test + the registrar's register() assertion). The activate-purge logic, the message/SKIP_WAITING handler, and the install precache are entirely uncovered. Worse, the only thing that triggers the purge is a human remembering to bump the literal "v1" string — there is no test that fails when CACHE_VERSION is stale across a deploy. If the guard (the version bump) is silently dropped, every test stays green while field devices serve a stale app chunk → white screen, the exact footgun the file's own header warns about.
findings.scenario: A future build changes a _next/static chunk but the dev forgets to bump CACHE_VERSION (or a refactor breaks the activate filter). The full suite (48 tests) passes. On deploy, returning field devices keep the old precached "/" shell + runtime-cached chunks, the new HTML references new hashed filenames that the old shell never requests → blank screen for the Ngäbe-Buglé crew in a Volcán dead zone, with no error and no failing test.
findings.fix: Add a node test that (a) reads public/sw.js as text and asserts the activate handler deletes caches not prefixed by CACHE_VERSION, and (b) a release-discipline guard: extract CACHE_VERSION and assert it is bumped relative to a committed baseline, or derive it from package.json version / a build hash so it can't go stale. Ideally also a jsdom/@vitest test driving the real sw.js handlers (install→cache.addAll, activate→old-cache deleted) via a fake CacheStorage, so the side-effecting code — not just the pure mirror — is exercised.

findings.title: Tests pin the pure sw-strategy.ts reimplementation, NOT the public/sw.js that actually ships — the two-copy drift is unguarded
findings.location: sw-strategy.test.ts:1-53 (tests src/lib/offline/sw-strategy.ts) vs public/sw.js:26-46 (a hand-copied duplicate)
findings.problem: sw.js says "The routing DECISION mirrors src/lib/offline/sw-strategy.ts ... Keep the two in sync; this file is the spec the test pins." But the test imports and verifies @/lib/offline/sw-strategy — a SEPARATE copy. public/sw.js has its OWN inlined chooseStrategy/isStaticAsset/isApiOrAuth (lines 26-46). Nothing asserts the two implementations agree. The "never cache a write" invariant is verified only on the copy that does NOT run in the browser. The shipped sw.js could diverge (e.g. someone edits isApiOrAuth in only one place) and every test stays green while the real SW caches a Supabase write or a stale RSC document.
findings.scenario: A maintainer adds `/functions/v1/` to isApiOrAuth in sw-strategy.ts (and its test passes) but forgets public/sw.js. A queued command replay POST is correctly network-only (method gate fires first), but a *GET* to an edge function gets served stale-while-revalidate from cache in the field while the unit test is green — the test verified a file that isn't deployed.
findings.fix: Either (a) generate public/sw.js's strategy block from sw-strategy.ts at build (single source), or (b) add a parity guard test that reads public/sw.js as text and asserts the chooseStrategy/isStaticAsset/isApiOrAuth bodies are byte-identical to the exported source (or runs the same table of url/method cases through a sandboxed eval of the sw.js functions). Without this, the 'mirror' comment is an honor-system invariant a test claims to pin but doesn't.

findings.title: The SW update → SKIP_WAITING path (the in-app half of the cache-bust) is mocked into a no-op and never executes in any test
findings.location: sw-register.test.tsx:26-32 (register returns {} , addEventListener: vi.fn()); sw-register.tsx:30-46 (updatefound→statechange→postMessage SKIP_WAITING)
findings.problem: The registrar's whole reason to add an `updatefound` listener is to fire SKIP_WAITING so a new build's shell takes over without a manual reload — the client half of the deploy cache-bust. The test stubs serviceWorker as `{ register, addEventListener: vi.fn() }` and `register` resolves `{}` (no `.installing`, no `.addEventListener`, no `updatefound`). So the entire updatefound/statechange/postMessage branch (sw-register.tsx:33-44) is dead code under test — never invoked, never asserted. The test only proves register('/sw.js') was called. If the SKIP_WAITING logic were deleted or broken (e.g. wrong state string, missing controller check), the suite stays green and field devices silently keep the old SW until every tab is closed.
findings.scenario: A refactor changes `installing.state === "installed"` to a wrong value or drops the postMessage. Tests pass. After a deploy the new SW installs but waits forever (no SKIP_WAITING), so the picker keeps running last week's bundle — and the bug ships because no test drives the update lifecycle.
findings.fix: Add a test that returns a registration with a fake `installing` worker and a real (synchronous) event emitter, fire `updatefound` then `statechange` with state='installed' and a controller present, and assert `installing.postMessage('SKIP_WAITING')` was called. Also cover the flag-OFF early-return (currently no test sets NEXT_PUBLIC_OFFLINE=off to prove the registrar no-ops).

findings.title: useSyncStatus().flush() and the drawer 'retry' never actually drain the queue — and useSyncStatus has no test at all
findings.location: useSyncStatus.ts:58-60 (retry/dismiss/flush all call engine.refresh, not a drain); no useSyncStatus.test.* exists
findings.problem: `flush: () => engine.refresh()` only RECOMPUTES the pending/dead counts — it does not call outbox.flush() or engine.drain(). Likewise `retry(uuid)` revives a dead-letter to 'queued' then calls engine.refresh() (not drain). So when the operator taps Retry on a dead-letter in the drawer while online, the entry is re-queued but NOT re-sent — it sits 'pending' until an unrelated `online` window event happens to fire engine.drain(). The engine exposes no public drain at all (sync.ts: only refresh is public; drain is internal, fired only by the onOnline listener and start()). This whole interaction layer — the operator's ONLY manual recovery path for a dead-letter — has zero tests (useSyncStatus.test.* does not exist; the island test only checks the pill renders + the drawer opens, with a transport that's never driven through a retry).
findings.scenario: A weigh-in dead-letters (reposo gate) in the field. The operator fixes the lot, opens the drawer, taps Retry. The entry flips dead→queued and the pill shows 'pending', but nothing is sent — the count just sits there. The picker assumes it synced and walks away; the write only goes through if/when the OS later emits an 'online' event. The slice's reassurance contract ('your weigh-in is safe, retry works') is quietly false, and no test would catch it.
findings.fix: (1) Make retry/flush actually drain: add an `engine.drain()` to the public SyncEngine surface (or have outbox.retry be followed by outbox.flush() when online) and wire the drawer's Retry/Flush to it. (2) Add a useSyncStatus test (render hook with a stubbed runtime) that: enqueues→flush→dead-letters, calls retry(), and asserts the transport is invoked again and the entry reaches 'done'. This is the highest-value missing test in the slice — it covers a real, reachable bug on the primary recovery path.

findings.title: The offline kill-switch flag (offlineFlagEnabled / NEXT_PUBLIC_OFFLINE parsing) has zero tests — the exact env-inlining pattern the repo was bitten by
findings.location: flag.ts:20-39 (offlineFlagEnabled string parsing + offlineCapable + offlineEnabled); no flag.test.* (confirmed via grep)
findings.problem: flag.ts is the documented instant kill-switch for the whole substrate and the gate sw-register.tsx reads to decide whether to register the SW. offlineFlagEnabled parses NEXT_PUBLIC_OFFLINE with a hand-rolled `v !== 'off' && v !== 'false' && v !== '0'` rule, and the file's own comment cites the MEMORY Vite optional-chaining-inlining bug as the reason it's written a specific way. None of this is tested: no test stubs NEXT_PUBLIC_OFFLINE to 'off'/'false'/'0'/undefined and asserts the boolean, and no test covers offlineCapable's SSR (window undefined) / no-IndexedDB branches or offlineEnabled's AND. The kill-switch could silently stop working (e.g. a typo making 'off' not disable, or the env not inlining) and the whole field rollout could be un-killable, with a fully green suite.
findings.scenario: A field issue appears; the operator sets NEXT_PUBLIC_OFFLINE=off to disable the substrate. A regression (or the inlining gotcha) makes the parse return true anyway, so the SW keeps registering and the outbox keeps queuing — the kill-switch is dead, exactly the 'a dead guard is itself an incident' failure mode, with no test to catch it.
findings.fix: Add a flag.test.ts using vi.stubEnv('NEXT_PUBLIC_OFFLINE', ...) over the matrix {undefined,'on','off','false','0',''} asserting offlineFlagEnabled, plus tests for offlineCapable (no-window → false; no-indexedDB → false) and offlineEnabled's AND. Cheap, and it pins the one switch the whole slice's graceful degradation hinges on.

findings.title: runtime.ts router transport (network-error vs rejected classification, the load-bearing distinction) is untested
findings.location: runtime.ts:46-78 (routerTransport: unregistered→rejected, action throw→network-error, ok→ok); no runtime.test.*
findings.problem: The slice's single most load-bearing rule is that a business rejection becomes a visible dead-letter while a network failure stays retryably queued. transport.ts (the Server-Action bridge) has a good test for this — but runtime.ts contains a SECOND, independent transport (routerTransport) that the real app actually wires into the singleton outbox (getRuntime), and it re-implements the same three-way classification: unregistered rpc → rejected, action resolves !ok → rejected, action throws → network-error. None of runtime.ts is tested (no runtime.test.*). The dead-letter-on-unregistered-rpc behavior (important in S0, where NO rpc is registered yet, so every enqueued command must dead-letter with a clear message rather than vanish) is asserted nowhere. If routerTransport mis-classified a throw as 'rejected' (dead-lettering a transient network blip forever) the suite stays green.
findings.scenario: In S0 the substrate is 'inert-but-ready'; if any surface enqueues before its rpc is registered, the command must land as a visible dead-letter. A regression that returns {kind:'ok'} for an unregistered rpc (silently dropping the write) — the precise 'never silently lost' violation — passes all 48 tests.
findings.fix: Add runtime.test.ts: register a fake action and assert ok/rejected/network-error mapping; assert an UNregistered rpc dead-letters with the 'No handler registered' message (and is therefore retryable once a later slice registers it); assert getRuntime() returns a stable singleton and that offlineEnabled=false selects the memory store. This closes the gap between the well-tested transport.ts copy and the copy that actually ships in the runtime.

findings.title: The QC-HOLD teeth (_prevent_held_lot_commit) fire only BEFORE INSERT — an UPDATE re-points a claim onto a held lot unchecked, asymmetric with the Phase-1 prevent_oversell precedent it claims to extend
findings.location: lines 217-223 (create trigger lot_reservations_prevent_held_commit / lot_shipments_prevent_held_commit — `before insert` only)
findings.problem: The slice's stated load-bearing invariant (lines 18-22) is 'a green lot under an OPEN qc_hold CANNOT be reserved or shipped … the gate is in the database', explicitly modeled on the Phase-1 prevent_oversell family. But prevent_oversell is declared `before insert OR UPDATE` (green_inventory.sql:135,139) precisely so the ceiling can't be bypassed by mutating an existing claim row; _prevent_held_lot_commit is `before insert` ONLY. So an UPDATE that re-points an existing lot_reservations/lot_shipments row's green_lot_code at a lot currently under an open hold sails straight through the teeth. This is a real defense-in-depth asymmetry with the sibling trigger it advertises itself as extending. Reachability today is bounded: authenticated clients only hold SELECT+INSERT on the claim tables (green_inventory.sql:305-306, no UPDATE grant), so a REST caller cannot reach the UPDATE path — hence MED, not CRIT. But the slice's own contract is that the data-layer gate holds 'even when a future code path mutates' the table, and a future slice that adds any UPDATE grant or owner-side mutation (e.g. a reservation-amend RPC) silently re-opens the hole.
findings.scenario: PROBE A (confirmed in PGlite against the full combined migration set): materialize JC-9011 (un-held) and reserve 10kg against it; place_qc_hold on JC-9001; then `update lot_reservations set green_lot_code='JC-9001' where green_lot_code='JC-9011'`. The UPDATE succeeds — the reservation now points at the held lot (final row: green_lot_code='JC-9001'), with NO exception from the teeth trigger. The Phase-1 prevent_oversell, by contrast, would have re-evaluated on the same UPDATE.
findings.fix: Change both triggers to `before insert or update on lot_reservations` / `lot_shipments` (mirroring green_inventory.sql:134-140). _prevent_held_lot_commit already keys off NEW.green_lot_code, so it works unchanged on UPDATE. Add a db.test.ts case that UPDATEs a claim onto a held lot and asserts it raises.

findings.title: v_cup_final_score double-counts a repeated attribute — no unique(session_id, attribute) and no supersede semantics, so a re-scored attribute inflates the headline cup score that drives the specialty band
findings.location: cupping_scores table (lines 64-75: no unique(session_id, attribute)); v_cup_final_score (lines 234-245: coalesce(sum(c.score),0))
findings.problem: cupping_scores is append-only (no UPDATE/DELETE — _qc_block_mutation, lines 145-147) and v_cup_final_score is `sum(c.score)` over ALL score rows in the session. There is no unique constraint on (session_id, attribute) and no supersede/latest-wins resolver. So if the same attribute is recorded twice in one session (a cupper fat-fingers acidity, notices, and re-enters it — the ONLY correction path on an append-only ledger), BOTH rows are summed. The final cup score — the number that maps through cupQualityBand / the green_lots.sca_grade vocabulary (Presidential/Specialty/Premium) and ultimately the price a Best-of-Panama Geisha micro-lot sells under — is silently inflated. The append-only design forbids the obvious fix (UPDATE the wrong row), yet the slice ships no superseding-row resolver, so the only way to 'correct' a score makes the total worse.
findings.scenario: PROBE G (confirmed in PGlite): one sca-cva session; record_cup_score(sid,'flavor',8,…,'g-1') then record_cup_score(sid,'flavor',8,…,'g-2') (same attribute, distinct idempotency keys — both legal, both accepted). v_cup_final_score returns final_score=16, attribute_count=2 for a single 8-point flavor attribute. A 62-point CVA lot can be pushed to 70+ by re-entering attributes, crossing band boundaries.
findings.fix: Either (a) add `unique (session_id, attribute)` on cupping_scores so a duplicate attribute is rejected at the data layer and the cupper must open a new session to re-cup, or (b) keep append-only but make v_cup_final_score latest-wins per attribute (e.g. distinct on (session_id, attribute) ordered by id desc, then sum) and document the supersede semantics. Add a db.test.ts that scores an attribute twice and asserts the final reflects exactly one value per attribute.

findings.title: v_qc_status.latest_cup_score is non-deterministic on tied occurred_at (no id tiebreaker) — same-day cuppings can show the wrong lot score in the QC banner
findings.location: v_qc_status, lines 288-293 (subquery `order by s2.occurred_at desc limit 1`)
findings.problem: The latest-cup-score subquery orders only by `s2.occurred_at desc limit 1` with no secondary key. occurred_at is the field wall-clock (often coarse — a cupper logs 'today' or a minute-granular offline timestamp), so two sessions on the same lot frequently share an occurred_at. When they tie, Postgres returns an arbitrary row, so the QC banner / v_qc_status.latest_cup_score the UI reads (src/lib/db/qc.ts getQcStatus) is non-deterministic and can display the lower score when the higher is the real latest, or vice-versa. The Phase-1 sibling reposo_status correctly uses `order by mr.occurred_at desc, mr.id desc` (drying_reposo.sql:211,221), and green_lots itself stamps graded_at — so the precedent for a deterministic tiebreaker is established in the same codebase and was dropped here.
findings.scenario: PROBE F (confirmed in PGlite): two cupping_sessions on JC-9031 with identical occurred_at '2026-01-01T00:00:00Z', totals 5 and 9. v_qc_status.latest_cup_score returned 5 — but with no tiebreaker the planner could equally return 9; the displayed 'latest' score does not track which session was actually recorded last.
findings.fix: Add the id tiebreaker: `order by s2.occurred_at desc, s2.id desc limit 1` (matching reposo_status). cupping_sessions.id is the generated-always identity so it is the monotonic insert-order key. Add a db.test.ts with two tied-occurred_at sessions asserting the higher-id session's score is reported.

findings.title: release_qc_hold relies on the mutation guard to reject a NULL occurred_at instead of defaulting it — a release with NULL silently leaves the lot HELD
findings.location: release_qc_hold lines 466-470 (`set released_at = p_occurred_at … where released_at is null`) interacting with _qc_holds_guard_mutation lines 181-184
findings.problem: release_qc_hold stamps `released_at = p_occurred_at` with no `coalesce(p_occurred_at, now())`. If p_occurred_at is NULL, the UPDATE tries to set released_at=NULL, which _qc_holds_guard_mutation rejects ('a qc_holds update must stamp a release'). The whole release transaction then raises and the hold stays OPEN. Every other timestamped writer in the spine defaults its clock (record_lot_event etc. via the column default / coalesce); release_qc_hold does not, so the function's correctness depends entirely on the caller never passing NULL. The TS wrapper (placeQcHold.ts:100-103) defaults occurredAt before the RPC, so the standard path is safe — hence LOW — but the documented QcHoldStore.rpc surface accepts arbitrary args, and a direct `.rpc('release_qc_hold',{p_occurred_at:null})` or a future caller that forwards a null wall-clock fails to release with a confusing 'must stamp a release' error rather than just releasing now.
findings.scenario: PROBE D (confirmed in PGlite): place a hold, then `select release_qc_hold('JC-9001', NULL, 'srv', 2, 'relD')` raises 'a qc_holds update must stamp a release (released_at)'; the open-hold count stays at 1 and a subsequent reservation is still blocked — the lot is stuck held.
findings.fix: Default the clock inside the RPC: `set released_at = coalesce(p_occurred_at, now())`. The DEFINER body is the right place since release is naturally idempotent on the open-hold predicate. Add a db.test.ts calling release with NULL occurred_at and asserting the hold is released.

findings.title: release_qc_hold does not advance the device_seq space of qc_holds — re-holding a lot with a previously-used (device_id, device_seq) crashes on the UNIQUE, which the release path never consumes
findings.location: qc_holds `unique (device_id, device_seq)` line 113; release_qc_hold lines 466-473 (stamps in place, writes no new row / no seq)
findings.problem: qc_holds carries `unique (device_id, device_seq)` (replay safety, copied from the event-ledger idiom). But unlike the lot_event ledgers where every state change is a NEW append (a fresh seq), a hold release is an in-place column stamp that consumes NO device_seq. The hold/release lifecycle therefore writes seqs only on place_qc_hold, while the offline outbox/device assigns a monotonically increasing seq to every operation it emits including releases. If a device's seq counter ever reuses a value against qc_holds (e.g. a place after a release whose seq was reused, or two devices sharing a device_id), the second place_qc_hold INSERT raises `duplicate key value violates unique constraint qc_holds_device_id_device_seq_key` instead of being handled by the idempotency_key dedup. The (device_id, device_seq) UNIQUE is a blunter dedup than idempotency_key for a place-then-release-then-place sequence.
findings.scenario: PROBE B (confirmed in PGlite): place_qc_hold(seq=50,key=k1) → release_qc_hold(seq=51) → place_qc_hold(seq=50,key=k2) raises duplicate key on qc_holds_device_id_device_seq_key; the new hold is NOT placed (open holds = 0) even though k2 is a fresh, legitimate hold.
findings.fix: This is a design-consistency flag: either (a) accept that holds are place-once/release-once and document that the device must always emit a fresh seq per place (matching the ledger contract), or (b) if release should consume a seq, model the release as a separate append row rather than an in-place stamp. At minimum add a db.test.ts pinning the intended behavior so the seq contract is explicit.

findings.title: Other agents' throwaway repro test files left committed-untracked in src/test/db (_rt_s6seq, _rt_s6sess, _s1repro) — they run under the |db| vitest project on every suite
findings.location: src/test/db/_rt_s6seq.db.test.ts, _rt_s6sess.db.test.ts, _s1repro.db.test.ts (untracked)
findings.problem: While red-teaming I found three leftover throwaway probe files in src/test/db matching the `*.db.test.ts` include glob, so they execute as part of the real DB suite (each spins a full PGlite migration replay, ~seconds each). These are not mine (I deleted all of my own probes); they appear to be from parallel agents working other slices. They are out of my assigned scope so I am FLAGGING, not deleting them per the scope-discipline rule. Two of the three names (_rt_s6seq, _rt_s6sess) reference S6 so they may be intended S6 coverage that was never finalized/renamed.
findings.scenario: `git status` shows them as untracked; `npx vitest run` under the |db| project picks them up via the `src/**/*.db.test.ts` include. They add migration-replay time to every run and risk shipping debug assertions into the suite the quality gate depends on.
findings.fix: Confirm with the slice owners whether _rt_s6seq/_rt_s6sess are real S6 tests to be renamed and kept (drop the `_rt_` prefix, add proper assertions) or deleted; delete _s1repro after the S1 agent confirms. Do not leave underscore-prefixed scratch files under the test glob.

findings.title: Sync pill + outbox drawer are English-only — the design mandates bilingual (es/ngäbere) on this field-facing surface
findings.location: sync-status.tsx:42-85 (VISUALS labels/aria) and sync-status-island.tsx:101-208 (drawer copy)
findings.problem: The design's cross-slice UI rail (PHASE2-DESIGN.md line 342) explicitly enumerates "the sync pill (S0)" among the surfaces that must ship "bilingual where field-facing", and S0's own dogfood framing is "the picker in a dead zone" — i.e. a ~90% Ngäbe-Buglé/Spanish-speaking crew. Every user-visible string in the slice is hard-coded English: pill labels "Up to date / Queued / Syncing / Offline / Needs attention", all aria strings ("Synced — everything is saved to the server", "Offline — … will sync when you reconnect"), and the drawer copy ("Sync activity", "All caught up", "Connected — changes save automatically", "Needs attention", "Waiting to sync", "Retry", "Dismiss"). The manifest declares lang "es" (manifest.webmanifest:11) but the root <html lang="en"> (layout.tsx:48) and the UI is English. There is no i18n seam, no Spanish copy, nothing in Ngäbere.
findings.scenario: A picker or supervisor whose working language is Spanish/Ngäbere loses signal at 1,700 masl, glances at the one piece of always-visible chrome meant to reassure them their weigh-in is safe, and reads only English. The reassurance the slice exists to deliver does not land for the exact crew it was designed for.
findings.fix: Introduce a tiny copy map keyed by locale (es default, en fallback; ngäbere strings where the family can supply them) and route every VISUALS label/aria + drawer string through it; set <html lang> from the same locale and align it with the manifest's "es". At minimum ship Spanish as the default since manifest already declares es. Add a render test asserting the Spanish label renders for the offline state.

findings.title: Offline app-shell load — the slice's #1 de-risk requirement — does not actually work: precached `/` is auth-gated, force-dynamic, and middleware-redirected
findings.location: sw.js:23 (PRECACHE_URLS=["/", …]) vs src/app/(app)/layout.tsx:7 (force-dynamic) + src/lib/supabase/middleware.ts:37-46 (getUser()+redirect) + src/lib/auth/redirect.ts
findings.problem: The spec's highest-risk note and PROBE P2-SPIKE-A item (a) make "offline app-shell load" the load-bearing thing S0 must prove. The SW precaches `/` at install (sw.js:23) and serves navigations stale-while-revalidate from cache. But `/` is rendered by the route group `(app)/page.tsx` under `(app)/layout.tsx` which is `export const dynamic = "force-dynamic"`, and EVERY request to `/` first passes through middleware that calls `supabase.auth.getUser()` (a network round-trip) and `authRedirect()` which 302-redirects `/` → `/login` whenever the session isn't valid. So at SW install, `cache.addAll(["/", …])` fetches `/` with `redirect:"follow"` and caches the `/login` document (or fails the whole atomic addAll if it 4xx/5xx's), meaning the precached "shell" under key `/` is the login page, not the app. And the app content is fully server-rendered dynamic data that cannot render offline at all. The dogfood-prerequisite "offline app-shell load" is therefore not achievable as built.
findings.scenario: Farm manager installs the PWA, then later goes offline and opens the app expecting the field-trustworthy shell. The SW serves the cached `/` — which was captured as the login redirect at install — or a stale dynamic page that can't hydrate its server data offline. The very thing the PROBE was supposed to verify before promoting the skeleton silently does not hold.
findings.fix: Add a dedicated static app-shell route (e.g. `/offline` or a static shell page) that renders without a live session/data, precache THAT, and have the SW serve it as the navigation fallback (the catch in staleWhileRevalidate's `caches.match("/")`). Exclude the auth-revalidating middleware from the SW-served navigation path, and don't precache an auth-gated dynamic `/`. Verify with an actual offline-load test (the PROBE item (a)) before treating S0 as landed.

findings.title: Dogfood moment is unreachable — the entire substrate is inert; nothing enqueues and no action can be drained to the server
findings.location: runtime.ts:38-43 (empty registry, registerCommand never called) — confirmed by grep: no caller of getEnqueueCommand/registerCommand/enqueueCommand outside src/lib/offline
findings.problem: S0's stated dogfood moment is concrete and demoable WITHIN the slice: "the farm manager turns off wifi, records three dummy actions, watches the sync pill say '3 queued', turns wifi back on, and sees them drain to the server with zero duplicates." As built, the substrate is fully inert: `registry` is empty, `registerCommand` is exported but called by nobody, and no UI surface calls `getEnqueueCommand()`. There is no dummy/seed action wired anywhere (grep for any caller returns nothing). So a manager cannot record even one action — the pill can only ever show "synced". Worse, even if something enqueued, `routerTransport` finds no handler and dead-letters it as a BUSINESS rejection (runtime.ts:50-57) — so the "3 queued → drain clean" demo would instead produce 3 dead-letters. The slice's own definition-of-done demo cannot be performed.
findings.scenario: Andres/the family follow the design's dogfood script on the deployed app to decide whether to trust the system in the field — and find no button anywhere produces a queued action; the pill never leaves "Up to date". The trust-building moment the family must believe before capturing a real lata offline is not achievable until a later slice (S2) ships.
findings.fix: Either (a) ship a minimal S0-local "dogfood" capture (a no-op/echo Server Action registered for a `dogfood_ping` rpc that the manager can fire from a hidden dev affordance) so the queued→drain→zero-dup loop is demoable inside S0 as the spec promises, or (b) explicitly down-scope the dogfood claim in the slice doc to "demoable once S2 registers the first rpc". As-is the slice claims a capability it cannot demonstrate.

findings.title: Client device identity (getDeviceId/nextDeviceSeq) is built but never wired into the enqueue path — and contradicts the server-minted device_id the field actions already use
findings.location: device.ts:30-48 (getDeviceId/nextDeviceSeq) — getDeviceId never called by runtime.ts/enqueueCommand.ts; outbox.enqueue requires caller-supplied deviceId (outbox.ts:33-34,124-138)
findings.problem: The spec's device-identity contract: "`device_id` is a per-install UUID in IndexedDB; `device_seq` is a monotonic per-device counter — together they give the causal ordering the lot_event schema reserved." device.ts implements exactly this. But nothing wires it in: `outbox.enqueue` auto-stamps `deviceSeq` via `nextDeviceSeq(store)` (good) yet takes `deviceId` from the caller's `CommandInput` (outbox.ts:34), and neither `getRuntime()` nor `enqueueCommand()` ever calls `getDeviceId()` to populate it. So the per-install device_id is orphaned. Meanwhile the EXISTING field actions mint device identity server-side with a CONSTANT id and a RANDOM seq — ferment/actions.ts:43 `FERMENT_DEVICE_ID = "server-ferment"` and `randomDeviceSeq()` (a 48-bit random, not monotonic). The two schemes are mutually exclusive: if a queued command replays through such an action, the client's (device_id, device_seq) causal pair is discarded and replaced by a server-random one — defeating the exact causal ordering S0's device.ts exists to provide, and the device.ts header's own stated goal ("continue the count instead of restarting … the exact serverless-cold-start bug the harvest action's randomDeviceSeq worked around").
findings.scenario: S2 (weigh) wires the outbox to a Server Action shaped like ferment/actions.ts. A picker's three offline weigh-ins replay; each gets a fresh server-side random device_seq and the constant "server-weigh" device_id, so the (device_id, device_seq) Lamport ordering the lot_event schema reserved is meaningless — two devices' events no longer interleave by a real per-device cursor. The investment device.ts represents is dead on arrival.
findings.fix: Resolve the device identity at the runtime seam: have `getRuntime()`/`enqueueCommand()` call `getDeviceId(store)` once and stamp it into every CommandInput, and make the field Server Actions ACCEPT the client-supplied deviceId/deviceSeq from the envelope (trusting the client pair) instead of overwriting with `server-ferment`/randomDeviceSeq(). Pick ONE authority for the causal pair — the client per-install identity, per the spec — and delete or gate the server-side minting to the no-envelope (online direct-tap) fallback only.

findings.title: No Server Action matches the outbox transport contract — the transport/envelope shape diverges from the repo's actual action convention
findings.location: transport.ts:19-26 (ServerAction returns {ok:true}|{ok:false}) and runtime.ts:21-28 (ActionFn same) vs src/app/(app)/ferment/actions.ts:32-35 (FermentActionState {status:'idle'|'success'|'error'})
findings.problem: S0 ships TWO transport bridges: `makeServerActionTransport` (transport.ts, exported but UNUSED by the runtime) and the runtime's own `routerTransport`. Both require actions shaped `(envelope:{rpc,args,occurredAt,deviceId,deviceSeq,idempotencyKey}) => Promise<{ok:true}|{ok:false;message?}>`. But every existing/Phase-2 field action in the repo is a `useActionState`-style form action returning `{status:'success'|'error'}` and taking `(prevState, FormData)` — NOT the envelope, NOT the {ok} shape (ferment/actions.ts is representative). So when a later capture slice tries to register its action, it cannot pass one as-is; an adapter must be written that doesn't exist, and the spec's "every later field-capture slice calls enqueueCommand() instead of the raw Server Action" port has no compatible action to call. The two divergent transport implementations (makeServerActionTransport vs routerTransport) also signal the seam wasn't settled.
findings.scenario: S2 author wires the weigh action and discovers the outbox transport expects {ok} + an envelope arg, but the repo idiom is {status} + (prevState, FormData). They either bolt on a per-action adapter (untested seam, two conventions to keep in sync) or change the outbox — friction the foundation slice was supposed to remove.
findings.fix: Decide the one action shape the outbox replays against and align it with the repo's existing action convention (e.g. have makeServerActionTransport map the repo's {status:'error'} → rejected and a thrown/network failure → network-error, accepting (prevState,FormData) by serializing the envelope into FormData), delete the unused duplicate transport, and ship one worked example wiring a real-shaped action through the outbox so the contract is proven, not asserted.

findings.title: Transport classifies ALL thrown errors as retryable network-error — a server 4xx/5xx that throws (not a clean {ok:false}) is retried forever instead of dead-lettering
findings.location: transport.ts:57-62 (catch → network-error) and runtime.ts:70-75 (same)
findings.problem: The slice's single most load-bearing invariant: a business rejection must dead-letter and NEVER be confused with a retryable network failure. The transport encodes this purely by HOW the action signals: a clean `{ok:false}` → rejected/dead; a THROW → network-error/requeue. That is correct ONLY if field actions catch every deterministic server refusal and convert it to `{ok:false}`. But the repo's actions surface RPC CHECK/FK violations by translating them into a returned error state — which is fine — yet ANY uncaught throw (a Next.js Server Action invocation that 500s, a serialization error, a non-2xx the framework rethrows, an unanticipated Postgres exception not mapped to a friendly message) is classified network-error and requeued forever. A deterministically-failing command (e.g. a malformed-args bug, or an oversell that raised an exception the action forgot to catch) would be retried on every reconnect indefinitely, never surfacing in the dead-letter drawer — exactly the 'confused with a retryable failure / silently lost from the operator's view' outcome the invariant forbids.
findings.scenario: A weigh command hits a code path where the action's RPC raises an exception the action didn't map to {ok:false} (e.g. a new constraint added in a later slice). On every reconnect the outbox replays it, the action throws, the transport calls it network-error, it stays queued. The pill shows a perpetual 'pending' that never drains and never moves to 'needs attention' — the operator never learns it's dead.
findings.fix: Classify by the failure's nature, not solely the signaling mechanism: inspect the thrown error / HTTP status — a 4xx or a recognized deterministic error → rejected (dead-letter); only genuine transport failures (offline, network throw, 5xx/timeout) → network-error. Add an attempts cap that escalates a stuck 'queued' entry to dead-letter after N reconnect-retries so no command can be retried forever invisibly, and a test for 'an action that throws a deterministic 4xx dead-letters, an action that throws a network error stays queued.'

findings.title: Deploy cache-bust is dead: CACHE_VERSION is a hardcoded literal with no build-time bump, so activate() never purges the old shell
findings.location: public/sw.js:16 (const CACHE_VERSION = "janson-v1";) + activate handler :57-71; build = plain `next build` per package.json scripts; no rewrite of sw.js anywhere (grep confirms only literal usages)
findings.problem: The slice's stated invariant is "the SW must cache-bust on deploy" and the file's own comment claims "The cache version is bumped per release so a new deploy purges the old shell on activate." Nothing bumps it. `CACHE_VERSION` is the constant string "janson-v1", and `npm run build` is vanilla `next build` (next.config.mjs has no SW step, package.json has no codegen/replace). So across EVERY deploy the cache keys (`janson-v1-precache`, `janson-v1-runtime`) are identical, and the activate purge `keys.filter(k => !k.startsWith("janson-v1"))` matches nothing and deletes nothing. The documented deploy-time guard against the "stale chunk → white screen" footgun never runs. Per the standing rule, a guard that silently stops running is itself the incident. Compounding: `sw.js` is byte-identical build-over-build, so the browser's own update check never sees a changed SW → `updatefound` in sw-register.tsx:30 never fires → SKIP_WAITING is never posted either. The precached `/` shell (PRECACHE_URLS, :23) is therefore held cache-first/SWR across deploys with no version-keyed eviction; the ONLY thing saving the app from a hard white screen is Next's content-hashed chunk filenames (new shell requests new hashes, which miss-and-fetch). The moment a deploy changes anything served stale-while-revalidate or precached under a stable URL (the root document, the manifest, favicon, or any non-hashed asset), field devices at 1,700 masl keep the stale copy indefinitely — exactly the failure this mechanism was written to prevent.
findings.scenario: Deploy build N, a Ngäbe-Buglé weigh-station device installs the SW and precaches `/` + manifest under `janson-v1-*`. Deploy build N+1 (changed shell markup / changed manifest). On next visit: activate runs, `keys.filter(k => !k.startsWith("janson-v1"))` = [] → zero deletions; the old `/` and old manifest are still served from `janson-v1-*`; sw.js is unchanged so no update is detected and SKIP_WAITING never fires. The device runs the build-N shell against build-N+1 data until the cache is manually cleared.
findings.fix: Make the cache key change every deploy. Cheapest $0 path: at build time inject a real version into sw.js — e.g. a tiny `postbuild`/prebuild script (or a Next route that serves sw.js with the value templated) replacing `"janson-v1"` with the git SHA or `Date.now()`/build id, so PRECACHE/RUNTIME keys differ per release and `activate` actually evicts the prior version. Also add a `controllerchange` listener in sw-register.tsx that reloads the page once the new SW takes control, and ensure sw.js is served with `Cache-Control: no-cache` so the browser re-fetches the script and detects the change.

findings.title: flush() has no in-flight lock and the sync engine's drain() is non-reentrant-guarded — overlapping drains double-send the same queued command
findings.location: outbox.ts flush() :141-170 (snapshot taken once at :143, no in-flight/draining guard); sync.ts drain() :126-143 + onOnline :145-147 + start() :152-160 (drain() invoked with no re-entrancy guard, from both the initial start() and every `online` event)
findings.problem: The slice promises "exactly-once replay on idempotency_key," but the CLIENT does nothing to enforce once-ness across concurrent drains — it leans entirely on the downstream RPC's dedupe. `flush()` snapshots `allEntries()` once and then `await transport.send()` per entry with no lock; `drain()` likewise has no `if (draining) return` guard and is fired both by the initial `await drain()` in start() AND by every `online` window event. On flaky high-altitude connectivity the `online` event commonly fires repeatedly (or fires while the initial start() drain is still awaiting a slow send). Two `flush()` calls then run interleaved, both read the same entry as status `queued` (the first hasn't written `done` back yet — the status flip happens only AFTER the awaited send returns, :151), and BOTH call `transport.send()` for that entry → the same field command is transmitted twice in one window. It carries the same idempotency_key so a correct server dedupes it, but (a) the client's own exactly-once invariant is not actually held — it's outsourced, and (b) the double-send races the server's claim-set dedupe (the Phase-1 prevent_oversell path), wasting round trips and risking a transient double-effect if the server dedupe isn't perfectly atomic.
findings.scenario: Offline weigh-in queued. Device crests a ridge, wifi flaps: `online`→`offline`→`online` in ~1s. start() already had an initial drain in flight on a 3G-slow send. Two drains overlap; both snapshot the single queued entry as `queued`; both POST the weigh-in. Server sees two requests with the same idempotency_key racing its dedupe insert.
findings.fix: Add a module-level in-flight guard to the outbox: `let flushing = false;` set at the top of flush(), `if (flushing) return summary;`, cleared in a finally. Independently guard the engine's drain() with a `draining` boolean so overlapping `online` events coalesce. Optionally mark an entry `sending` before the await and skip non-`queued` entries (already done) so even a re-entrant flush can't pick the same row.

findings.title: flush() writes a stale entry snapshot back unconditionally — a concurrent dismiss()/retry() during a slow send is silently clobbered (a dismissed command resurrects)
findings.dimension: DATA-LAYER INVARIANT BYPASS (never silently lost / never blindly retried)
findings.location: flush() :147-165 — `attempted` is a copy of the start-of-flush snapshot; after `await transport.send()` it does `store.put(ENTRY_PREFIX + entry.uuid, attempted)` with no re-read / no existence check; dismiss() :197-200 deletes the key; retry() :184-195 rewrites it
findings.problem: flush() takes `attempted = { ...entry }` from a snapshot, awaits the network send, then unconditionally `store.put()`s that stale copy. It never re-reads the row to confirm it still exists or is still in the state it expects. So any concurrent mutation of that key during the await window is lost on a last-writer-wins basis. The dangerous case: an operator dismisses a still-queued entry (dismiss() deletes the key) while a flush is mid-send on it; when the slow send resolves, flush() `put`s the snapshot back — resurrecting the just-dismissed command as `done` (or worse, `network-error`, leaving it queued to send AGAIN). That violates "never silently lost / never blindly retried": the operator's explicit dismiss is silently undone, and a command they thought was gone re-appears or re-sends. Symmetric clobber exists for a concurrent retry() (its state flip is overwritten by the stale put).
findings.scenario: Dead-letter drawer (later slice) over the same singleton outbox: a queued or revived entry is mid-send on slow signal; the operator taps Dismiss; dismiss() deletes the row; the send resolves; flush() re-`put`s the stale snapshot — the dismissed weigh-in is back. Even today, with the engine-driven flush, a manual dismiss racing an auto-drain reproduces it.
findings.fix: In flush(), before the post-send write, re-read the row (`store.get(key)`) and only persist the status transition if the row still exists and is still the `queued` entry being sent (compare uuid + status); if it's gone or changed, skip the write. Equivalently, scope a read-modify-write per entry under the in-flight guard from finding #2 so dismiss/retry can't interleave inside a single entry's send.

findings.title: Service Worker classifies any path ending in .js/.css (incl. /sw.js itself and non-hashed top-level scripts) as immutable cache-first, pinning stale code with no eviction
findings.location: isStaticAsset() :26-33 (the `/\.(?:js|css|woff2?|...)$/` catch-all) used by chooseStrategy() :45-51; mirrored verbatim in public/sw.js isStaticAsset :26-33 and cacheFirst() :73-82
findings.problem: `isStaticAsset` treats EVERY path ending in .js/.css as cache-first-immutable, not just the content-hashed `/_next/static/*`. cache-first (sw.js :73-82) returns the cached copy forever and never revalidates. Combined with finding #1 (CACHE_VERSION never bumps, so activate never evicts), any non-content-hashed .js/.css served at a stable URL is pinned to its first-seen bytes permanently. That includes `/sw.js` itself (matches `\.js$` → cache-first), and any top-level script/style without a hash in the name. Content-hashed Next chunks are safe because their URL changes; but the rule over-reaches to stable-URL scripts and to the SW script, which is exactly the class of file whose bytes change in place on deploy. This is the same stale-chunk → broken-app footgun the slice was meant to defeat, re-introduced by an over-broad classifier.
findings.scenario: Any stable-named script (a third-party widget, an analytics snippet, or a future `/app.js`) is updated in place on deploy. Field devices keep executing build-N bytes indefinitely — cache-first never revalidates and (per #1) the cache is never version-evicted.
findings.fix: Restrict cache-first to genuinely immutable, content-hashed URLs only — i.e. `pathname.startsWith("/_next/static/")` plus the explicit shell files (favicon.svg, manifest.webmanifest). Route all other .js/.css through stale-while-revalidate (or network-first) so a stable-URL script can update. Explicitly exclude `/sw.js` from any caching strategy so SW update detection is never served from cache. Keep sw-strategy.ts and public/sw.js in lockstep (they currently duplicate the bug).

findings.title: isApiOrAuth treats every same-origin /auth/* GET as network-first, but the offline navigation fallback bypass means an auth GET offline throws instead of degrading
findings.dimension: DATA-LAYER INVARIANT BYPASS (write-never-cached vs. fail-closed offline)
findings.location: networkFirst() :96-109 (re-throws when offline and nothing cached) vs. the SWR branch's shell fallback :128-134; isApiOrAuth :34-40
findings.problem: chooseStrategy sends `/auth/*` and `/rest/v1/*` GETs to networkFirst, which on offline with no cached copy re-throws (:107). Only the stale-while-revalidate branch has the `|| caches.match("/")` shell fallback (:130-133). So an offline navigation that resolves to an auth/RSC route classified as API (any same-origin `/auth/...` document GET) rejects the fetch with no shell fallback — the user gets the browser's hard offline error instead of the cached app shell, even though this is a read. It doesn't corrupt data or cache a write (the write invariant holds: non-GET is network-only), but it's a degradation gap: a field user offline on an auth-pathed navigation sees a dead page rather than the offline-safe shell. Minor because the primary field routes (/weigh, /harvests) are SWR and fall back correctly.
findings.scenario: Offline device navigates to an `/auth/...`-prefixed page (e.g. a callback or guarded route surfaced as a document GET). networkFirst throws, no cached entry, no shell fallback → hard error page instead of the offline shell.
findings.fix: In the fetch handler, give network-first navigations the same offline shell fallback as SWR (fall back to `caches.match("/")` for document requests when the network throws and nothing is cached), or scope isApiOrAuth strictly to `*.supabase.co` + `/rest/v1/*` data calls and let same-origin document navigations always take the SWR-with-shell-fallback path.

findings.title: verify_chain reads lot_event, NOT attendance_event — the "Chain verified" badge is vacuously green for every worker, and every test mocks the verifier so the suite cannot catch it
findings.location: people.test.ts:624-653 (verifyAttendanceChain describe); people.ts:379-387; verify_chain def in supabase/migrations/20260621092000_event_log_units_lot_graph.sql:297-330; attendance ledger in supabase/migrations/20260622090000_people_system.sql:190-209
findings.problem: The crew profile sheet shows a user-facing integrity trust signal — a green "Chain verified" / amber "Chain unverified" badge (worker-profile-sheet.tsx:108,146-150) — sourced from verifyAttendanceChain -> rpc('verify_chain', {stream_key:'attendance:<id>'}). But verify_chain (event_log migration line 297-330) loops over `lot_event` ONLY: `for r in select * from lot_event e where e.stream_key = verify_chain.stream_key`. The attendance ledger is a SEPARATE table, `attendance_event` (people migration line 190), with its OWN hash trigger. lot_event has ZERO rows for any 'attendance:<id>' key, so the loop body never executes and verify_chain returns the vacuous `true`. The badge is therefore PERMANENTLY GREEN for every worker and verifies ZERO real attendance rows. I proved this against the real migrations in PGlite: verify_chain('attendance:w-06')=true while lot_event rows=0 and attendance_event rows=2; and after directly corrupting a stored attendance hash, verify_chain STILL returned true — it cannot detect attendance tampering at all. This is the TEST-EFFICACY failure: the ONLY test of this contract (people.test.ts:624-653) stubs `.rpc` to resolve {data:true} / {data:false} and asserts the boolean is passed through — it mocks away the very thing it claims to verify (that verify_chain actually validates the attendance chain). The DB suite (p2s1_people.db.test.ts) NEVER calls verify_chain for an attendance stream at all, and the page/component tests hardcode `verifyAttendanceChain: vi.fn(async () => true)` (crew/__tests__/page.test.tsx:53) and pass literal `chainVerified` props (worker-profile-sheet.test.tsx:144-170). So all 57 green S1 tests pass while a compliance/integrity badge silently lies. The guard is effectively absent and no test would fail if you deleted the verifier entirely.
findings.scenario: Open /crew, expand any rehire-eligible worker's profile -> the badge reads "Chain verified". An admin (or a labor-law auditor reviewing the Ngäbe-Buglé crew's presence proof) directly mutates an attendance_event.hash or back-dates an event row in the DB. Reload the profile: it STILL reads "Chain verified". The integrity signal the family and any auditor would trust is structurally incapable of going amber. Repro (throwaway, since deleted): seed w-06, record two attendance events, `select verify_chain('attendance:w-06')` -> true with 0 lot_event rows; corrupt attendance_event.hash with triggers disabled -> verify_chain still true.
findings.fix: Highest-value missing test (DB-layer, kills the bug): in p2s1_people.db.test.ts add a test that records two real attendance_event rows for a worker, then asserts `verify_chain('attendance:<id>')` returns TRUE for the intact chain AND returns FALSE after a hash is tampered (UPDATE attendance_event.hash via session_replication_role=replica to bypass the block trigger). That test fails today on BOTH counts (returns true/true), pinning the bug. Production fix: give the worker ledgers their own verifier (e.g. verify_attendance_chain(worker_id) that loops over attendance_event with attendance_event_set_hash's canonical-bytes math), and repoint people.ts verifyAttendanceChain at it — OR make verify_chain table-aware. The TS-seam test in people.test.ts:624-653 should additionally assert the RPC NAME it calls matches the real verifier, not a generic verify_chain that targets the wrong table.

findings.title: PII-bearing worker_stream_event hash chain (enroll/rehire/cert events) is computed by a trigger but NEVER verified or chain-asserted by any test
findings.location: p2s1_people.db.test.ts:399-423 (only attendance chain is asserted); worker_stream_event trigger in supabase/migrations/20260622090000_people_system.sql:259-282
findings.problem: worker_stream_event is the dedicated PII-scoped append-only hash-chained ledger for WORKER_ENROLLED / WORKER_REHIRED / WORKER_CERTIFIED / POR_OBRA_SIGNED events (migration line 142-160), with a real BEFORE INSERT hash trigger (line 259-282). The DB suite has a 'hash-chain verifies on the worker ledgers' describe block (line 399), but it ONLY asserts the attendance_event chain (line 413-422: prev_hash null-first, then prev_hash == prior hash). There is ZERO assertion that worker_stream_event chains at all — grep for worker_stream_event + (prev_hash|hash|chain) across the whole test tree returns nothing but the people.ts doc comment. And verify_chain is never called for any 'worker:<id>' stream anywhere in the codebase. So the PII ledger's hash chain is entirely unpinned: a regression that broke worker_stream_event_set_hash (e.g. stopped folding prev_hash, or mis-ordered the head select) would ship green. This matters because worker_stream_event is the EVIDENCE ledger the rehire 'dignity moment' and cert-gated hazard work (S12) build on; an unverifiable chain is a silent integrity hole specifically on the migrant-crew PII the slice exists to protect.
findings.scenario: A future edit to worker_stream_event_set_hash (or to lot_event_canonical_bytes, which both ledgers share) breaks the per-stream linkage for worker_stream_event. record_certification / rehire_worker still insert rows and all S1 tests stay green because nothing asserts the worker_stream chain links. The 'append-only + hash-chained' guarantee the comarca-origin/cert evidence depends on is now hollow with no test signal.
findings.fix: Add a DB test mirroring the attendance one (line 413-422) but over worker_stream_event for a 'worker:<id>' stream after two stream events (e.g. an enroll + a cert): assert rows[0].prev_hash is null, rows[0].hash truthy, rows[1].prev_hash === rows[0].hash. Then add a tamper variant once a worker-stream verifier exists. This is cheap and pins the second of the two worker ledgers the slice claims to hash-chain.

findings.title: Por-obra partial-window supersede creates an UNRESOLVABLE rate gap, and no test covers the supersede-with-narrower-window case — only the full-overlap happy path is tested
findings.location: p2s1_people.db.test.ts:199-251 (por-obra describe); sign_por_obra_contract supersede UPDATE in supabase/migrations/20260622090000_people_system.sql:637-640; resolver v_active_por_obra at line 456-477
findings.problem: sign_por_obra_contract supersedes the prior open contract UNCONDITIONALLY by worker+task (migration line 637-640: `update por_obra_contracts set superseded_by=new_id where worker_id=... and task_kind=... and superseded_by is null`), with NO regard to whether the new contract's effective window actually covers the old one's. The DB test (line 217-231) only exercises a NEW open-ended contract superseding a prior open-ended one (windows fully overlap), so the resolver always finds a row. But if a worker signs an open-ended 'picking' rate from 2026-06-01, then later signs a NARROWER 'picking' contract only for July (effective_from 2026-07-01, effective_to 2026-07-31), the June contract gets superseded and v_active_por_obra('w','picking','2026-06-15') resolves to NOTHING — the rate the worker actually agreed to and worked under in June becomes UNRESOLVABLE. For a piece-rate (por-obra) payroll feeding a ~90% Ngäbe-Buglé crew, an unresolvable rate on a date they picked is a direct pay-correctness defect. The test suite's claimed invariant is 'supersede + rate immutability + resolver', but the resolver is only tested on the case where supersede can't strand a window. The highest-value MISSING test is exactly this partial-window-supersede gap; its absence is why the data-corruption-shaped behavior ships green.
findings.scenario: sign_por_obra_contract('w-06','picking','per-lata',3.0,'2026-06-01',null,'s1','po1') then sign_por_obra_contract('w-06','picking','per-lata',5.0,'2026-07-01','2026-07-31','s2','po2'). Now v_active_por_obra('w-06','picking','2026-06-15') returns 0 rows — payroll for the June latas this worker picked has no rate. (Verified in concept against the resolver's WHERE: superseded_by is null AND effective_from<=date AND (effective_to is null OR effective_to>=date) — the June row is superseded, the July row isn't yet effective.)
findings.fix: Add a DB test asserting the partial-window case: it should FAIL today (resolver returns 0 rows for the June date). Then decide the contract: either supersede should be window-aware (only supersede contracts whose window the new one covers), or the resolver/supersede must keep the strand resolvable. Whatever the chosen behavior, pin it with this test so the 'resolver resolves the rate the worker agreed to' invariant is real, not just tested on the full-overlap path.

findings.title: Leftover red-team scratch db.test.ts files are matched by the db project glob (src/**/*.db.test.ts) — underscore-prefix does NOT exclude them; one was failing/green-asserting a known bug, polluting the gate
findings.location: vitest.config.ts db project `include: ["src/**/*.db.test.ts"]` (no exclude for _-prefixed scratch files); transient files observed at src/test/db/_s1repro.db.test.ts and src/test/db/_redteam_s1.db.test.ts during this review
findings.problem: During this review the db test dir contained `_s1repro.db.test.ts` and `_redteam_s1.db.test.ts` — clearly throwaway red-team repro files (naming, console.log-heavy, asserting the BROKEN behavior as 'expected', e.g. `expect(verify_chain('attendance:w-06')).toBe(true)` pinning the vacuous-true bug AS IF it were correct). The db project glob `src/**/*.db.test.ts` matches them (the leading underscore is NOT excluded), so they run as part of `npm run test` — the project's sole quality gate (CLAUDE.md: gate = build green + test green). I observed one of them in a FAILING state (`× ... anon CAN EXECUTE _resync_worker_crew ... (no revoke was issued)`), i.e. a red db suite, and another that ENCODES a bug as a passing expectation. They were deleted mid-review (cleanup by another agent), but the structural exposure remains: any future scratch `_*.db.test.ts` silently joins the gate, and a bug-pinning 'expect(broken).toBe(true)' test actively cements wrong behavior. A red or bug-affirming scratch file in the only gate is a guardrail-integrity problem.
findings.scenario: An agent drops a `_repro.db.test.ts` to investigate a bug, asserts the current (wrong) output to characterize it, and forgets to delete it. `npm run test` now either goes red (blocking the gate) or — worse — goes green while a test literally asserts the broken behavior is expected, so a later real fix that corrects the behavior makes the scratch test fail and looks like a regression.
findings.fix: Exclude scratch patterns from the db project: add `exclude: ["src/**/_*.db.test.ts"]` (or a dedicated `**/scratch/**`) to the db project config, mirroring how the ui project already excludes `*.db.test.ts`. Separately, never commit a test that asserts known-broken output as the expected value — bug repros must be deleted or flipped to the corrected expectation alongside the fix.

findings.title: Single-reading ferment curve renders nothing (invisible chart on the very first reading of every batch)
findings.location: lines 87-95, 163-182 (linePath build + path render; no point markers)
findings.problem: FermentCurve only draws an SVG <path> from the line/area path string. With exactly ONE reading of a kind, `coords` has one element, so `linePath` is just a moveto: `d="M<x> <y>"` with zero line segments. An SVG path containing only a moveto renders NOTHING — no stroke, no dot. The area-fill path (line 166) becomes a degenerate zero-width shape (`M x y L x plotBottom L x plotBottom Z`, all same x) and also renders nothing. There are no <circle>/point markers anywhere (grep confirms only the gradient <stop>s). The empty-state only triggers at `series.length === 0` (line 53), so a 1-reading series falls through to this invisible render. The curve test (ferment-curve.test.tsx) only covers the 3-point case and the 0-point empty state — the single-point case is untested.
findings.scenario: Field worker starts a ferment, logs the FIRST pH reading. They open the cockpit expecting to see their reading plotted. The pH card shows an axis row '0h … 0h' and a completely blank plot area (the target band line is the only mark, if a recipe is bound). It looks broken/empty until a SECOND reading is logged. This is the literal first-use state of every batch.
findings.fix: Render an explicit point marker for every reading so a 1-point series is visible: add `{coords.map((c,i) => <circle key={i} cx={c.x} cy={c.y} r={3} fill={color} vectorEffect="non-scaling-stroke" />)}` (note: with preserveAspectRatio="none" the circle will be stretched into an ellipse — better to compute marker positions in real pixels, or render markers as small HTML dots absolutely-positioned over the SVG like the target chip). Minimum: special-case length===1 to draw a dot. Add a curve test for the single-reading series asserting a visible mark exists.

findings.title: CUT NOW alert text fails WCAG-AA contrast (4.21:1) — the single most important moment in the slice is hard to read
findings.location: lines 49-67 (cut-now banner: text-cherry on bg-cherry-100/90; subtext text-cherry/80)
findings.problem: The 'Cut now' banner is the closed-loop signal the family hits instead of guessing the cup-defining cut-point. Its main line uses `text-cherry` (#b5482e) at `text-sm` (14px) `font-semibold` (600) on `bg-cherry-100/90` (#f6ddd4@90% over paper). Measured contrast = 4.21:1, which FAILS WCAG-AA 4.5:1 — 14px/600 is not 'large text' (large = 18.66px normal or 14px BOLD/700). The subtext 'the ferment window is closing' uses `text-cherry/80` at `text-xs` (12px) → 3.12:1, also failing 4.5:1. The global quality gate explicitly requires AA on the real (possibly-animated) background.
findings.scenario: A worker in bright Volcán daylight glances at the cockpit at the cut-point. The most consequential alert in the whole make-quality loop is the lowest-contrast text on the page (4.21:1), and its supporting time line is 3.12:1 — borderline illegible outdoors / for low-vision users, on the one screen where misreading the cut ruins the cup.
findings.fix: Darken the alert text to a token that passes AA on cherry-100: use a dark cherry/ink (e.g. add `--color-cherry-700` ~#7a121e and use it for the banner text + subtext, or use `text-ink` for the body). Bump the main line to `font-bold` AND a darker red, and drop the `/80` opacity on the subtext. Re-measure ≥4.5:1 on `bg-cherry-100/90`.

findings.title: Temp & Brix curves have no y-axis scale and no value readout — colored line with zero numeric reference
findings.location: lines 100-116 (Temperature/Brix cards) + ferment-curve.tsx lines 185-203 (only pH gets a value readout; axis row is x/hours only)
findings.problem: FermentCurve renders only an X-axis row (hours) below the SVG; the only Y-value shown anywhere is the pH `target {targetPh}` chip (lines 186-196), and that exists ONLY when `kind==="ph" && targetPh!==null`. The Temperature and Brix cards render a bare colored line with NO y-axis labels, NO gridline values, and NO latest-value readout. The SVG aria-label encodes the range for screen readers, but a sighted user sees an unscaled squiggle: they cannot tell whether temp is 18°C or 33°C, or what the current Brix is. The y-domain is also auto-widened per-data (lines 68-74), so the line's vertical position is not even comparable between batches.
findings.scenario: A worker opens the cockpit to check fermentation temperature. The Temperature card shows an orange line rising and falling but with no numbers on it at all — they have to mentally guess the scale, or go log another reading to see the raw value echoed in the success toast. For a glance-and-go field cockpit this defeats the purpose of the chart.
findings.fix: Add a minimal y-readout to every curve (not just pH): show the latest value + a min/max y-axis label pair. E.g. render the latest reading value as an HTML chip (like the pH target chip) for temp/brix too, and add 'yMin … yMax' end labels to the axis row. Cheapest: surface the latest value beside each CardTitle in the tracker (`Temperature 24.6°C`).

findings.title: X-axis labels collapse to '0h / 0h / 0h' for early/short ferments (fractional hours rounded to zero)
findings.location: lines 78, 199-203 (xMax = max(hours,1); axis labels use Math.round(xMax/2) and Math.round(xMax))
findings.problem: The x-axis labels are `0h`, `Math.round(xMax/2)h`, `Math.round(xMax)h`. For the common early-ferment case where all readings are within the first hour (hoursElapsed like 0.2, 0.5, 0.8 → xMax=0.8, floored to 1 by `max(hours,1)`), the labels become `0h`, `Math.round(0.5)=0` → `0h`, `Math.round(0.8)=1` → `1h` — or with xMax exactly under 1, `0h / 0h / 1h`. Within the first 30 minutes all three collapse to `0h / 0h / 0h`. The time axis is meaningless during the first hour of every ferment.
findings.scenario: Worker logs three pH readings in the first 40 minutes of a fast anaerobic ferment. The pH curve's x-axis reads '0h … 0h … 0h' so the time progression is invisible even though three distinct readings exist.
findings.fix: Show fractional hours when xMax < a few hours (e.g. `xMax < 3 ? num(x,1) : Math.round(x)`), or switch the unit to minutes under 1h. Use the existing `num()` helper rather than `Math.round`.

findings.title: LogReadingForm value input keeps its old number after a successful submit (no reset)
findings.location: lines 26-108 (form is never replaced or reset on success; uncontrolled input retains its value)
findings.problem: Unlike StartFermentForm (which swaps to a success panel on `state.status === "success"`, start-ferment-form.tsx:49-63), LogReadingForm stays mounted and only shows a small inline 'Reading logged.' status (lines 92-99). The value <input> is uncontrolled with no key-bump or reset, so React/Next.js leaves the previously-typed number sitting in the field after submit. A worker logging a SERIES of readings sees the prior reading still in the box, inviting an accidental duplicate re-submit of the same value (the idempotencyKey is minted once per mount, so it does dedupe an identical immediate re-tap — but any edit produces a real new reading, and a stale '4.8' left in the field is a foot-gun).
findings.scenario: Worker logs pH 4.8 → success. The field still shows 4.8. They glance away, come back, and tap 'Log reading' again thinking the field cleared → a second 4.8 reading lands (the idempotencyKey only blocks the exact same submission within the same mount; on a re-render or remount it's a fresh reading).
findings.fix: Reset the field after success: bump a `key` on the <input> (or the <form>) when `state.status === "success"`, or move focus back to the value input cleared. Optionally also mint a fresh idempotencyKey per successful submit so the next reading is a clean exactly-once anchor.

findings.title: Empty-state subtext on the ferment curve fails AA contrast (3.08:1)
findings.location: lines 53-65 (empty state: text-muted-fg/70 at text-xs on bg-white/45)
findings.problem: The per-kind empty state's helper line 'Log a reading to start the live curve.' uses `text-muted-fg/70` at `text-xs` (12px) on `bg-white/45` over paper. Measured contrast = 3.08:1, failing WCAG-AA 4.5:1 for body text. The primary line `text-muted-fg` (no opacity) at 5.83:1 is fine, but the /70 helper is below threshold.
findings.scenario: On a fresh batch with no readings, the curve panels each show the empty state; the guidance subtext is below AA contrast for low-vision users / bright-light field viewing.
findings.fix: Drop the `/70` opacity on the helper line (use full `text-muted-fg`), or use a darker token. Same fix applies to the board card timestamps using `text-muted-fg/70` (ferment-board.tsx:80) if they fall below AA on the glass card.

findings.title: Reading-value error text is not programmatically linked to the input (aria-invalid without aria-describedby)
findings.location:
lines 67-81 (input sets aria-invalid but error

at line 79 has no id and no aria-describedby)
findings.problem:
The value input sets `aria-invalid` when there's a field error, and renders the error message in a sibling

(line 79-81), but that

has no `id` and the input has no `aria-describedby` pointing at it. A screen-reader user who focuses the invalid field hears 'invalid' with no announced reason. Same pattern in StartFermentForm (lotCode/method errors, start-ferment-form.tsx:91-93, 136-138).
findings.scenario: A worker enters a pH the SQL CHECK rejects (e.g. 99). The inline error renders visually, but a screen-reader user navigating the form gets no association between the error text and the field.
findings.fix:
Give each field-error

an id (e.g. `value-error`) and set `aria-describedby={fieldError('value') ? 'value-error' : undefined}` on the matching input/select. Apply across LogReadingForm and StartFermentForm.

findings.title: The /plan slice ships zero write UI — schedulePasada/replanPasada/recordMaturationSignal are dead code; the page is read-only and (on a fresh seed) permanently empty
findings.location: page.tsx renders only <HarvestPlanner/>; actions.ts:76/105/133 export 3 Server Actions; grep for importers/`use client`/button/form/Dialog across src/components/sections/planning + plan/** returns NOTHING; seed.sql touches 0 S8 tables (plot_phenology/maturation_signal/pasada_schedule)
findings.problem: The ROADMAP (lines 79, 318, 326) defines S8 as the 'pasada SCHEDULER' whose world-class bar is 'auto-spawn a task into the existing Tasks board' and 're-plan around a rain front'. The three Server Actions that do exactly that (and atomically fire a task — the slice's headline behavior) have ZERO consumers: no component imports plan/actions, the whole slice is pure Server Components with no `use client`, no button, no form, and no shared Dialog. So nothing in the running app can schedule a pasada, re-plan one, or log a bloom/GDD/NDVI signal. Worse, every sibling write surface (tasks/processing/harvests/ferment/inventory/plots) wires a shared-Dialog + button; planning is the only slice with a write port and no door to it. Compounding it: seed.sql populates `plots` but NONE of the S8 tables, so v_harvest_readiness LEFT-JOINs to null phenology (every plot renders 0% / 'Developing — weeks early' / 'No bloom logged'), v_pasada_calendar returns empty, the 'Ready to pick' tile is always 0, and PasadaTimeline shows ONLY its empty-state forever.
